source_text:
Electrical risks have the potential to cause death and serious injury through direct or indirect contact with electricity. The most common risks are:

 Electric shock causing injury or death, if contact is made with energised parts.   
 Fire, arcing or explosion causing burns, for example when moisture or dust enter electrical equipment, or an explosive atmosphere is present.   
 Toxic gases causing illness or death.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The TARGET sets a duty for a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per Part 3.1). The SOURCE describes what electrical risks are and gives examples of common electrical hazards (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases), which is information that would support identifying hazards as part of risk management.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate that risks are being managed in accordance with Part 3.1; it only lists potential electrical risks/hazards. It lacks required risk-management elements such as identifying hazards in the specific workplace context, assessing risks (if required), implementing and maintaining control measures, and reviewing controls. Therefore it does not fulfill the TARGET obligation on its own.
compliance.remediation: Add a Part 3.1-aligned risk management process for electrical risks: (1) identify electrical hazards in the workplace (equipment, installations, environments like moisture/dust/explosive atmospheres), (2) assess risks where necessary, (3) implement controls using the hierarchy of control (e.g., de-energise/isolate, lockout-tagout, RCDs, guarding, maintenance/testing, competent persons, procedures/training, PPE), (4) document responsibilities and procedures, and (5) monitor and review control effectiveness (including after incidents/changes).

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The SOURCE describes key electrical hazards (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases). The TARGET sets specific preconditions/controls required before commencing electrical work on energised equipment, including conducting and recording a risk assessment, ensuring clear access/egress, identifying and ensuring access to isolation/disconnection points, and authorisation after consultation. The SOURCE content could be used as input to the TARGET’s required risk assessment, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not meet the TARGET requirements. It provides a general hazard description but does not demonstrate that: (a) a risk assessment for the proposed energised work was conducted; (b) the work area and isolation point are clear of obstructions for access/exit; (c) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly; (d) the work was authorised after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace; (2) the risk assessment was conducted by a competent person and recorded. Therefore, it is non-compliant with the TARGET obligations as written.
compliance.remediation: Before commencing energised electrical work, complete and document a task-specific risk assessment conducted by a competent person; verify and document that the work area and isolation/disconnection point have unobstructed access/egress; ensure the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled/marked and can be operated quickly; obtain and document authorisation after consultation with the workplace management/control person. Maintain records of the assessment and authorisation.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a specific safety obligation during energized electrical work: prevent persons from inadvertently contacting exposed energized components (i.e., prevent creation of electrical risk via inadvertent contact). The SOURCE discusses electrical risks generally (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) and mentions shock from contact with energized parts, which is within the same risk domain. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state any required controls or measures to ensure persons are prevented from inadvertently making contact with exposed energized components while energized work is carried out. It only describes potential risks. As such, it does not demonstrate fulfillment of the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements/controls for energized electrical work to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energized components (e.g., isolation where possible, barriers/insulation, exclusion zones, lockout/tagout where applicable, use of insulating PPE and tools, supervision and access control, signage, and procedures for working on or near exposed energized parts).

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address electrical hazards in workplaces. The TARGET imposes specific duties on a PCBU to prevent persons/plant/things coming within unsafe distances of overhead/underground electric lines and, if that is not reasonably practicable, to conduct a risk assessment and implement controls consistent with it and any electricity authority requirements. The SOURCE discusses general electrical risks (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) but does not address electric line approach distances, risk assessment, or required control measures. Therefore a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate the required controls or processes in the TARGET. It provides hazard awareness only and lacks: (1) an assurance of maintaining safe distances from overhead/underground lines; (2) a statement that a risk assessment will be conducted when safe distance is not reasonably practicable; (3) implementation of controls consistent with the risk assessment; and (4) alignment with electricity supply authority requirements. As written, it does not meet the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements/procedures to: (1) identify overhead/underground electric lines and establish/maintain prescribed safe approach distances; (2) where safe distance cannot be ensured, conduct a documented risk assessment for the proposed work; (3) implement and document control measures consistent with the assessment (e.g., exclusion zones, spotters, isolation/de-energisation, barriers, permits, signage, supervision); and (4) consult and comply with any electricity supply authority requirements and applicable electricity safety legislation.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes electrical risks (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) that arise from contact with energised parts and related hazards. The TARGET imposes specific duties before electrical work: test equipment to determine if it is energised, treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. These are directly connected because they are controls intended to prevent the shock/arc/fire hazards described in the SOURCE.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is informational and does not state that equipment is tested by a competent person before work, that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, or that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit procedural requirements consistent with the TARGET: (1) require pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to confirm whether it is energised (per applicable testing rules), (2) require all exposed parts to be treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised, and (3) require high-voltage exposed parts to be earthed after de-energising. Document responsibilities, competency criteria, and testing/verification steps.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes electrical risks (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) associated with electrical equipment and environments. The TARGET defines what counts as “electrical equipment” for the purposes of a regulatory Part, including equipment above extra-low voltage and equipment in fire/explosion-risk atmospheres. Because the SOURCE is safety guidance about hazards arising from electrical equipment and conditions that the TARGET scopes/defines, a compliance alignment check is relevant (the SOURCE content would typically be used to support obligations applying to “electrical equipment” as defined).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not comply with the TARGET as a definitional provision. The TARGET’s requirement is to define “electrical equipment” with specific inclusions (voltage threshold, hazardous atmosphere location, cathodic protection system reference) and exclusions (certain motor car/motorcycle propulsion-related equipment). The SOURCE provides hazard examples but does not define “electrical equipment,” does not reference extra-low voltage, hazardous atmosphere scoping as a definitional criterion, AS 2832.1:2015 cathodic protection systems, or the vehicle exclusion. Therefore it does not meet the TARGET’s definitional content.
compliance.remediation: Add a definition section for “electrical equipment” that mirrors the TARGET: include the voltage > extra-low voltage criteria, inclusion for equipment in fire/explosion-risk atmospheres, inclusion for active impressed current cathodic protection systems per AS 2832.1:2015, and explicitly state the exclusion for motor car/motorcycle propulsion-related vehicle equipment. Keep the hazard list as supplementary guidance, but separate it from the formal definition.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes electrical hazards (shock, fire/arcing/explosion, toxic gases) and mentions environmental contributors like moisture and dust. The TARGET sets specific legal duties for a PCBU to manage such electrical risks by requiring regular inspection/testing of certain socket-supplied equipment used in damaging environments (including moisture and dust), plus recordkeeping. Because the SOURCE is about the risks that the TARGET controls through mandated inspection/testing and records, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state any controls or processes required by the TARGET (no requirement that equipment is regularly inspected/tested by a competent person; no mention of applicability to socket-outlet supplied equipment; no exemption/inspection-before-use for new equipment; no prohibition on using unsafe equipment by reference; no testing records, retention period, or required record contents/tagging). Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add and implement procedures requiring: (1) identification of in-scope electrical equipment (socket-outlet supplied and used in environments with moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust); (2) regular inspection and testing by a competent person at appropriate intervals; (3) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (4) ensuring unsafe equipment is not used; and (5) maintaining testing records until next test or disposal, including tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via tags).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1: The texts are functionally related because the SOURCE describes electrical hazards (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) that can arise from contact with energised equipment, while the TARGET imposes a specific duty to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised electrical equipment during electrical work—an important control to prevent electric shock and related incidents.

STEP 2: The SOURCE does not address the TARGET obligation. It provides a general description of risks but does not state any requirement or control to ensure de-energised equipment is not inadvertently re-energised (e.g., lockout/tagout, isolation, verification, signage, access control). Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET duty.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit controls/procedures requiring isolation and prevention of inadvertent re-energisation during electrical work (e.g., lockout/tagout, physical isolation, tagging, verification of de-energisation, control of keys/switchgear access, and clear responsibilities and checks before re-energising).

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The SOURCE describes electrical risks (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) that arise from contact with electricity, including scenarios involving energised equipment. The TARGET sets a legal/operational obligation on a business to ensure electrical work on energised equipment is not carried out unless specific necessity conditions apply. Risk identification in the SOURCE is relevant to deciding whether energised work should be avoided and controlled under the TARGET.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation. It provides general hazard information but does not state any requirement to prohibit energised electrical work except under the listed exceptions, nor does it document decision criteria (necessity/no reasonable alternative), authorization, or controls for energised work. Therefore, it cannot be considered compliant with the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE content (or associated procedure) to explicitly require that electrical work on energised equipment must not be performed unless one of the permitted conditions applies (health/safety necessity, work requires energisation, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). Add a decision/approval process to justify energised work, and include procedural controls for when energised work is permitted (e.g., documented justification, competent persons, isolation where possible, testing provisions).

matches.target_title: 164 Use of socket outlets in hostile operating environment
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies in the following circumstances: 

(a) electrical equipment is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; 

(b) electrical equipment is moved between different locations in circumstances where damage to the equipment or to a flexible electricity supply cord is reasonably likely; 

(c) electrical equipment is frequently moved during its normal use; 

(d) electrical equipment forms part of, or is used in connection with, an amusement device. 

(2) In a circumstance set out in subregulation (1), a person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that any electrical risk associated with the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment through a socket outlet is minimised by the use of an appropriate residual current device. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) Without limiting subregulation (2), the residual current device must have a tripping current that does not exceed 30 milliamps if electricity is supplied to the equipment through a socket outlet not exceeding 20 amps. 

(4) Subregulation (2) does not apply if the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment: 

(a) does not exceed 50 volts alternating current; or 

(b) is direct current; or 

(c) is provided through an isolating transformer that provides at least an equivalent level of protection; or 

(d) is provided from a non-earthed socket outlet supplied by an isolated winding portable generator that provides at least an equivalent level of protection.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related because both concern electrical risks in workplaces. The TARGET imposes a specific duty to minimise electrical risk in certain higher-risk circumstances by using an appropriate residual current device (RCD), with specific technical requirements and exceptions. The SOURCE discusses general electrical hazards (shock, fire/explosion, toxic gases) but does not describe any controls or measures that would satisfy the TARGET’s specific RCD requirement.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET because it does not state that an RCD is used, does not address the listed triggering circumstances (moisture/dust/heat/vibration/movement/amusement devices), does not specify an RCD trip current (≤30 mA for socket outlets ≤20 A), and does not indicate whether any exceptions apply (≤50 VAC, DC, isolating transformer, or isolated generator). Therefore, based on the provided SOURCE text alone, the obligation in the TARGET is not met.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure/policy to require use of an appropriate RCD to minimise electrical risk when equipment is used in the circumstances listed in (1)(a)-(d). Specify that for socket outlets ≤20 A the RCD must have a tripping current ≤30 mA. Document when exceptions apply (≤50 VAC, DC supply, isolating transformer, or isolated generator) and how equivalent protection is ensured. Include implementation details (selection, installation, testing/inspection, and recordkeeping) to evidence compliance.

source_text:
Section 146 of the WHS Regulation defines “electrical work” as:

 connecting electricity supply wiring to electrical equipment or disconnecting electrical supply wiring from electrical equipment; or   
 installing, removing, adding, testing, replacing, repairing, altering or maintaining electrical equipment or an electrical installation.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related because both define the term “electrical work” for WHS regulatory purposes. The SOURCE provides the core definition, and the TARGET reproduces that definition and adds explicit exclusions/clarifications.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The TARGET’s definition in (1)(a)–(b) matches the SOURCE definition verbatim in substance (connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing/removing/adding/testing/replacing/repairing/altering/maintaining electrical equipment or an electrical installation). The additional provisions in TARGET (2) list activities that are not considered electrical work; these do not contradict or omit the SOURCE definition and instead narrow/clarify scope. Therefore, the TARGET is compliant with the SOURCE definition.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines the scope of “electrical work” (activities such as connecting/disconnecting supply wiring and installing/testing/repairing/maintaining electrical equipment or installations). The TARGET text defines “electrical equipment” (what items count as electrical equipment for the purposes of the Part, including inclusions/exclusions such as certain vehicle equipment). While the concepts are related in the broader regulatory scheme (electrical work often involves electrical equipment), the TARGET does not impose an obligation that the SOURCE is attempting to satisfy, nor does it define or constrain the SOURCE’s definition of “electrical work” in the provided excerpt. Therefore, there is no direct functional relationship requiring a compliance check between these two excerpts alone.

matches.target_title: 145 Meaning of electrical installation
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical installation means a group of items of electrical equipment that: 

(a) are permanently electrically connected together; and (b) can be supplied with electricity from the works of an electricity supply authority or from a generating source. 

(2) An item of electrical equipment may be part of more than 1 electrical installation. 

(3) In subregulation (1)(a): 

(a) an item of electrical equipment connected to electricity by a plug and socket outlet is not permanently electrically connected; and   
(b) connection achieved through using works of an electricity supply authority is not a consideration in determining whether or not electrical equipment is electrically connected.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE defines “electrical work” as including activities performed on “electrical equipment” or an “electrical installation.” The TARGET defines what constitutes an “electrical installation” (including what counts as permanently connected). Because the SOURCE relies on the meaning of “electrical installation” to determine the scope of “electrical work,” the texts are functionally related and a compliance/consistency check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE definition of “electrical work” does not conflict with the TARGET definition of “electrical installation,” but it is incomplete for compliance purposes because it does not incorporate or reflect the TARGET’s criteria for what qualifies as an “electrical installation” (e.g., plug-and-socket equipment not being permanently connected, and the clarification about supply authority works not being a consideration for electrical connection). If the SOURCE is intended to be used operationally to classify work as “electrical work,” it should align with and reference the TARGET definition to avoid misclassification (e.g., treating plug-in equipment as part of an electrical installation when it is not permanently connected). Therefore, it is not compliant as a standalone compliance-ready statement against the TARGET’s definitional requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE guidance/definition usage to explicitly reference and apply the TARGET definition of “electrical installation,” including the exclusions/clarifications (e.g., plug-and-socket connections are not permanently connected), or add a cross-reference stating that “electrical installation” has the meaning given in the relevant regulation section defining it.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE defines the scope/meaning of “electrical work” under WHS Regulation s146. The TARGET sets specific preconditions/obligations that must be ensured before “electrical work on energised electrical equipment” commences. Because the TARGET obligations apply when an activity falls within the definition of “electrical work,” the definition in the SOURCE is functionally related and necessary to determine when the TARGET duties are triggered.

STEP 2 (Compliance): A compliance determination cannot be made because the SOURCE is only a definition and does not describe any controls, procedures, or actions addressing the TARGET’s requirements (risk assessment by a competent person, recorded assessment, clear access/egress, marked/accessible/quickly operable isolation point, and authorisation after consultation). Therefore, compliance is not assessable from the SOURCE alone.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The TARGET imposes a duty on a PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The SOURCE defines what constitutes “electrical work” (activities involving connecting/disconnecting supply wiring and installing/testing/maintaining electrical equipment/installations). This definition helps determine the scope of activities that may give rise to electrical risks that must be managed under the TARGET, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET obligation to manage risks in accordance with Part 3.1. It only provides a definition of “electrical work” and contains no requirements or controls for risk management (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, implementation of controls, maintenance/testing regimes, or review). Therefore, it does not fulfill the TARGET duty and is non-compliant as a standalone response to the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Add or reference procedures and controls requiring the PCBU to manage electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (e.g., identify electrical hazards arising from electrical work, assess risks, implement and maintain control measures for design/installation/protection/maintenance/testing, and review controls). Ensure the definition of “electrical work” is used to scope when these risk-management processes must be applied.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text only defines the scope of “electrical work” (i.e., what activities count as electrical work). The TARGET text imposes a duty on a PCBU to manage unsafe electrical equipment (disconnect/isolate, prevent reconnection until safe, replace/remove). A definition of electrical work does not, by itself, address or evidence compliance with the operational safety obligations in the TARGET. Therefore, there is no direct functional relationship requiring a compliance check between these two excerpts alone.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Yes, they are functionally related. The SOURCE defines the scope of what counts as “electrical work” (e.g., connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing, testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installations). The TARGET imposes an operational prohibition/condition on carrying out “electrical work” on energised electrical equipment unless specific exceptions apply. The definition in the SOURCE is necessary to determine whether an activity falls within the TARGET’s regulated activity.

STEP 2 (Compliance): Not assessable from the SOURCE alone. The SOURCE is only a definition and does not state whether electrical work on energised equipment is being performed, nor whether any of the TARGET’s exception conditions (a)–(d) are met, or whether required controls/procedures are in place. Therefore compliance cannot be determined from the provided SOURCE text.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines the scope of “electrical work” (what activities count as electrical work). The TARGET text provides an applicability exemption for a specific Division, stating it does not apply to work done by/for an electricity supply authority on equipment it controls/operates for transforming/transmitting/supplying electricity. A definition of electrical work does not attempt to satisfy, implement, or evidence compliance with an exemption about a Division’s applicability; they address different regulatory functions (definition vs. carve-out). Therefore, a direct compliance check between them is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Yes. The SOURCE defines the scope of “electrical work.” The TARGET imposes duties that apply “before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment” and during work (treat exposed parts as energised; earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising). Therefore, the definition in the SOURCE determines when the TARGET obligations are triggered, making them functionally related.

STEP 2 (Compliance): Cannot be determined from the SOURCE. The SOURCE is only a definition and does not state any controls or actions (testing by a competent person, treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, or earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising). Without evidence of procedures or practices, compliance with the TARGET cannot be assessed.
compliance.remediation: Provide or implement documented procedures and evidence that, before any activity meeting the definition of “electrical work,” electrical equipment is tested by a competent person to confirm it is not energised; exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising (including alignment with Regs 157 and 161 testing requirements).

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Yes. The SOURCE defines what constitutes “electrical work” under the WHS Regulation. The TARGET imposes specific duties on a PCBU when “electrical work on energised electrical equipment” is carried out. The definition in the SOURCE is necessary to determine whether an activity falls within the scope of the TARGET obligations, so a compliance check is relevant.

STEP 2 (Compliance): Not assessable from the SOURCE. The SOURCE only provides a definition and does not describe any controls, processes, or evidence of compliance (e.g., competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, SWMS content, safety observer requirements, or risk assessment outcomes). Therefore compliance with the TARGET cannot be determined from the provided SOURCE text alone.
compliance.remediation: Provide evidence/procedures showing that when electrical work (as defined) is performed on energised electrical equipment: (1) it is done by a competent person with suitable, tested, and well-maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) a compliant safe work method statement is prepared and followed (identification of work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review); (3) a qualified/competent safety observer is present unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies; and (4) the PCBU ensures, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE.

source_text:
Electrical work does not include work on electrical equipment that is operated by electricity at extra-low voltage except electrical equipment that:

 is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or  is, or is part of an active impressed current cathodic protection system.

Appendix 1 What is not electrical work in CSIRO within the Electrical Safety Procedure lists the activities that are considered electrical work under Section 146 of the WHS Regulations.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts define the scope of “electrical work” and exclusions. The SOURCE appears to be an internal procedure statement intended to align with (or interpret) the regulatory definition/exclusions in the TARGET, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET because it provides only a narrow exclusion (extra-low voltage equipment) with two exceptions, and then refers to an appendix for other activities. The TARGET definition is broader (connecting/disconnecting supply wiring; installing/testing/repairing/maintaining electrical equipment/installations) and lists multiple explicit exclusions (plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical components without electrical risk, simple replacements like fuses/light bulbs, manufacturing process work, certain duct/conduit work under conditions, mounting equipment not related to connection, supervised assisting, and eligibility work). The SOURCE does not reflect these exclusions and could misclassify work as “electrical work” or “not electrical work” compared to the TARGET. Additionally, the SOURCE’s extra-low voltage exclusion is not presented as one of the TARGET’s exclusions and may conflict unless it is clearly framed as an additional internal clarification that does not override the regulatory definition.
compliance.remediation: Revise the SOURCE section to explicitly align with the TARGET definition and exclusions: (1) include the TARGET’s core definition of electrical work; (2) reproduce or accurately summarize all TARGET exclusions (a)–(h) and any jurisdictional notes as applicable; (3) if retaining the extra-low voltage statement, clearly position it as an internal clarification consistent with the regulation (and ensure it does not contradict or omit regulatory exclusions); (4) ensure Appendix 1 is complete, mapped to the TARGET clauses, and cross-referenced so the procedure is self-consistent and auditable.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The TARGET defines what counts as “electrical equipment” for the purposes of the Part, including specific inclusions (hazardous atmosphere areas and active impressed current cathodic protection systems) and an explicit exclusion for certain vehicle equipment. The SOURCE is describing what “electrical work” does not include, with an exception for extra-low voltage equipment in the same two special cases (hazardous atmosphere and cathodic protection). This indicates the SOURCE is attempting to align internal guidance on what is/is not electrical work with regulatory definitions and scope triggers in the TARGET.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not fully compliant/aligned with the TARGET. The TARGET’s definition of electrical equipment covers equipment used/operated at voltages greater than extra-low voltage (a broad category), and also includes the two special cases regardless of voltage. The SOURCE statement focuses only on excluding work on equipment operated at extra-low voltage (with the two exceptions), but does not reflect the broader TARGET scope for equipment > extra-low voltage, nor does it incorporate the TARGET’s explicit exclusion for certain motor car/motorcycle propulsion-related equipment. Additionally, the SOURCE references an internal appendix for what is electrical work, but the provided SOURCE excerpt alone does not demonstrate coverage of the TARGET’s full definitional elements and exclusions.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE/internal procedure text to explicitly align with the TARGET definition by: (1) clarifying how work on equipment used/operated at > extra-low voltage is treated (i.e., generally within scope), (2) retaining the two special inclusions (hazardous atmosphere areas and active impressed current cathodic protection systems), and (3) adding/confirming the vehicle (motor car/motorcycle propulsion unit) exclusion where applicable, or explicitly stating it is addressed elsewhere in the procedure/appendix with a clear cross-reference.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE defines what is (and is not) considered “electrical work,” focusing on exclusions for extra-low voltage equipment with specific exceptions (hazardous atmospheres; impressed current cathodic protection). The TARGET defines a scope exclusion for an entire Division, stating it does not apply to work performed by/for an electricity supply authority on equipment it controls/operates for transforming/transmitting/supplying electricity. These address different regulatory concepts (definition of electrical work vs. applicability of a regulatory Division to electricity supply authorities) and neither text is clearly implementing or fulfilling the other’s requirement. Therefore, a direct compliance check between them is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines what is and is not considered “electrical work” (specifically excluding most extra-low voltage equipment except in hazardous atmospheres or cathodic protection systems). The TARGET text imposes duties and conditions for performing electrical work on energised electrical equipment. These address different compliance questions: classification/scope (SOURCE) versus controls/permissions for energised work (TARGET). The SOURCE is not attempting to implement or satisfy the TARGET’s requirement about when energised electrical work may be performed, so a direct compliance check is not necessary based on the provided excerpts.

matches.target_title: 145 Meaning of electrical installation
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical installation means a group of items of electrical equipment that: 

(a) are permanently electrically connected together; and (b) can be supplied with electricity from the works of an electricity supply authority or from a generating source. 

(2) An item of electrical equipment may be part of more than 1 electrical installation. 

(3) In subregulation (1)(a): 

(a) an item of electrical equipment connected to electricity by a plug and socket outlet is not permanently electrically connected; and   
(b) connection achieved through using works of an electricity supply authority is not a consideration in determining whether or not electrical equipment is electrically connected.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE defines exclusions from “electrical work” and references “electrical installation” (e.g., equipment that is part of an electrical installation in a hazardous atmosphere area). The TARGET defines what constitutes an “electrical installation” (permanent electrical connection, supply source, plug/socket not permanent). Because the SOURCE relies on the concept of “electrical installation” to scope what is/is not electrical work, the TARGET definition is functionally relevant and a compliance/consistency check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not align with or incorporate the TARGET’s definitional criteria for “electrical installation” (e.g., permanence of connection; plug-and-socket equipment not being permanently connected). The SOURCE uses “electrical installation” without clarifying that plug-in extra-low-voltage equipment would generally not be part of an electrical installation under the TARGET definition, which can change whether the SOURCE’s exception applies. As written, the SOURCE is incomplete/ambiguous relative to the TARGET definition and could misclassify work as excluded/included.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure text (or the referenced Appendix) to explicitly adopt or cross-reference the TARGET definition of “electrical installation,” including the permanence requirement and the plug-and-socket clarification, and then restate the extra-low-voltage exclusion/exception in a way that is consistent with that definition (e.g., clarify when extra-low-voltage equipment is considered part of an electrical installation).

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines the scope of what is (and is not) considered “electrical work” (with exceptions) and points to an internal appendix listing activities considered electrical work. The TARGET text sets specific mandatory controls for carrying out electrical work on energised electrical equipment (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, SWMS content, safety observer requirements and exceptions). Because the SOURCE does not address energised electrical work controls (SWMS, safety observer, tool/PPE requirements) and is instead a definitional/scope statement, it is not functionally fulfilling the obligations in the TARGET. A direct compliance check is therefore not applicable based on the provided SOURCE excerpt alone.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines what is and is not considered “electrical work” (specifically excluding most extra-low voltage equipment work, with limited exceptions) and references an internal appendix listing activities considered electrical work. The TARGET text imposes specific preconditions (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility, authorisation) that must be ensured before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment. Because the SOURCE does not address requirements for energised electrical work (risk assessment, isolation, authorisation) and instead focuses on scope/definitions, it is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligations. A compliance check against the TARGET cannot be meaningfully performed on the SOURCE content alone.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general duty for a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per WHS risk management requirements). The SOURCE defines what is and is not considered “electrical work” (with exceptions) and points to an appendix listing activities considered electrical work. These are functionally related because defining the scope of “electrical work” affects how the organisation identifies electrical hazards/activities and applies appropriate controls, competency/licensing, and risk management processes.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The TARGET requires active risk management for electrical risks (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing, etc.). The SOURCE does not describe any risk management process (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment, control measures, verification, review) and instead focuses on definitional scope/exclusions for electrical work. Therefore, the SOURCE text alone does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation to manage electrical risks; it is insufficient as a control statement for Part 3.1 risk management.
compliance.remediation: Add or reference explicit procedures that implement Part 3.1 risk management for electrical risks (identify hazards, assess risks where required, implement and maintain controls, verify effectiveness, and review). Ensure the procedure covers electrical risks across the lifecycle (design, installation, maintenance, testing) and clarifies how the “electrical work” scope definition triggers required controls (e.g., competent persons, permits, isolation/LOTO, inspection/testing, hazardous areas, cathodic protection systems).

(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines the scope of what is (and is not) considered “electrical work,” particularly excluding most extra-low voltage equipment except in specified hazardous contexts. The TARGET text imposes procedural duties (testing for energisation, treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts) that apply “before electrical work is carried out.” Because the SOURCE does not describe any process for testing/isolating/earthing and is primarily a definitional scope statement, it is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligations. A compliance check against the TARGET cannot be meaningfully performed from the SOURCE alone.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE defines what is and is not considered “electrical work” (including exceptions for extra-low voltage equipment and references to WHS Regulations s146 and an internal CSIRO appendix). The TARGET defines how references to “electrical equipment” or “electrical installation” are to be interpreted in relation to a PCBU—i.e., only equipment/installations under the person’s management or control. These provisions address different regulatory functions (scope of ‘electrical work’ vs. attribution/interpretation of equipment under management/control) and the SOURCE is not attempting to satisfy an obligation created by the TARGET. Therefore, a direct compliance check between them is not necessary.

source_text:
Electrical Safety Procedure Section 1 – HSE Risk Management

Prior to undertaking any electrical work or activities involving a possible electrical hazard that has the potential to cause injury or property/infrastructure damage an Activity Risk Assessment must be completed. A Safe Work Instruction (SWI) must be completed for recurring electrical work and energised electrical work.

Section 147 of the WHS Regulation specifies that an employer “must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace”. This includes electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace.

Section 2 of the Code of Practice 2018 – Managing electrical risks in the workplace provides the following guidance that may assist when completing an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA):

 different hazards associated with electrical equipment and installations;   
 considerations when assessing the nature and severity of risks associated with electrical hazards and electrical work; and   
 considerations when identifying and implementing control measures to eliminate or reduce the risks with associated with electrical hazards.

Refer to Section 7 of this guideline for information on risk assessment consideration for energised electrical work.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a legal duty for a PCBU/employer to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The SOURCE is an internal electrical safety procedure describing how electrical risks are to be managed (via Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions), and it explicitly references WHS Regulation s147 and the Code of Practice guidance. Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally intended to implement/fulfill the obligation described in the TARGET.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with the TARGET by requiring risk management actions prior to electrical work (completion of an Activity Risk Assessment) and additional procedural controls for recurring and energised electrical work (SWIs), which are consistent with managing electrical risks. It also covers the same scope of electrical risks cited in the TARGET (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) by referencing WHS Regulation s147 and the Code of Practice. No direct contradiction or omission that would undermine the duty is evident from the provided excerpts.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets internal requirements for managing electrical risks, including completing an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) before electrical work and completing a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for energised electrical work. The TARGET is a specific legal requirement for energised electrical work, mandating preconditions including a risk assessment (by a competent person and recorded), site/access conditions, isolation point marking/access/operability, and formal authorisation after consultation. Because the SOURCE addresses risk assessment and energised electrical work controls, it is functionally related and should be checked against the TARGET.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET 1(a) by requiring an ARA prior to electrical work and requiring an SWI for energised electrical work. However, it does not explicitly require that the risk assessment for energised work be conducted by a competent person and recorded (TARGET (2)), nor does it include the additional mandatory preconditions in TARGET (1)(b)-(d): clearing obstructions for access/egress, ensuring the isolation/disconnection point is labelled/clear/quick to operate, and authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. The SOURCE also references a guideline section for energised work considerations but does not state these specific requirements. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly include: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) verification that the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of quick operation (and address the exception where applicable); and (4) a documented authorisation step after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace before commencing energised electrical work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for managing electrical risks via Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions (including for energised electrical work). The TARGET imposes specific legal requirements for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, safe work method statement content, safety observer requirements, and ensuring proper use). Because the SOURCE appears intended to operationalize electrical risk management and procedures for energised work, a compliance check against the TARGET is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET as written. While it requires an Activity Risk Assessment and an SWI for energised electrical work, it does not explicitly require: (1) energised work be carried out by a competent person with tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) a safe work method statement (SWMS) prepared for the work, including the specific minimum content (identify work, hazards/risks, control measures, and implementation/monitoring/review); (3) a safety observer with specified competence and recent (12-month) rescue/resuscitation assessment, or the limited exception where only testing is performed and a risk assessment shows no serious risk; or (4) the PCBU ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. The SOURCE references risk management generally and points to other sections/guidance, but those elements are not present in the provided SOURCE text, so compliance cannot be demonstrated.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure (and/or the referenced SWI/ARA process) to explicitly incorporate the TARGET requirements for energised electrical work, including: requiring a SWMS for energised work with the mandated content; mandating competent persons and specifying suitability/testing/maintenance requirements for tools, testing equipment and PPE; requiring a safety observer meeting competence and 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment criteria (and documenting the testing-only/no-serious-risk exception via a risk assessment); and requiring the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE. Ensure these requirements are documented, implemented, and auditable.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for managing electrical risks (Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions for recurring/energised work) and references WHS duties to manage electrical risks. The TARGET imposes specific pre-work and work-practice obligations for electrical work: testing to confirm de-energised status, treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. These are both about controlling electrical risks during electrical work, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state the TARGET’s specific mandatory controls (pre-work testing by a competent person to determine energisation status; treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising). It only requires an ARA and SWI and provides general guidance references. Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the specific procedural requirements in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI templates to explicitly require: (1) testing of electrical equipment by a competent person before work to determine whether it is energised (and reference the applicable testing method requirements); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage exposed parts, mandatory earthing after de-energising. Ensure these steps are embedded as mandatory controls in ARA/SWI for relevant tasks and include verification/recording requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
Relevance: The SOURCE sets procedural requirements for managing electrical risks, including requiring an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) before electrical work and requiring a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for recurring and energised electrical work. The TARGET sets a specific legal constraint: energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, work requires energisation, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). Because the SOURCE governs energised electrical work planning/controls, it is functionally related to the TARGET and should ensure those preconditions are met.
