Compliance: The SOURCE does not state the prohibition on energised electrical work by default, nor does it require verification/documentation that one of the TARGET conditions (a)-(d) applies before energised work proceeds. It requires an ARA and SWI, and references risk management guidance, but it does not explicitly incorporate the TARGET’s necessity tests or the requirement to avoid energised work unless justified. Therefore it does not fully satisfy/reflect the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements to explicitly: (1) prohibit energised electrical work unless one of the regulatory justifications (a)-(d) is met; (2) require the ARA/SWI to document which justification applies and why de-energisation is not reasonably practicable; and (3) include a mandatory check/approval step confirming the justification prior to commencing energised work (including where energised testing is performed).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out an electrical safety risk-management procedure requiring an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) and Safe Work Instructions (SWI) for certain electrical work, referencing WHS Regulation duties to manage electrical risks. The TARGET imposes a specific WHS obligation on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, with recordkeeping requirements. Both address management of electrical risks at the workplace; therefore a compliance check is necessary to see whether the SOURCE procedure covers the TARGET’s inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not specify any requirement to (a) regularly inspect and test in-scope electrical equipment (plug-in equipment used in harsh/damaging environments), (b) ensure testing is performed by a competent person, (c) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before first use, or (d) keep testing records with the required minimum content (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) until next test/removal. Because these concrete obligations are absent from the SOURCE, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly require: (1) identification of in-scope plug-in electrical equipment used in environments likely to cause damage (moisture/heat/vibration/mechanical damage/corrosives/dust); (2) regular inspection and testing intervals/trigger points and that testing is performed by a competent person; (3) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (4) prohibition on use of unsafe equipment (cross-reference reg 149); and (5) recordkeeping for each test including tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date, retained until next test or disposal/removal, with allowance for tagging as the record.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out risk management requirements for electrical work, including completing an Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions, and references WHS Regulation duties to manage electrical risks. The TARGET imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work: prevent persons from creating electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. Because the SOURCE explicitly addresses energised electrical work (requiring SWIs and referring to further guidance), it is functionally related to the TARGET obligation and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require controls to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components while energised work is being performed (e.g., barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, supervision, access control). It focuses on completing assessments/instructions and general risk management guidance, but does not state the specific preventative requirement in the TARGET. Therefore, it does not demonstrably fulfill the TARGET obligation as written.
compliance.remediation: Amend the Electrical Safety Procedure/SWI requirements for energised electrical work to explicitly mandate measures that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (e.g., establish exclusion zones and access control, use insulating barriers/covers and shrouding, lockable panels/guards where practicable, signage, dedicated safety observer where required, and verification that exposed live parts are controlled before and during work). Ensure the ARA/SWI templates include a specific check/control item addressing this requirement.

matches.target_title: 162 Record keeping
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies if a person conducting a business or undertaking prepares: 

(a) a risk assessment under regulation 158; or (b) a safe work method statement under regulation 161. 

(2) Subject to subregulation (3), the person must keep: 

(a) a copy of the risk assessment until at least 28 days after the work to which it relates is completed; and 

(b) a copy of the safe work method statement until the work to which it relates is completed. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(3) If a notifiable incident occurs in connection with the work to which the assessment or statement relates, the person must keep the assessment or statement (as applicable) for at least 2 years after the incident occurs. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.   
(4) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is readily accessible to any worker engaged by the person to carry out electrical work to which the assessment or statement relates. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.   
(5) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is available for inspection under the Act. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE requires completing an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) before electrical work and completing a Safe Work Instruction (SWI) for recurring and energised electrical work. The TARGET regulation applies when a PCBU prepares a risk assessment (under reg 158) or a safe work method statement (SWMS) (under reg 161) for electrical work, and then imposes document retention and accessibility/inspection obligations. Because the SOURCE is directing the creation of risk assessment-type and safe-work documents for electrical work, it is functionally related to the TARGET’s requirements about keeping and making those documents accessible.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address any of the TARGET’s specific obligations: (a) keeping copies for required periods (28 days after completion for risk assessments; until completion for SWMS; 2 years after a notifiable incident), (b) ensuring copies are readily accessible to workers during the retention period, or (c) ensuring copies are available for inspection under the Act. Additionally, the SOURCE references ARA and SWI, while the TARGET refers to risk assessments under reg 158 and SWMS under reg 161; the SOURCE does not map its document types to these regulatory instruments or state that its documents meet those requirements. Therefore, based on the provided text alone, the SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly require: (1) retention of electrical-work risk assessments for at least 28 days after the work is completed; (2) retention of SWMS (or the equivalent document, if SWI is intended to function as SWMS) until the work is completed; (3) retention of the relevant assessment/statement for at least 2 years after any notifiable incident connected to the work; (4) that copies are readily accessible to workers performing the electrical work for the entire retention period; and (5) that copies are available for inspection under the Act. Also clarify/document how ARA and SWI correspond to (or satisfy) reg 158 risk assessments and reg 161 SWMS requirements.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets a general requirement to complete an Activity Risk Assessment (ARA) before electrical work/activities involving electrical hazards, and references WHS Regulation duties to manage electrical risks. The TARGET imposes a specific duty regarding working near overhead/underground electric lines: maintain safe distances, and if not reasonably practicable, conduct a risk assessment and implement controls consistent with the assessment and any electricity authority requirements. Because the SOURCE describes risk assessment and control planning for electrical hazards, it is functionally related to the TARGET’s requirement to assess and control risks when safe distances to electric lines cannot be maintained.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET because it does not explicitly require ensuring safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, does not state the conditional trigger ('if not reasonably practicable to ensure safe distance'), and does not require that control measures align with electricity supply authority requirements for the line. While the SOURCE requires an ARA for electrical hazards generally, it lacks the TARGET’s specific obligations for electric line proximity work.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly address work near overhead and underground electric lines by: (1) requiring maintenance of prescribed safe approach distances so far as reasonably practicable; (2) requiring a specific risk assessment when safe distances cannot be maintained; (3) requiring implementation of controls consistent with that assessment; and (4) requiring consultation/compliance with any applicable electricity supply authority requirements (e.g., isolation, de-energisation, permits, spotters, barriers).

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out an electrical safety risk-management approach (Activity Risk Assessment and Safe Work Instructions) intended to manage electrical risks at the workplace under WHS obligations. The TARGET imposes a specific operational control requirement for managing electrical risk: unsafe electrical equipment must be disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/removed. Both address management of electrical risks at the workplace, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state the specific required actions for unsafe electrical equipment (mandatory disconnection/isolation, prohibition on reconnection until repaired/tested safe, or replacement/permanent removal). It focuses on completing risk assessments and SWIs and references general duties and guidance, but it does not implement or evidence the TARGET’s concrete control measures. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Update the Electrical Safety Procedure to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment is immediately disconnected/isolated from supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and only returned to service after repair and testing confirms it is safe, or else replaced/permanently removed. Define criteria/process for determining “reasonable grounds” equipment is unsafe, assign responsibilities, and include verification/recordkeeping steps (e.g., isolation, tagging, test results, disposal/replacement records).

source_text:
Electrical workers must be deemed competent to carry out electrical work.

Electrical work competencies are regulated by the states and territories, as a result your location will determine what types of competencies are required for the work you carry out.

Table 1 provides an overview of what licences are required in each state and territory for specific electrical work. If an electrical licence isn’t required, then competency must be demonstrated through the successful completion of a practical competency assessment as agreed by CSIRO.

Table 1 Electrical licence and competency assessment requirements by state/territory

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” and lists exclusions and supervision/eligibility conditions, which establishes when licensing/registration requirements apply. The SOURCE sets a competency requirement for electrical workers and references state/territory licensing and competency assessment requirements. Because competency/licensing obligations depend on whether an activity is “electrical work” (and whether it is excluded), a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET because it does not incorporate or align with the TARGET’s definition and exclusions for “electrical work.” The SOURCE broadly states that “electrical workers must be deemed competent to carry out electrical work” and then discusses licensing/competency assessments by jurisdiction, but it does not clarify which tasks are considered electrical work versus excluded tasks (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, manufacturing-process assembly, supervised assistance, supervised conduit work under specified conditions, or eligibility work). Without these distinctions, the SOURCE could incorrectly impose competency/licensing expectations on activities that the TARGET explicitly excludes or fail to specify supervision conditions where the TARGET requires them.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE to (1) adopt or reference the TARGET definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions, (2) explicitly state that competency/licensing requirements apply only to activities that meet the definition of electrical work (and note the excluded activities), and (3) include the TARGET’s supervision/conditions for excluded-but-permitted activities (e.g., supervised assistance, conduit/duct work conditions, eligibility work). Also ensure Table 1/jurisdictional guidance is mapped to these definitions and references the Appendix/jurisdictional notes where applicable.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address competency requirements for electrical work. The TARGET imposes specific duties on a PCBU for energised electrical work (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, SWMS, safety observer, and ensuring proper use). The SOURCE discusses competency/licensing requirements by jurisdiction and CSIRO practical competency assessment where no licence is required. This is functionally related because the SOURCE could be used to support the TARGET’s requirement that work be carried out by a competent person.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET subregulation (1)(a) by stating electrical workers must be deemed competent and referencing licensing/competency assessment. However, it does not address several mandatory TARGET elements for energised electrical work: (1)(a) tools/testing equipment/PPE suitability, testing, and maintenance; (1)(b) requirement for a safe work method statement and its required contents; (1)(c) requirement for a safety observer and the observer’s qualifications/competence including rescue/resuscitation assessment within 12 months; (2) ensuring proper use of tools/PPE; and (5) the limited exception for when a safety observer is not required. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fulfill the TARGET obligations and is non-compliant as a standalone control/statement against the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE/control documentation to explicitly require for energised electrical work: (1) use of suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) preparation and use of a SWMS meeting the specified content requirements; (3) presence of a qualified/competent safety observer, including documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months (or document the testing-only + risk assessment exception); and (4) procedures to ensure workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE. Also clarify that jurisdictional licensing/competency assessment is in addition to these energised-work controls.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern electrical safety and competency requirements for people performing electrical-related tasks. The TARGET imposes specific duties on a PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is tested by a competent person before work, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until proven otherwise (and HV parts earthed after de-energising). The SOURCE discusses competency/licensing requirements for electrical workers, which is related to (but not sufficient for) the TARGET’s requirement for competent testing and specific safe-work steps.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state that electrical equipment must be tested before work to determine whether it is energised, nor does it require treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, nor earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. It only addresses general competency/licensing and assessment. Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the specific procedural obligations in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure/policy to explicitly require: (1) pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to determine energisation status (with reference to applicable testing method requirements), (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Also define/verify who is a “competent person” for testing (training, authorisation, and jurisdictional licensing where applicable) and maintain records of testing and competency.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern prerequisites for performing electrical work and the requirement that work be carried out by competent persons. The TARGET imposes specific preconditions before energised electrical work commences, including that the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person and recorded. The SOURCE discusses competency/licensing requirements for electrical workers, which is related to (but does not fully address) the TARGET’s procedural obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by stating electrical workers must be deemed competent and that competency may be demonstrated via licensing or practical assessment. However, it does not address the TARGET’s required controls before energised work begins: (a) conducting a risk assessment, (b) clearing obstructions for access/egress, (c) marking/clearing/ensuring operability of isolation points, (d) authorisation after consultation with workplace management/control, and (2) that the risk assessment is recorded. Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE (or associated procedure) to explicitly require, before energised electrical work starts: (1) a risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded; (2) the work area is cleared to allow easy access/exit; (3) isolation/disconnection points are clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly (or document the stated exception where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related because both address the requirement for electrical work/testing to be performed by a "competent person." The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person and that records are kept. The SOURCE discusses competency/licensing requirements for electrical workers and how competency must be demonstrated (including via practical assessment) depending on jurisdiction, which could be part of ensuring the tester is competent.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not meet the TARGET requirements. It only states that electrical workers must be competent and that licensing/competency varies by state/territory, with possible practical competency assessment. It does not address: (1) the obligation to regularly inspect and test specified equipment used in harsh/damaging environments; (2) the exemption/requirement for new and unused equipment (inspect for obvious damage before use); (3) the obligation to keep testing records until next test/removal; or (4) the required record contents (tester name, date, outcome, next test date) and tagging option. Therefore, it is not compliant with the TARGET as a control/procedure for inspection/testing and recordkeeping.
compliance.remediation: Add/implement a procedure that: (1) identifies in-scope equipment (plug-in equipment used in environments likely to cause damage); (2) sets inspection/testing intervals and ensures testing is performed by a competent person (define competency criteria, including any required licence/assessment by jurisdiction); (3) requires pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (4) mandates recordkeeping until next test or disposal/removal; and (5) specifies record fields (tester name, test date, outcome, next due date) and allows tagging as the record format.

matches.target_title: 95 Reassessment of competency of licence holder
matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets out general requirements for electrical workers to be competent and notes that licensing/competency requirements vary by Australian state/territory, with competency assessments used where licences are not required. The TARGET concerns a regulator’s power to require reassessment of a licence holder’s competency for high risk work under a high risk work licence. These address different regulatory schemes (electrical work licensing/competency vs high risk work licence reassessment) and the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill or implement the specific obligation/power described in the TARGET.

matches.target_title: 171 Competence of worker—general diving work—qualifications
matches.target_text:
(1) A person must not carry out any type of general diving work unless the person holds a certificate for general diving work, issued by a training organisation, that demonstrates that the person has acquired the relevant competencies for that type of general diving work.   
(2) This regulation does not apply in relation to incidental diving work or limited scientific diving work.   
(3) In subregulation (1), relevant competencies means the competencies specified in AS/NZS 2815 (Training and certification of occupational divers) that are relevant to the type of general diving work to which subregulation (1) applies.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE concerns competency and licensing requirements for electrical workers, varying by Australian state/territory, including practical competency assessments agreed by CSIRO where no licence is required. The TARGET sets a legal requirement for certificates and competencies for general diving work (with exceptions) and references AS/NZS 2815. These address different industries (electrical work vs occupational diving) and the SOURCE is not attempting to satisfy the TARGET’s obligations.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text addresses licensing/competency requirements for electrical workers by state/territory (i.e., who is competent/authorised to perform electrical work). The TARGET text imposes conditions under which energised electrical work may be performed (i.e., when live work is permitted/justified and alternatives considered). These are different obligations: competency/licensing does not demonstrate or address the necessity/justification controls for working on energised equipment. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligation in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address competency requirements for performing work that includes electrical aspects. The TARGET requires maintenance/inspection/testing of amusement devices/ropeways to be done by a competent person, and specifically requires that anyone performing a detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation must be qualified (or assisted by someone qualified) to inspect electrical installations. The SOURCE discusses electrical worker competency and notes that licensing/competency requirements vary by state/territory, with competency demonstrated via licensing or a practical assessment.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring electrical workers to be competent and acknowledging jurisdictional licensing/competency pathways. However, it does not explicitly address the TARGET’s specific obligation for detailed inspections of amusement devices/ropeways that include electrical installations—namely, that the inspector must be qualified to inspect electrical installations (or be assisted by someone who is). The SOURCE also does not mention maintenance/inspection/testing being performed in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or a maintenance manual. Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE policy/procedure to explicitly require that: (1) maintenance/inspection/testing of amusement devices or passenger ropeways is performed by a competent person and in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual; and (2) any detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation is performed only by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations, or by a competent person assisted by such a qualified person. Include jurisdiction-specific qualification/licensing criteria and verification steps.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both concern managing electrical safety at workplaces. The TARGET imposes a duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety from electrical risks (including installation, maintenance, and testing). The SOURCE addresses only one control element—ensuring electrical workers are competent/licensed per jurisdictional requirements and, where no licence is required, completing a practical competency assessment. While worker competency supports risk management, it does not demonstrate that the PCBU is managing electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (e.g., identifying hazards, implementing and maintaining risk controls, ensuring safe systems of work, inspection/testing regimes, and other required measures). Therefore, the SOURCE is insufficient to meet the broader obligation in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Expand the policy/procedure to explicitly require PCBU risk management for electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1, including hazard identification, risk assessment (as required), implementation of controls (e.g., isolation, RCDs, lockout/tagout, maintenance/testing schedules), verification/recordkeeping, and supervision/monitoring—while retaining the competency/licensing requirements as one control measure.

source_title: 4.2 Electrical work – who can do what?
source_text:
Electrical work, whether energised or de-energised, must only be carried out by a person who is deemed competent under applicable state/territory electrical legislation.

An electrical worker’s training and qualifications will impact the type of electrical work they can perform. Table 2 outlines what electrical work electrical workers in certain positions should be able to perform based on their qualifications and training.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” and lists exclusions and supervision/eligibility carve-outs. The SOURCE sets a rule about who may carry out electrical work (competent persons under applicable state/territory electrical legislation) and references qualifications/training determining what work can be performed. Because the SOURCE is governing performance of “electrical work,” it must align with the TARGET’s definition and exclusions.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET as written because it applies the “must only be carried out by a competent person” requirement to all electrical work “whether energised or de-energised,” without acknowledging that the TARGET explicitly excludes certain activities from the definition of electrical work (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs where safely performed by non-experts, certain supervised assistance, etc.). Under the TARGET, those excluded activities are not “electrical work,” so the SOURCE’s blanket restriction could incorrectly impose competency/legislative requirements on tasks that the TARGET says are not electrical work. Additionally, the SOURCE does not reflect the TARGET’s allowance for supervised assistance and eligibility-related work (non-energised) under specified conditions.
compliance.remediation: Revise the SOURCE to (1) adopt or reference the TARGET definition of “electrical work” and its exclusions, and (2) clarify that the competency/legislative requirement applies to activities that meet the definition of electrical work, while excluded tasks may be performed by others where permitted and safe. Also incorporate the TARGET’s supervision/assistant and eligibility provisions (e.g., supervised assistance without contact with energised equipment; eligibility work on non-energised equipment) and ensure any internal tables (e.g., Table 2) align with these categories.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets a requirement about who may perform electrical work (must be a competent person under applicable legislation) and references training/qualifications determining what work can be performed. The TARGET imposes specific duties on a PCBU for energised electrical work, including that it be carried out by a competent person and with additional controls (suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS, safety observer, and ensuring proper use). Because both address competency and conditions for performing electrical work (particularly energised work), a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET clause (1)(a) by requiring electrical work be done by a competent person. However, it does not address key TARGET requirements for energised electrical work: ensuring suitable/tested/maintained tools, testing equipment and PPE; performing work in accordance with a SWMS meeting specified content; having a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation (or documenting the testing-only/risk-assessment exception); and ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/PPE. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully fulfill the TARGET obligations and is non-compliant relative to the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE policy/procedure to explicitly require for energised electrical work: (1) use of suitable, properly tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) preparation and use of a SWMS that identifies the work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review; (3) a competent safety observer present, with documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months, or document the testing-only exception supported by a regulation 158(1)(a) risk assessment showing no serious risk; and (4) PCBU measures to ensure workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
Relevance: Both texts address controls around who may perform electrical work and the safe status of electrical equipment (energised vs de-energised). The SOURCE sets a competency requirement for performing electrical work, which is part of the broader safety framework implicated by the TARGET’s requirements before and during electrical work.

Compliance: The TARGET imposes specific duties on the PCBU to (1) ensure electrical equipment is tested by a competent person before work to determine if it is energised, (2) treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and (3) earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. The SOURCE does not mention mandatory pre-work testing to determine energisation, does not state the “treat as energised until isolated and proven de-energised” rule, and does not address earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fulfill the TARGET obligations and is non-compliant relative to the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE policy/procedure to explicitly require: (1) pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to determine energised status (with reference to applicable testing procedures), (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and verified de-energised, and (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Also assign responsibility to the PCBU for ensuring these steps occur before and during electrical work.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related because both concern prerequisites/controls for performing electrical work, specifically the requirement for competence. The TARGET imposes duties on a PCBU before energised electrical work commences, including that the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person. The SOURCE addresses who may carry out electrical work (competent under legislation) and notes training/qualifications affect what work can be performed. This overlaps with the TARGET’s competency requirement and broader preconditions for energised work.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not meet the TARGET requirements. While it establishes a general competency requirement for carrying out electrical work, it does not address the TARGET’s specific mandatory preconditions for energised electrical work: (a) conducting a risk assessment (and recording it), (b) ensuring the work area is clear for access/egress, (c) ensuring the isolation/disconnection point is labelled, unobstructed, and quickly operable (or addressing the stated exception), and (d) authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Therefore, the SOURCE is incomplete relative to the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/policy to explicitly require, before energised electrical work starts: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area is clear for safe access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled/marked, unobstructed, and quickly operable (or document applicability of the supply-side/not-accessible exception); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address controls around who may perform electrical work and under what conditions. The TARGET imposes a specific duty on a PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is not carried out unless strict necessity conditions are met. The SOURCE sets a competency/qualification requirement for electrical work (energised or de-energised), which is related to electrical work governance, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s core obligation: prohibiting energised electrical work unless one of the listed necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The SOURCE only states that electrical work must be performed by a competent person and that qualifications affect what work can be performed. This is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the TARGET’s requirement to avoid energised work except in narrowly defined circumstances.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit policy/procedure requiring energised electrical work to be avoided and only permitted when one of the TARGET conditions (a)-(d) is satisfied, including a documented justification/approval process. Reference testing requirements (including reg 155 where applicable) and require verification that no reasonable alternative exists before authorising energised work, in addition to the existing competency/qualification controls.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts regulate electrical work. The TARGET imposes a specific duty on a PCBU to ensure electrical work is not performed while equipment is energised (subject to exceptions). The SOURCE addresses who may perform electrical work (competency/qualifications) and does not address the energised-work prohibition, so a compliance check is necessary.
STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not include a requirement to ensure electrical work is not carried out on energised equipment, nor does it reference the TARGET’s prohibition or its exceptions/controls. Therefore it does not fulfill the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit requirement that electrical work must not be carried out on energised electrical equipment, except where permitted under the relevant Division, and specify the controls/authorization process for any permitted energised work (e.g., justification, risk assessment, isolation/LOTO, permits, competent persons, PPE, supervision). Also assign responsibility to the PCBU to ensure compliance.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern controls around electrical work and who/how work is performed. The TARGET imposes a specific duty on a PCBU during energised electrical work to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (i.e., prevent electrical risk). The SOURCE addresses competency/qualification requirements for persons performing electrical work, which is related to managing electrical work safely, but it does not address the TARGET’s specific requirement to prevent contact with exposed energised components while energised work is occurring.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state any measures (e.g., isolation, barriers, exclusion zones, insulating covers, supervision, access control) to prevent persons from inadvertently contacting exposed energised components during energised work. It only requires competent persons to perform electrical work and references training/qualification-based task allocation. Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements/procedures for energised electrical work to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components, such as: establishing exclusion zones and access control; using physical barriers/guards and insulating shrouds/covers; signage and spotters where needed; ensuring only authorised persons are present; and documenting these controls in an energised work method statement/permit and verifying implementation.

matches.target_title: 152 Application of Division 4
matches.target_text:
This Division does not apply to work carried out by or on behalf of an electricity supply authority on the electrical equipment, including electric line-associated equipment, controlled or operated by the authority to transform, transmit or supply electricity. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets a general requirement that electrical work (energised or de-energised) must be performed only by a person deemed competent under applicable electrical legislation, and notes that qualifications/training determine what work can be performed. The TARGET is an exemption/scope provision stating that a particular Division does not apply to work performed by or for an electricity supply authority on equipment it controls/operates for transforming/transmitting/supplying electricity. The TARGET does not impose a competency/qualification obligation; it defines when a Division is inapplicable. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill an obligation defined in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern electrical safety compliance and the use of a “competent person.” The SOURCE sets a competency requirement for carrying out electrical work, while the TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is inspected and tested by a competent person and that records are kept. Because inspection/testing is an electrical safety activity that must be performed by a competent person, the SOURCE is related but does not fully address the TARGET’s specific obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET because it does not: (1) require regular inspection and testing of in-scope electrical equipment (socket-outlet supplied and used in damaging environments); (2) address the exemption/requirement for new and unused equipment (inspect for obvious damage before use); (3) require keeping testing records until next test or removal/disposal; or (4) specify required record contents (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) or allow tagging as a record. It only states that electrical work must be done by a competent person and references training/qualifications, which is insufficient to meet the TARGET’s inspection/testing and recordkeeping duties.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements that the PCBU must: (1) ensure in-scope electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person; (2) for new/unused equipment, inspect for obvious damage before first use and clarify the exemption from initial testing; (3) maintain records of testing until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed; and (4) ensure records include tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date (e.g., via a tag). Also define/align “competent person” with the applicable WHS/electrical safety regulatory definition for inspection/testing.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address electrical safety obligations at a workplace. The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks. The SOURCE describes a control measure/requirement about who may perform electrical work (competent persons under electrical legislation), which is one way a PCBU could manage electrical risk. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET obligation because it only addresses worker competency/authorization for performing electrical work. The TARGET requires broader risk management for electrical risks (e.g., design, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) in accordance with Part 3.1, which typically includes identifying hazards, assessing risks, implementing and maintaining controls, and reviewing control effectiveness. The SOURCE does not state that the PCBU must manage electrical risks, does not reference Part 3.1 processes, and does not cover the broader lifecycle activities listed in the TARGET example.
compliance.remediation: Expand the SOURCE requirement into a PCBU duty statement aligned to Part 3.1 risk management: explicitly require identification of electrical hazards, risk assessment (as required), implementation of controls (including ensuring only competent/licensed persons perform electrical work), maintenance/testing regimes, and periodic review of controls for electrical equipment/installations across design, installation, protection, maintenance and testing.

source_text:
Electrical workers must complete a practical electrical competency assessment prior to carrying out electrical work if an appropriate electrical licence is not required by their local state/territory regulator. Table 1 Electrical licence and competency assessment requirements by state/territory identifies when an electrical competency assessment is required.

The electrical competency assessment evaluates whether an electrical worker has the necessary skills to carry out electrical work on non-fixed electrical equipment and can demonstrate safe work practices in line with the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure.

An electrical worker, if applicable, must successfully pass an electrical competency assessment as agreed by CSIRO within the past 5 years.

This assessment does not permit electrical workers to carry out any type of electrical work that requires an appropriate electrical licence within their local state or territory.

Refer to Appendix 2 of this guideline for the electrical competency assessment template that must be used.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address competency requirements for performing electrical work. The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is carried out by a competent person (with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE), in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), and (generally) with a competent safety observer who has been assessed within the previous 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. The SOURCE describes a CSIRO electrical competency assessment process for electrical workers (primarily for non-fixed equipment and where a licence is not required). This is functionally related because the SOURCE is a mechanism to establish/maintain worker competency, which is a prerequisite under the TARGET for energised electrical work.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially supports the TARGET’s competency requirement by requiring a practical competency assessment and safe work practices, but it does not cover several mandatory TARGET elements for energised electrical work: (1) it does not require or mention a SWMS with the specified contents; (2) it does not require a safety observer, nor specify observer competence and 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment; (3) it does not address ensuring tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, properly tested, maintained, and properly used; (4) the SOURCE assessment currency is 5 years, which does not meet the TARGET’s 12-month assessment requirement for the safety observer (and does not explicitly address energised work competency at all). Therefore, as a control for compliance with the TARGET obligations for energised electrical work, the SOURCE is insufficient and non-compliant.
compliance.remediation: Update the CSIRO guideline/procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) a SWMS for energised electrical work meeting the TARGET content requirements; (2) a safety observer unless the testing-only + risk assessment exception applies, and require the observer to be competent and assessed within the previous 12 months in rescue and resuscitation; (3) controls ensuring tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable, properly tested, maintained, and used properly; (4) clarify that the competency assessment covers energised electrical work (or add a separate energised-work authorisation/competency process) and align any relevant refresher/assessment intervals with regulatory requirements.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal requirements for electrical workers to complete a competency assessment (where a licence is not required) before performing certain electrical work, and clarifies that the assessment does not substitute for licensing. The TARGET imposes specific preconditions on a PCBU before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility, and authorisation). These address different obligations (worker competency vs. pre-work controls for energised work). The SOURCE does not appear to be intended to fulfill the TARGET’s requirements, nor does it address energised electrical work controls, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded, which determines when licensing/registration requirements may apply. The SOURCE sets an internal CSIRO requirement for a competency assessment for electrical workers performing certain electrical work when a local licence is not required, and explicitly states it does not authorise licensed work. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary to ensure the SOURCE’s internal controls align with the TARGET’s scope/definitions and do not inadvertently permit unlicensed electrical work.

STEP 2 (Compliance): Not fully compliant/aligned. The SOURCE relies on the concept “electrical work” but does not define it or explicitly align its scope to the TARGET’s definition/exclusions. It states the assessment is for work on “non-fixed electrical equipment,” but the TARGET’s definition of electrical work is broader (includes installing/testing/repairing/maintaining electrical equipment or installations) and also lists exclusions (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs). Without incorporating these definitions/exclusions, the SOURCE could (a) require assessments for activities that the TARGET says are not “electrical work,” and/or (b) fail to clearly prevent assessed-but-unlicensed workers from performing tasks that are “electrical work” under the TARGET and may require licensing depending on jurisdiction. The SOURCE’s disclaimer about not permitting licensed work helps, but it does not operationally map tasks to the TARGET’s categories and jurisdictional notes.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE guideline to explicitly adopt or reference the jurisdiction’s definition of “electrical work” (including exclusions) and add a task-based decision framework: (1) determine whether the activity is “electrical work” per TARGET; (2) if yes, determine whether local licensing/registration is required; (3) if licence required, prohibit unless appropriately licensed/registered; (4) if licence not required, require CSIRO competency assessment (within 5 years) where applicable. Also clarify how “non-fixed electrical equipment” maps to the TARGET categories and include jurisdictional notes/links to state/territory regulators.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern prerequisites and safety controls before performing electrical work. The TARGET imposes a specific pre-work obligation: electrical equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and exposed parts must be treated as energised until proven otherwise (and HV parts earthed after de-energising). The SOURCE addresses worker competency assessment/licensing requirements before carrying out electrical work, which is related to ensuring competent performance of electrical tasks.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state that, before electrical work is carried out, the equipment must be tested to determine whether it is energised, nor does it require treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, nor earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. It focuses on competency assessment and explicitly notes it does not permit licensed work, but it does not implement the TARGET’s specific testing and exposed-part handling requirements. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/guideline to explicitly require that before any electrical work on electrical equipment: (1) the equipment is tested by a competent person to confirm whether it is energised (with reference to the applicable testing method/procedure), (2) exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Define/align the meaning of “competent person” and cross-reference the relevant testing/isolation procedures.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal requirements for electrical workers to complete a practical electrical competency assessment (where a licence is not required) before performing electrical work on non-fixed equipment, including a 5-year recency requirement and use of a specific template.
