The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested in hostile operating environments, and to keep specified testing records/tags. These address different compliance domains: worker competency/licensing vs equipment inspection/testing and recordkeeping. The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET’s inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_text:
The regulator may direct a licence holder to obtain a reassessment of the competency of the licence holder to carry out the high risk work covered by the licence if the regulator reasonably believes that the licence holder may not be competent to carry out that work. 

Examples 

1 The training or competency assessment of the licence holder did not meet the standard required to hold the licence.   
2 The regulator receives information that the licence holder has carried out high risk work incompetently. 

Subdivision 3 Amendment of licence document

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal CSIRO requirement for electrical workers to complete a practical electrical competency assessment (where a local electrical licence is not required) and clarifies it does not substitute for any legally required electrical licence. The TARGET concerns a regulator-directed reassessment of a licence holder’s competency for high risk work covered by a licence. These address different regimes (internal competency assessment for non-licensed electrical work vs regulatory reassessment tied to an existing high-risk work licence), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill an obligation defined in the TARGET.

matches.target_title: 114 Accredited assessor must act in accordance with accreditation
matches.target_text:
(1) An accredited assessor must not conduct a competency assessment unless: 

(a) the competency assessment relates to a class of high risk work for which the assessor is accredited; and   
(b) the accredited assessor conducts the competency assessment for or on behalf of an RTO. 

(2) An accredited assessor must not issue a notice of satisfactory assessment unless the competency assessment relates to a class of high risk work for which the assessor is accredited. 

(3) An accredited assessor who conducts a competency assessment must do so in accordance with the conditions of accreditation imposed under regulation 121. 

(4) An accredited assessor who issues a notice of satisfactory assessment must do so in accordance with any conditions of accreditation imposed under regulation 121. 

(5) Subregulations (1) to (4) do not apply if the regulator is the accredited assessor. 

Note 

See section 43 of the Act.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal requirements for electrical workers to complete an electrical competency assessment (for non-licensed electrical work on non-fixed equipment) and clarifies it does not substitute for any legally required electrical licence. The TARGET regulates how an accredited assessor may conduct and certify competency assessments for classes of high risk work, including requirements that the assessor be accredited for that class and act for/on behalf of an RTO. The SOURCE does not reference high risk work, accredited assessors, RTOs, or issuing notices of satisfactory assessment under the cited regulatory framework. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not necessary based on the provided texts alone.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal requirements for electrical workers to complete a competency assessment (where a licence is not required) before performing certain electrical work on non-fixed electrical equipment, and clarifies it does not authorise licensed work. The TARGET imposes a legal duty on a PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is not carried out unless specific necessity-based exceptions apply. The SOURCE does not address energised electrical work conditions, necessity tests, or controls for working live; therefore it is not attempting to fulfil the TARGET obligation and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal requirements for CSIRO electrical workers to complete an electrical competency assessment (for certain electrical work on non-fixed equipment) when a local electrical licence is not required, and clarifies it does not substitute for any legally required electrical licence. The TARGET imposes duties on the person with management/control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is performed by a competent person and per manufacturer/manual, and adds a specific qualification requirement for detailed inspections that include an electrical installation. These address different regulated activities (amusement devices/ropeways vs CSIRO electrical work generally) and different competency/qualification frameworks. There is no clear indication the SOURCE is intended to satisfy the TARGET obligation, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety from electrical risks at the workplace. The SOURCE sets internal requirements for electrical workers to complete a competency assessment (where a licence is not required) and to demonstrate safe work practices. This is a control measure aimed at managing electrical risks, so a compliance check is relevant.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially supports the TARGET by requiring competency assessment and safe work practices for certain electrical work. However, the TARGET requires the PCBU to manage electrical risks broadly (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) in accordance with Part 3.1, which typically includes systematic risk management (identify hazards, assess risks, implement and maintain controls, review controls). The SOURCE does not explicitly establish a comprehensive risk management process for electrical risks at the workplace, does not assign PCBU responsibilities, and focuses narrowly on worker competency/licensing boundaries. Therefore, it does not fully demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation on its own.
compliance.remediation: Augment the SOURCE (or link it to a broader electrical risk management procedure) to explicitly require the PCBU to manage electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1, including: hazard identification, risk assessment (where required), implementation of controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, RCDs, inspection/testing regimes, maintenance, design/installation controls), training/competency as one control, recordkeeping, and periodic review of control effectiveness. Clarify roles/responsibilities and ensure coverage across the lifecycle activities listed in the TARGET (design through testing).

source_text:
The verification of electrical competency must establish whether the electrical worker:

1. Has the licences, qualifications, training and experience necessary to safely carry out the required electrical work; and   
2. Can demonstrate an understanding of the specific risks and control measures needed to carry out specific electrical work tasks.

What to consider when completing a verification form?

Prior to carrying out electrical work in CSIRO, an electrical worker must complete and submit a verification of electrical competency form to their line manager for consideration and approval.

When completing the form consider the following:

a. How do you plan on carrying out the electrical work – i.e. is it de-energised or energised? If energised, do you have an appropriate electrical licence? An application to undertake energised electrical work need to be completed to obtain permission to work energised.   
b. Does your training/qualifications/licences/experience address the competencies required for the electrical tasks you intend to carry out? – copies of qualifications/training/licences need to be attached to the verification form.   
c. Have you completed Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, if applicable, for the electrical work you intend to carry out – copies of the risk assessment and SWIs need to be attached to the verification form.

What to consider when reviewing and approving a verification form?

The Line Manager along with the Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) should review the details in Part 2 & 3 of a submitted Verification of electrical competency.

When completing Part 4 & 5, discuss the electrical tasks to be performed and consider the following:

a. Does the licence/qualification/training/experience address the work described in Part 2? i.e. Do they require an electrical licence, additional training, equipment/tools, supervision?   
b. Any of the electrical work tasks need to be performed energised – an application to undertake energised electrical work needs to be completed and submitted by the electrical worker/competent person to obtain permission to work energised.   
c. Was there a previous electrical approval – is this a renewal or has there been a change in the electrical tasks?   
d. Has the worker provided copies of licences, certificates, qualification – copies must be provided. If the worker is unable to provide copies, approval cannot be given, and electrical work must be ceased until all supporting documentation provided.   
e. Are the licences, certificates, qualifications still in date and valid e.g. some licences have a review/renewal date. If out of date, the worker should be notified, and it noted on the verification form.   
f. Have all known risks and hazards been assessed for the work to be undertaken, has it been documented and have the control measures been implemented - copies of Activity Risk Assessments and Safe Work Instructions must be provided for the electrical tasks. If the worker is unable to provide copies, approval cannot be given, and electrical work must cease until the completed risk assessments and SWI, if applicable, have been provided.   
g. Are there further requirements for the worker to be approved - where it has been identified that additional training, supervision, procedures, etc are required, it is to be noted on the workers verification form and actioned appropriately.

Setting conditions and limitations to electrical work

Upon reviewing and approving verification form, a Line Manager may want to set conditions or limitation to what electrical work an electrical worker can carry out based on the risk and competency of the work.

Examples of conditions and limitations include:

 Electrical work is restricted to testing only, (no working on energised circuits).   
 Working on energised circuits strictly in accordance with the conditions of an electrical licence.  Working strictly under supervision by an electrically competent and qualified electrical worker. Isolate, tag, de- energise using appropriate PPE and work on de-energised equipment or installation.   
Restricted to electrical measurements on live external terminals, with appropriate PPE. Replacement of components in accordance with engineering specifications, like-for-like on deenergised circuits.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes CSIRO’s internal process for verifying electrical competency and approving electrical work, including considerations for energised work, risk assessments, SWIs, and conditions/limitations. The TARGET sets specific legal duties for a PCBU when energised electrical work is carried out (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE; safe work method statement with required content; safety observer with specified competence and recent rescue/resuscitation assessment; and limited exception for testing with a risk assessment showing no serious risk). Because the SOURCE procedure is intended to control/authorise electrical work (including energised work), it is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET obligations and warrants a compliance check.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is partially aligned but does not fully meet the TARGET requirements for energised electrical work. It requires competency verification (licences/qualifications/training/experience) and requires risk assessments and SWIs, and it requires a separate application/permission process for energised work. However, it does not explicitly require: (1) ensuring tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, properly tested, and maintained in good working order; (2) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; (3) a safe work method statement with all mandated elements (identify work; hazards/risks; control measures; implementation/monitoring/review) — SWIs and activity risk assessments may cover some elements but are not stated to meet the specific SWMS content requirements; (4) a safety observer for energised work, including the requirement that the observer be assessed within the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate; and (5) the specific exemption logic for testing-only work tied to a risk assessment showing no serious risk. Due to these missing explicit controls, the SOURCE cannot be confirmed compliant with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/forms to explicitly incorporate TARGET requirements for energised electrical work: (1) require verification that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable for the task, have been properly tested (where applicable), and are maintained; (2) require the PCBU/manager to ensure (so far as reasonably practicable) workers use such equipment properly (e.g., supervision/spot checks/training sign-off); (3) require a SWMS (or explicitly define that the SWI + risk assessment package constitutes a SWMS) that includes all elements in TARGET(3)(a)-(d); (4) mandate a safety observer for energised work and specify observer competency plus documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months; (5) document the testing-only exception criteria, requiring a risk assessment demonstrating no serious risk when no safety observer is used.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets an internal process for verifying electrical worker competency and approving/controlling electrical work, including energised work. The TARGET sets mandatory preconditions a PCBU must ensure before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment, clear access/egress, isolation point requirements, and authorisation after consultation). Because the SOURCE procedure is intended to govern/permit energised electrical work, it is functionally related and should be checked against the TARGET obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns but does not fully satisfy the TARGET requirements. It requires risk assessments/SWIs to be completed and provided, and it requires an application/permission process for energised work, which supports TARGET (1)(a), (2) and (1)(d) in principle. However, it does not explicitly require that, before energised work starts: (i) the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access and exit (TARGET 1(b)); (ii) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly (TARGET 1(c)(i)-(iii)); and (iii) the authorisation occurs after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace (TARGET 1(d))—the SOURCE mentions line manager/ESO approval but not this specific consultation requirement. Additionally, while the SOURCE requires risk assessments, it does not explicitly state they must be conducted by a competent person and recorded for the purposes of energised work (TARGET 2(a)-(b)), though documentation is implied. Due to these missing explicit controls/requirements, the SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/forms to explicitly require, as mandatory preconditions for any energised electrical work: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) confirmation/checklist that the work area is clear of obstructions for easy access/exit; (3) confirmation/checklist that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, readily accessible for exit, and operable quickly (or document applicability/exemption where relevant); and (4) documented authorisation by the PCBU/authorising person after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. Incorporate these items into the energised work application and line manager/ESO approval workflow.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets internal requirements for verifying electrical worker competency and approving electrical work (including conditions for energised/de-energised work, risk assessments, SWIs, and approvals). The TARGET imposes specific WHS-style obligations on a PCBU before electrical work is carried out: (1) test equipment by a competent person to determine if it is energised; (2) treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Because the SOURCE governs how electrical work is authorised and controlled, it is functionally related to (and should help ensure compliance with) the TARGET obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require that, before electrical work on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised. It discusses planning whether work is de-energised or energised and mentions isolate/tag/de-energise in example limitations, but it does not mandate pre-work testing as a required step. The SOURCE also does not clearly state that exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised, nor does it address the requirement to earth each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully cover the TARGET’s specific procedural obligations and is not compliant as a control document for those requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/forms/SWIs to explicitly require: (1) mandatory pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to confirm energised/de-energised status (with reference to the applicable testing method/SWI); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage work, mandatory earthing of exposed parts after de-energising. Ensure these steps are embedded as approval prerequisites (e.g., checklist items in the verification/approval sections) and reflected in required risk assessments/SWIs.

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal process for verifying an electrical worker’s competency (licences, training, risk assessments, approvals/conditions for electrical work, including energised work). The TARGET imposes a different duty on the PCBU: to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person in specified harsh environments, and to keep specific testing records (tester name, date, outcome, next test date; tag allowed). The SOURCE does not address inspection/testing of equipment, testing frequency, applicability conditions (socket outlet + damaging environment), or the required testing records. Therefore the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. The SOURCE establishes an internal process for verifying electrical competency and setting conditions/limitations before a worker performs electrical work. Because the SOURCE process is intended to govern who may perform electrical work and under what conditions, it is functionally related to the TARGET definitions and should be checked to ensure it correctly scopes and applies to the kinds of work that legally count as “electrical work” (and does not over/under-apply to excluded activities).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not align with the TARGET in scope/definitions. The SOURCE broadly applies to “electrical work” in CSIRO and requires verification/approval, risk assessments, and (for energised work) an application/permission process. However, it does not distinguish between (i) electrical work as defined in the TARGET and (ii) activities explicitly excluded from “electrical work” (e.g., plug-in connection via cord and socket, non-electrical component work with no electrical risk, replacing a fuse/light bulb where safely done by non-experts, certain supervised conduit/duct work under specified conditions, assisting under direct supervision without contact with energised equipment, etc.). Without incorporating these exclusions/conditions, the SOURCE may incorrectly require electrical-worker verification for tasks that are not “electrical work” under the TARGET, and it also lacks explicit mapping to the TARGET’s conditional exclusions (e.g., supervision and de-energised requirements for ducts/conduits/troughs). Therefore, it is not compliant with the TARGET’s definitional boundaries (at minimum, it is incomplete/insufficiently specified relative to the TARGET).
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/form and guidance to explicitly adopt or reference the TARGET definition of “electrical work” and list the exclusions/conditional exclusions. Add a decision step (or checklist) to classify the task as (1) electrical work, (2) excluded activity, or (3) excluded only if conditions are met (e.g., de-energised + supervised). Ensure verification/approval requirements apply only to tasks that meet the TARGET definition (or clearly justify any stricter internal requirement), and document supervision/de-energisation conditions where the TARGET requires them.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. The SOURCE describes an internal process for verifying electrical worker competency, requiring risk assessments/SWIs, approvals, and controls for energised work. This is functionally related because competency verification and task controls are mechanisms a PCBU may use to manage electrical risks.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially supports the TARGET duty by requiring verification of licences/training/experience, understanding of risks and controls, documented risk assessments and SWIs, and additional permission for energised work. However, the TARGET duty is broader (managing electrical risks across design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, and testing of electrical equipment/installations) and requires risk management in accordance with Part 3.1. The SOURCE does not explicitly establish a comprehensive risk management system aligned to Part 3.1 (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment where required, implementation of controls using the hierarchy of controls, and ongoing review/monitoring) for all electrical risks at the workplace; it focuses mainly on worker competency verification and approval conditions. Therefore, based on the provided text alone, it cannot be confirmed that the PCBU is managing electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1, so it is not fully compliant with the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Expand or link the procedure to a documented electrical risk management framework that explicitly meets Part 3.1 requirements (identify hazards, assess risks where required, implement controls using the hierarchy of controls, and review control effectiveness) across the full lifecycle of electrical equipment/installations (design through maintenance/testing). Ensure the competency verification process is integrated as one control within that broader system, with clear responsibilities, monitoring, and review.

source_text:
Section 154 of the WHS Regulations states that electrical work is not to be carried out on energised electrical equipment. However, exemptions do apply in certain circumstances provided in Section 157 of the regulations. Refer to Section 7 of this guideline for more information about energised electrical work requirements.

Wherever possible electrical work must be carried out de-energised, this includes testing and fault finding. All possible de-energised working methods must be identified and tried before working energised should be considered.

‘Test for Dead’ before you touch

Before electrical work is carried out, the equipment needs to be tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. You need to ensure:

 Each exposed part of the equipment is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
 Each high voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised.

Electrical equipment that is de-energised must not be inadvertently energised while work is being carried out.

Work on Electrical Installations

Electrical work on an installation involving any part thereof, in service following construction, alteration or repair, it shall be verified, as far as practicable, that the installation is safe to energise and will operate in accordance with the requirements of AS 3000. Precautions shall be taken to ensure the safety of persons and to avoid damage to property and the electrical installation equipment during inspecting and testing.

Where electrical installation is an alteration or repair to an existing electrical installation, it shall be verified that the alteration or repair complies with this AS 3000 and does not impair the safety of the existing electrical installation.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking:

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of.

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

source_text:
All electrical tools and equipment should be suitable for the work and be maintained in good working order, including regular maintenance, inspection and testing. Inadequate maintenance may lead to serious electrical risks, for example an insulating medium might conceal a mechanical defect that could cause an open circuit in a testing device. Where any doubt exists about adequacy of the insulation of tools and equipment they should not be used and removed from service.

Insulating barriers and insulating mats

Insulating covers and mats used for electrical purposes should comply with AS/NZS2978: Insulating mats for electrical purposes. Insulated barriers should be of suitable material to effectively separate electrical workers from adjacent energised electrical equipment or energised parts at a higher voltage e.g. protection from low voltage energised parts when working with extra-low voltage. These items should be visually inspected before and after each use. If the items have a defect that adversely affect the performance, then it must be removed from service.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE provides operational guidance on maintaining electrical tools/equipment and removing them from service when insulation adequacy is in doubt or defects are found. The TARGET imposes a legal duty to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/removed. Both address handling of unsafe electrical equipment, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE requires that doubtful/defective tools and insulating mats/barriers be 'not used' and 'removed from service' after inspection, which aligns partially with the TARGET's intent. However, it does not explicitly require disconnection/isolating from the electricity supply, nor does it state the conditions for re-connection (only after repair/testing found safe) or replacement/permanent removal. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend procedures to explicitly require that any electrical equipment reasonably believed to be unsafe is disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and only returned to service after repair or testing confirms it is safe; otherwise it must be replaced or permanently removed from use. Include clear criteria for 'unsafe' and documented controls (lockout/tagout, records of testing/repair).

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general duty for a PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per Part 3.1). The SOURCE provides specific controls and practices (maintenance/inspection/testing of electrical tools and equipment; inspection/removal-from-service criteria for insulating mats/barriers) that are directly within the scope of managing electrical risks. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE describes specific risk controls (suitability and maintenance of tools/equipment; do-not-use/remove-from-service when insulation adequacy is in doubt; compliance with AS/NZS2978 for insulating mats; visual inspection before/after use; removal from service if defective). However, the TARGET requires the PCBU to "manage risks" in accordance with Part 3.1, which typically includes a broader risk management process (identify hazards, assess risks if required, implement and maintain controls, and review controls). The SOURCE does not explicitly address the overall risk management process, responsibilities of the PCBU, or systematic risk management requirements across design/construction/installation/protection activities mentioned in the TARGET example. As written, the SOURCE is only a partial subset of controls and does not demonstrate full alignment with the broader duty to manage electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1.
compliance.remediation: Augment the SOURCE content to explicitly align with Part 3.1 risk management requirements by: (1) stating the PCBU duty to identify electrical hazards, assess risks where necessary, implement controls using the hierarchy of control, and maintain/review those controls; (2) expanding coverage beyond tools/mats to include electrical installations and lifecycle activities (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) as applicable; (3) documenting procedures/records for inspections, testing, maintenance, defect reporting, removal-from-service, and periodic review/audit of control effectiveness.

matches.target_title: 151 Untested electrical equipment not to be used
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that electrical equipment is not used if the equipment: 

(a) is required to be tested under regulation 150; and (b) has not been tested. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address conditions under which electrical equipment should or should not be used. The TARGET imposes a specific legal duty not to use equipment that is required to be tested under regulation 150 unless it has been tested. The SOURCE discusses maintenance/inspection/testing generally and removal from service where insulation adequacy is in doubt, which is functionally related to controlling electrical equipment use.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require that equipment subject to mandatory testing under regulation 150 must not be used unless it has been tested. It refers to regular maintenance, inspection and testing, and removal from service when insulation is doubtful or when insulating mats/barriers are defective, but it does not establish the specific prohibition tied to the regulatory testing requirement (reg 150) or the condition 'has not been tested'. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit requirement/procedure stating that any electrical equipment that is required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used unless it has been tested (and is within the required test interval), including controls such as tagging/records, pre-use verification, and removal from service until testing is completed.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The TARGET imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure that during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. The SOURCE discusses suitability, maintenance, inspection/testing of electrical tools/equipment, and the use/inspection/removal-from-service of insulating barriers and mats. These controls are functionally related because insulating barriers/mats and properly maintained tools are common measures to prevent inadvertent contact and resulting electrical risk during energised work. However, the SOURCE does not explicitly require ensuring 'all persons' are prevented from contact, does not address exposed energised components specifically, and frames requirements as 'should' rather than 'must', so it does not fully meet the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the procedure to explicitly require that, during energised electrical work, the PCBU must implement controls to prevent any person from inadvertently contacting exposed energised components (e.g., insulating barriers/covers, shrouding, exclusion zones, supervision, access control). Replace advisory language ('should') with mandatory requirements ('must') and include verification steps (pre-job checks, monitoring during work) demonstrating the prevention of inadvertent contact.

(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.
