The SOURCE provides guidance on maintaining and inspecting electrical tools/equipment and insulating mats/barriers; the TARGET imposes a legal duty on the person with management/control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is carried out by a competent person and per manufacturer/designer recommendations or a competent-person maintenance manual, and adds a qualification requirement for detailed inspections involving electrical installations. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not meet key TARGET requirements. It does not (a) assign responsibility to the person with management/control of an amusement device/ropeway, (b) require maintenance/inspection/testing to be performed by a competent person, (c) require adherence to designer/manufacturer recommendations or a formal maintenance manual prepared by a competent person, or (d) address the specific competency/qualification requirement for detailed inspections that include an electrical installation. The SOURCE is general electrical safety guidance and standards for insulating mats/barriers, not a maintenance/inspection/testing governance framework for amusement devices/ropeways as required by the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Create/implement a maintenance and inspection program for the amusement device/passenger ropeway that: (1) is owned by the person with management/control; (2) specifies maintenance, inspection and testing intervals and methods in accordance with manufacturer/designer recommendations or a maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; (3) ensures the work is performed by competent persons; and (4) ensures any detailed inspection involving electrical installations is performed by a qualified electrical inspector or by a competent person assisted by a qualified person. Retain records demonstrating competency/qualifications and completion of required activities.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets operational safety requirements for electrical tools/equipment (maintenance, inspection/testing, removal from service if insulation is doubtful) and for insulating mats/barriers (standard compliance, inspection before/after use, removal if defective). The TARGET only defines what counts as “electrical equipment” (and exclusions) for the purposes of a legal Part; it does not impose maintenance/inspection/standard-compliance obligations. Because the TARGET is definitional rather than an obligation that the SOURCE is attempting to satisfy, there is no direct compliance relationship to check.

source_text:
If an electrical worker needs to carry out electrical work on a site other than their home site, they should notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) for the site they plan to work on of their intention to conduct electrical work.

Upon request, electrical workers should provide the ESO with a copy of their completed Verification of electrical competency, Activity risk assessment and SWI for review.

Example – Notifying a Site ESO when working on a different site

An Electrical worker from Bribie Island needs to go to St Lucia to carry out electrical testing of instrumentation. If there is a Site ESO at St Lucia, the worker should the Site ESO of their plans to conduct electrical work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural expectation: when an electrical worker performs work at a site other than their home site, they should notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer and provide competency verification/risk assessment/SWI upon request. The TARGET imposes specific legal duties on a PCBU for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, SWMS requirements, safety observer requirements and exceptions). The SOURCE does not address energised work controls, SWMS content/requirements, safety observer, or PCBU obligations; it focuses on site notification/coordination. Therefore the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the obligations defined in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural expectation: when an electrical worker performs work at a site other than their home site, they should notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer and provide competency verification/risk assessment/SWI upon request. The TARGET imposes specific legal duties on a PCBU before energised electrical work commences (conduct and record a risk assessment by a competent person, ensure access/egress and isolation point marking/accessibility, and authorisation after consultation with workplace management/control). While both concern electrical safety, the SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s required controls for energised work (area/isolator conditions, authorisation/consultation, recorded risk assessment requirements) and is not clearly intended to fulfill those specific obligations. Therefore a direct compliance check between these two texts is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) when performing electrical work at a site other than their home site, and to provide competency verification/risk assessment/SWI upon request. The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and to treat exposed parts as energised until isolated/tested, with additional earthing requirements for high-voltage exposed parts. Notification to an ESO and document provision do not address the TARGET’s specific obligations regarding testing for energisation, treating exposed parts as energised, or earthing high-voltage parts. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation and a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement: when an electrical worker performs electrical work at a site other than their home site, they must notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer and provide competency/RA/SWI documents upon request. The TARGET text is definitional, specifying what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. It does not impose any obligation about notifying a Site ESO or providing documentation. Because the TARGET does not define a requirement that the SOURCE is attempting to satisfy (it only defines scope), a direct compliance check between the two is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning: The TARGET sets a general legal duty on a PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace (WHS duty). The SOURCE describes an administrative control/procedure for electrical workers working away from their home site (notifying the Site Electrical Safety Officer and providing competency verification and risk assessment documents). This procedure is functionally related because it can be part of how a PCBU manages electrical risks across sites. However, the SOURCE alone does not demonstrate that the PCBU is managing electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (e.g., identifying hazards, implementing and maintaining risk controls for design/installation/maintenance/testing, ensuring safe systems of work, etc.). It only addresses notification and document review, which is insufficient to meet the broader duty described in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Expand the procedure to explicitly align with Part 3.1 risk management requirements: require identification of electrical hazards at the host site, assessment of risks, implementation of controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, testing and tagging, access/permit controls, supervision/competency verification), consultation/coordination between home and host site, and documented review/monitoring of controls. Clarify PCBU responsibilities and ensure the Site ESO review results in required controls being implemented before work starts.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) when performing electrical work at a site other than their home site, and to provide competency verification/risk assessment/SWI on request. The TARGET imposes a legal duty on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested, and that testing records are kept with specified details. These address different obligations (work authorization/notification vs equipment inspection/testing and recordkeeping) and the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the applicable Site Electrical Safety Officer when performing electrical work at a site other than their home site, and to provide competency verification/risk assessment/SWI upon request. The TARGET sets a legal duty on a PCBU restricting when electrical work on energised electrical equipment may be carried out (only under specific necessity conditions). These address different obligations (site notification/authorization vs. energized-work permissibility) and the SOURCE does not attempt to satisfy or implement the TARGET’s energized-work restrictions.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) when performing electrical work at a different site and to provide competency/assessment documents upon request. The TARGET imposes a duty on a PCBU to prevent persons from creating electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components while energised electrical work is being carried out. These address different controls (administrative notification/document review vs. physical/operational risk controls during energised work). The SOURCE does not attempt to implement or evidence the TARGET obligation, so a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) when performing electrical work at a different site and to provide competency verification, risk assessment, and SWI upon request. The TARGET imposes a legal duty on a PCBU to maintain safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, and if not practicable, to conduct a risk assessment and implement control measures consistent with the assessment and any electricity authority requirements. These address different hazards and duty-holders (worker notification/authorization vs. PCBU safe-distance controls around electric lines). The SOURCE does not purport to implement or evidence compliance with the TARGET obligation, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets an internal procedural requirement for electrical workers to notify the Site Electrical Safety Officer when performing electrical work at a site other than their home site, and to provide competency verification and risk documentation upon request. The TARGET imposes a legal duty on a PCBU conducting construction work to comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for electrical installations on construction/demolition sites. The SOURCE does not address compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019, construction/demolition site electrical installation requirements, or PCBU-level obligations; it is about site notification and document review. Therefore, the texts are not functionally related in a way that enables a direct compliance check.

source_text: To ensure that electrical work on de-energised equipment cannot be inadvertently re-energised, workers are required to isolate the equipment at the electrical supply source. The switch must be locked out and tagged to prevent re-energisation. Workers must test the electrical equipment to confirm that it has been de-energised before commencing work. The worker should use personal locks and danger tags at the isolation point to prevent the risk of inadvertent re-energisation.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
Relevance: Both texts address controls required before/when performing electrical work, specifically around isolation/disconnection points and preventing inadvertent energisation. The SOURCE describes lockout/tagout and testing for de-energised work, which is functionally related to the TARGET’s requirements that must be ensured before electrical work on energised equipment commences (including risk assessment, access/egress, clearly marked and accessible isolation point, and authorisation).

Compliance: The SOURCE does not address several mandatory elements in the TARGET: (1)(a) conducting a risk assessment, including that it be conducted by a competent person and recorded (2); (1)(b) ensuring the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c)(i)-(iii) ensuring the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly; and (1)(d) authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. While the SOURCE includes isolation, lockout/tagging, and testing for de-energised status, it does not demonstrate these additional prerequisites, so it is not compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Add procedures to: (1) conduct a documented risk assessment by a competent person before commencing energised electrical work; (2) ensure the work area and isolation point are free of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) ensure the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled and can be operated quickly; and (4) require formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes controls for working on de-energised electrical equipment, including isolation/lockout/tagout and testing to confirm de-energisation. The TARGET imposes duties on the PCBU to ensure testing before electrical work and to treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and determined not energised, plus earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. These are functionally related because the SOURCE is describing practices intended to meet the TARGET’s requirements.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with TARGET (1) by requiring testing to confirm equipment is de-energised before commencing work. However, it does not explicitly require that the testing be performed by a competent person, as required by TARGET (1). It also does not address TARGET (2)(a) (treat each exposed part as energised until isolated and determined not energised) or TARGET (2)(b) (earthing each high-voltage exposed part after being de-energised). Therefore, the SOURCE is only partially aligned and is not fully compliant with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure to: (1) explicitly require pre-work testing by a competent person; (2) state that all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) require earthing of high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation (where applicable), consistent with the applicable testing method requirements.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to ensure de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised during electrical work. The SOURCE describes controls (isolation at supply source, lockout/tagout, personal locks and danger tags, and testing for de-energisation) that are intended to prevent inadvertent re-energisation while work is performed. Therefore, the texts are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary.
STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE specifies isolating the equipment at the electrical supply source, locking out and tagging the switch, using personal locks and danger tags at the isolation point, and testing to confirm de-energisation before commencing work. These measures directly address preventing inadvertent re-energisation during the work, aligning with the TARGET requirement to ensure it is not inadvertently re-energised. While the TARGET is framed as a PCBU duty and the SOURCE is framed as worker actions, the described controls are consistent with what a PCBU would implement to meet the duty.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address preventing electrical equipment from being energised/used in an unsafe state by requiring isolation/disconnection and controls to prevent re-energisation/reconnection. The SOURCE describes a lockout/tagout and test-for-dead process intended to prevent inadvertent re-energisation, which is functionally related to the TARGET obligation to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe (or removed).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring isolation at the supply source, lockout/tagging, and verification testing before work. However, it does not explicitly require that once isolated the equipment is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found safe, nor does it address replacement or permanent removal from use. It also frames duties as 'workers are required' rather than the PCBU ensuring the outcome. Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Amend the procedure to explicitly require that any unsafe electrical equipment, once isolated/disconnected, must not be reconnected until it has been repaired or tested and confirmed safe by a competent person, or alternatively replaced/permanently removed from use. Also state the PCBU’s responsibility to ensure these controls are implemented and enforced (e.g., authorization/permit to re-energise, documented test/repair records, and controlled removal/replacement process).

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

The TARGET imposes a duty specifically for electrical work on energised equipment to prevent persons from creating electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. These are different work states (de-energised vs energised) and different primary hazards (re-energisation vs contact with exposed live parts). Therefore the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligation in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not necessary.

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for working on de-energised equipment (isolation at supply source, lockout/tagout, and test-for-dead before work). The TARGET sets duties for electrical work on energised electrical equipment (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, safe work method statement, and safety observer/risk assessment). Because the TARGET obligations apply to energised work and the SOURCE addresses de-energised isolation/LOTO controls, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations defined in the TARGET, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address controls around electrical work and the energisation state of equipment. The SOURCE sets requirements for working on de-energised equipment (isolation, lockout/tagout, test for dead). The TARGET sets a legal obligation that electrical work on energised equipment must not be carried out unless specific exceptions apply. Because the SOURCE is part of a system of work intended to avoid energised work (by isolating/de-energising), it is functionally related to the TARGET’s prohibition/exception framework and warrants a compliance check.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s core requirement: prohibiting energised electrical work unless one of the listed exceptions applies (necessity for safety, necessity for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The SOURCE also does not require documenting/justifying why energised work is necessary, nor does it set conditions/controls for when energised work is permitted. While the SOURCE promotes de-energised work (which aligns with the intent of avoiding energised work), it is incomplete relative to the TARGET obligation because it lacks an explicit ban on energised work and the exception criteria. Therefore it is not compliant with the TARGET as a standalone procedure/policy statement.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure to explicitly state that electrical work on energised equipment must not be performed unless one of the permitted exceptions applies (health and safety necessity, necessary for proper work, testing required under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Add a requirement to assess and document the justification for energised work, and specify that testing on energised equipment is only allowed within those permitted circumstances. Ensure the procedure cross-references the relevant regulation(s) and includes approval/authorization steps for any energised work.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes an obligation on the PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out while equipment is energised. The SOURCE describes controls (isolation, lockout/tagout, testing for de-energisation) intended to ensure work occurs on de-energised equipment, so it is functionally related and suitable for a compliance check.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE focuses on preventing inadvertent re-energisation and verifying de-energisation before work, but it does not explicitly state the overarching requirement that electrical work must not be carried out while equipment is energised, nor does it address the TARGET’s qualifier 'Subject to this Division' (i.e., any permitted exceptions/conditions under the broader regulatory division). As written, it partially supports the obligation but does not fully demonstrate the PCBU-level assurance that energised work is prohibited except as allowed under the Division.
compliance.remediation: Amend the procedure/policy to explicitly require that electrical work must not be performed on energised equipment (except where permitted under the applicable Division and with required controls/authorisations). Include a clear prohibition statement, define any allowed exceptions and approval process, and link the isolation/lockout-tagout/testing steps as mandatory prerequisites for commencing work.

matches.target_title: 211 Emergency stops
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant at a workplace is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the person with management or control of plant at the workplace must ensure that the multiple emergency stop controls are of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the design of plant at a workplace includes an emergency stop control, the person with management or control of the plant at the workplace must ensure that:

(a) the stop control is prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control is coloured red; and   
(c) the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: STEP 1: The SOURCE text describes lockout/tagout and verification of de-energisation for electrical isolation to prevent inadvertent re-energisation during work on de-energised equipment. The TARGET text sets design/management requirements for plant emergency stop controls (multiple E-stops must be stop-and-lock-off; E-stop must be prominent, red, accessible, and fail-safe against circuit malfunction). These address different safety controls: energy isolation for maintenance vs emergency stop control design/operation. The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligations, so a compliance check against the TARGET is not necessary.

source_text: Isolation points must be fitted with control mechanisms that prevent the electrical equipment from being inadvertently re-energised. The control mechanism should require a deliberate action to engage or dis-engage the device. It must withstand conditions that could lead to the isolation failing. Examples of this include switches with a built-in lock, and lock out for switches, circuit breakers, fuses and safety lockout jaws ‘hasps’.

matches.target_title: 210 Operational controls
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that any operator's controls are:

(a) identified on the plant so as to indicate their nature and function and direction of operation; and   
(b) located so as to be readily and conveniently operated by each person using the plant; and   
(c) located or guarded to prevent unintentional activation; and 

(d) able to be locked into the "off" position to enable the disconnection of all motive power.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the need for plant to be operated during maintenance or cleaning cannot be eliminated, the person with management or control of the plant at a workplace must ensure that the operator's controls:

(a) permit operation of the plant while a person is undertaking the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and 

(b) while the plant is being maintained or cleaned, either: 

(i) cannot be operated by any person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; or 

(ii) if subparagraph (i) cannot be complied with because the plant must be operated by a person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant, cannot be operated except by a person authorised by the person with management or control of the plant for that purpose; and 

(c) will allow operation of the plant in such a way that any risk associated with the activities in relation to any person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning: 

(i) is eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable; or (ii) if it is not reasonably practicable to eliminate the risk, is minimised so far as is reasonably practicable.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address preventing unintended energisation/activation of plant/electrical equipment through isolation/controls and lock-off mechanisms. The SOURCE describes requirements for isolation points and control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation, which aligns with the TARGET requirement that operator controls be guarded to prevent unintentional activation and be lockable in the off position to disconnect motive power.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that (i) prevent inadvertent re-energisation, (ii) require deliberate action to engage/disengage, and (iii) include lockable devices/lockout arrangements (e.g., built-in locks, lockout for switches/circuit breakers/fuses/hasps). This satisfies the TARGET’s core obligations relevant here: preventing unintentional activation (TARGET 1(c)) and being able to be locked into the off position to enable disconnection of motive power (TARGET 1(d)). The TARGET also includes additional requirements (identification, location, and specific maintenance-operation authorization controls) that are not addressed by the SOURCE, but those are broader than the SOURCE’s scope and do not contradict it; within the overlapping subject matter (lock-off / prevention of inadvertent activation), the SOURCE is compliant.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address controls around electrical isolation/disconnection points to manage safety during electrical work. The SOURCE specifies design/fitment requirements for isolation points (prevent inadvertent re-energisation via deliberate-action control mechanisms and robustness, e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The TARGET sets preconditions before energised electrical work begins, including requirements about the isolation/disconnection point being clearly marked, unobstructed, and operable quickly, plus risk assessment, authorisation, and consultation. Because both concern the isolation point used in electrical work controls, a compliance comparison is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not cover several mandatory TARGET requirements: (a) conducting and recording a risk assessment by a competent person; (b) ensuring the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (c) ensuring the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of being operated quickly; and (d) authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. While the SOURCE adds a related safety control (preventing inadvertent re-energisation via lockout/deliberate action), it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET’s procedural and labeling/accessibility/operability requirements. Therefore, it is not compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Add procedures and requirements to ensure, before energised electrical work starts: (1) a competent person conducts and records a risk assessment; (2) the work area is cleared of obstructions for safe access/egress; (3) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly; (4) formal authorisation is obtained after consultation with the workplace controller/manager. Retain the SOURCE lockout/deliberate-action controls as an additional safeguard.

matches.target_title: 211 Emergency stops
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant at a workplace is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the person with management or control of plant at the workplace must ensure that the multiple emergency stop controls are of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) If the design of plant at a workplace includes an emergency stop control, the person with management or control of the plant at the workplace must ensure that:

(a) the stop control is prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control is coloured red; and   
(c) the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety controls that prevent unintended re-energisation/restart of plant/electrical equipment. The SOURCE requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energising and require deliberate action (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The TARGET imposes requirements for emergency stop controls, including that multiple emergency stops must be of the “stop and lock-off” type (cannot restart until reset) and that stop controls must not be adversely affected by electrical/electronic malfunction. These are functionally related because both concern preventing unintended restart/energisation and ensuring reliable control mechanisms.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s specific emergency-stop requirements: (1) it does not state that multiple emergency stop controls are “stop and lock-off” such that restart is impossible until the same emergency stop is reset; (2) it does not cover prominence/marking/accessibility or red colouring of emergency stop actuators; and (3) while it mentions withstanding conditions leading to isolation failing, it does not explicitly require that the emergency stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical/electronic circuit malfunction. Therefore, the SOURCE is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Add/verify emergency stop control requirements: ensure any multiple emergency stop controls are “stop and lock-off” and prevent restart until the activated emergency stop is reset; ensure emergency stop controls are prominent, clearly/durably marked, immediately accessible to each operator, and actuators are red; ensure the emergency stop function is fail-safe and not adversely affected by electrical/electronic circuit malfunction. Document these design/installation controls and inspection/testing procedures.

matches.target_title: 191 Emergency stop controls
matches.target_text:
(1) If plant is designed to be operated or attended by more than 1 person and more than 1 emergency stop control is fitted, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides for the multiple emergency stop controls to be of the "stop and lock-off" type so that the plant cannot be restarted after an emergency stop control has been used unless that emergency stop control is reset. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If the design of the plant includes an emergency stop control for the plant, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides: 

(a) for the stop control to be prominent, clearly and durably marked and immediately accessible to each operator of the plant; and   
(b) for any handle, bar or push button associated with the stop control to be coloured red; and   
(c) that the stop control cannot be adversely affected by electrical or electronic circuit malfunction. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety controls intended to prevent unintended energisation/restart of electrical plant. The SOURCE requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation and require deliberate action (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The TARGET requires emergency stop controls—especially multiple E-stops—to be of the stop-and-lock-off type (cannot restart until reset), be prominent/marked/accessible, have red actuators, and be resilient to electrical/electronic malfunction. These are functionally related because both concern preventing unintended restart/energisation via control mechanisms.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET’s core intent (preventing inadvertent re-energisation/restart and requiring deliberate action via lockout). However, it does not address several explicit TARGET requirements: (1) stop-and-lock-off behavior for multiple emergency stops (reset required), (2) prominence/clear durable marking/immediate accessibility, (3) red colour requirement for handles/bars/push buttons, and (4) ensuring the stop control is not adversely affected by electrical/electronic circuit malfunction. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Augment requirements/specification to explicitly cover emergency stop controls: ensure multiple E-stops are stop-and-lock-off (restart only after the actuated E-stop is reset); require E-stops to be prominent, clearly/durably marked, and immediately accessible to each operator; specify red colour for associated actuators (handle/bar/push button); and include design measures so the stop function is fail-safe/not adversely affected by electrical/electronic circuit malfunction.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address electrical safety controls to prevent unintended energisation during electrical work. The SOURCE describes requirements for isolation points and control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energising, which is functionally related to the TARGET obligations to test for energisation, treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earth high-voltage parts after de-energising.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not cover the TARGET’s required pre-work testing by a competent person to determine whether equipment is energised, nor the requirement to treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, nor the requirement to earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. It focuses on lockable/deliberate isolation control mechanisms and robustness of isolation, which may support isolation practices but does not satisfy the specific testing and earthing obligations in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements/procedures that: (1) before electrical work, a competent person tests the equipment to confirm whether it is energised (per applicable testing rules); (2) exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Ensure these steps are integrated with the isolation/lockout controls described in the SOURCE.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a duty to ensure de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised during electrical work. The SOURCE describes specific controls for isolation points (control mechanisms requiring deliberate action, able to withstand failure conditions, e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices) that are intended to prevent inadvertent re-energisation. Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET obligation.
STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE specifies implementing isolation control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation and gives examples (locks/lockout devices/hasps) consistent with ensuring equipment remains de-energised while work is performed. This meets the TARGET requirement at a control-measure level. Note: the TARGET is broader (a duty to "ensure"), but the SOURCE provides appropriate measures aligned with that duty and does not contradict it.

matches.target_title: 190 Operational controls
matches.target_text:
(1) A designer of plant must ensure that the design provides for any operator's controls for the plant to be: 

(a) identified on the plant so as to indicate their nature and function and direction of operation; and   
(b) located so as to be readily and conveniently operated by each person using the plant; and   
(c) located or guarded to prevent unintentional activation; and   
(d) able to be locked into the "off" position to enable the disconnection of all motive power. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If the need for plant to be operated during maintenance or cleaning cannot be eliminated, the designer of the plant must ensure that the design provides for operator's controls that: 

(a) permit operation of the plant while a person is undertaking the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and   
(b) while the plant is being maintained or cleaned, cannot be operated by any person other than the person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning of the plant; and   
(c) will allow operation of the plant in such a way that any risk associated with the activities in relation to any person who is carrying out the maintenance or cleaning: (i) is eliminated so far as is reasonably practicable; or (ii) if it is not reasonably practicable to eliminate the risk, is minimised so far as is reasonably practicable.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for electrical isolation points to prevent inadvertent re-energisation and to require deliberate action (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The TARGET requires plant operator controls to be guarded/located to prevent unintentional activation and to be able to be locked into the "off" position to disconnect motive power. These address the same functional safety objective (prevent unintended energisation/activation and enable lockable isolation), so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE requires (i) control mechanisms preventing inadvertent re-energisation, (ii) deliberate action to engage/disengage, (iii) robustness against conditions leading to isolation failure, and provides examples including built-in locks and lockout for switches/circuit breakers/fuses/hasps. This aligns with TARGET (1)(c) preventing unintentional activation and (1)(d) lockable "off" position for disconnection of motive power. The SOURCE does not explicitly cover TARGET (1)(a) identification/labeling or (1)(b) convenient location, nor the special maintenance-operation controls in TARGET (2); however, those are broader design obligations beyond the SOURCE’s isolation-point focus. For the overlapping requirement area (lockable, deliberate, preventing inadvertent re-energisation), the SOURCE is consistent with and supports compliance.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address control of electrical hazards via isolation/disconnection to prevent unsafe energisation. The SOURCE describes requirements for isolation points/control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation, which is a means of ensuring isolated unsafe equipment is not reconnected. The TARGET imposes a duty to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe (or removed).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially supports the TARGET by addressing prevention of inadvertent re-energisation through deliberate-action control mechanisms (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). However, it does not cover key TARGET requirements: (a) ensuring unsafe equipment is disconnected/isolated (as a duty tied to 'unsafe' status), (b) ensuring it is not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe, or (c) replaced/permanently removed. Because these obligations are not stated/ensured in the SOURCE, it is not fully compliant with the TARGET requirement set.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements/procedures that: (1) unsafe electrical equipment must be identified and disconnected/isolated from supply; (2) once isolated it must not be reconnected until repaired or tested and verified safe; and (3) if not repairable, it must be replaced or permanently removed from use. Include responsibilities, tagging/lockout steps, and verification/testing criteria.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text addresses requirements for isolation points and lockout/control mechanisms to prevent inadvertent re-energisation during isolation (i.e., de-energised/isolated state control). The TARGET text addresses a different obligation: when electrical work is carried out on energised equipment, persons must be prevented from creating electrical risk by inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. While both relate to electrical safety, they govern different scenarios (isolated equipment vs energised work/contact prevention), so the SOURCE is not directly fulfilling the specific obligation in the TARGET and a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 222 Industrial robots
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies to a person with management or control of an industrial robot or other remotely or automatically energised plant at a workplace.

(2) The person must not direct or allow a worker to work in the immediate vicinity of the plant if it could start without warning and cause a hazard, unless suitable control measures are in place to control the risks to health and safety. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(3) If the remote or automatic energising of the plant could lead to risks to health and safety, the person must ensure that access to the area in the immediate vicinity of the plant is controlled at all times: (a) by isolating the area; or (b) by: (i) providing interlocked guards; or (ii) if a risk remains, providing presence-sensing devices; or (iii) if a risk then remains, providing permit to work systems.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
Relevance: Both texts address preventing hazardous unexpected energisation/start-up. The SOURCE requires isolation points to have control mechanisms that prevent inadvertent re-energisation (e.g., lockable switches/lockout devices). The TARGET requires that where remotely/automatically energised plant (e.g., industrial robots) could start without warning and create a hazard, suitable control measures must be in place and access to the immediate vicinity must be controlled (isolation of the area, interlocked guards, presence-sensing devices, permit-to-work systems).

Compliance: The SOURCE provides a specific control measure (lockable isolation/lockout to prevent re-energisation) but does not address the TARGET’s broader requirements: (a) controlling access to the area in the immediate vicinity, (b) use of interlocked guarding and presence-sensing devices in a hierarchy, and (c) permit-to-work systems where residual risk remains. Therefore, the SOURCE alone is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the TARGET regulation.
compliance.remediation: Augment the procedure/requirements to explicitly meet the TARGET controls: (1) prohibit work near plant that can start without warning unless risk controls are implemented; (2) implement continuous access control to the immediate vicinity via area isolation and/or interlocked guards; (3) add presence-sensing devices where guarding does not fully control risk; (4) implement a permit-to-work system for any remaining risk; and (5) integrate lockout/tagout (as described in the SOURCE) as part of the isolation/access-control system.

source_text:
To meet the legislative requirements the following must be considered and addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment prior to carrying out the work:

a. The area where the electrical work is carried out is clear of obstructions so far as to allow for ease of access and exit   
b. The point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its supply is: Clearly marked and labelled, and Cleared of obstructions so as to allow easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and – Capable of being operated quickly c. The person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE is describing what must be considered in an Activity Risk Assessment before carrying out electrical work, which corresponds directly to the TARGET regulation setting mandatory preconditions before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences (risk assessment, clear access/egress, isolation point requirements, and authorisation/consultation). Therefore a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE substantially covers TARGET subregulation (1)(b), (1)(c)(i)-(iii), and (1)(d). However, it does not fully meet TARGET requirements because: (1) TARGET (1)(a) requires that a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work; the SOURCE implies an Activity Risk Assessment exists but does not explicitly require it be conducted prior to commencement for energised work. (2) TARGET (2) requires the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person and recorded; the SOURCE does not mention competency of the assessor or recording/documentation. (3) TARGET (3) provides an exception to (1)(c) for certain supply-side/main switch situations; the SOURCE does not address this conditional applicability (not necessarily a breach if not needed, but it is a gap versus the full regulatory text). Because the SOURCE omits mandatory elements in (2), it is not fully compliant with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure/ARA requirements to explicitly: (a) require a risk assessment be conducted before commencing electrical work on energised equipment; (b) require the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person; (c) require the risk assessment be recorded (documented and retained). Optionally add guidance on the exception in subregulation (3) to clarify when isolation-point requirements do not apply.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes items to be addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment before carrying out electrical work (access/egress, isolation/disconnection point marking/accessibility/operability, and authorisation/consultation). The TARGET sets mandatory requirements for electrical work on energised electrical equipment, including: competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, a safe work method statement (SWMS) with specific content, and (subject to exceptions) a safety observer with specified competencies. Both texts concern pre-work risk controls and procedural requirements for electrical work, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not meet the TARGET requirements. It does not require (a) that energised electrical work be performed by a competent person with suitable, properly tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (b) that the work be carried out in accordance with a SWMS, nor does it specify the SWMS content elements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review); (c) that a safety observer be present with required competence and recent rescue/resuscitation assessment, nor does it address the exception process tied to a risk assessment showing no serious risk. The SOURCE covers some general risk assessment considerations (access/egress, isolation point accessibility/labeling, authorisation) but omits core mandatory controls in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure/Activity Risk Assessment framework to explicitly require, for energised electrical work: (1) performance by a competent person using suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained, and ensuring proper use; (2) preparation and use of a SWMS that includes identification of the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls will be implemented, monitored, and reviewed; (3) provision of a qualified safety observer assessed within the last 12 months as competent in rescue and resuscitation, unless the work is only testing and a documented risk assessment demonstrates no serious risk.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general duty for a PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per Part 3.1). The SOURCE describes specific pre-work controls to be addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment before carrying out electrical work. These are functionally related because the SOURCE appears to be one mechanism to manage electrical risks, which is the obligation described in the TARGET.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE includes some risk controls (clear access/egress, accessible and operable isolation/disconnection point, and authorization after consultation). However, the TARGET requires managing electrical risks broadly in accordance with Part 3.1, covering the full risk management process (identify hazards, assess risks if required, implement and maintain controls, review controls) and applies to electrical risks across design, installation, maintenance, testing, etc. The SOURCE is narrower (focused on access/isolation/authorization prior to work) and does not explicitly address the broader Part 3.1 risk management requirements or ongoing review/maintenance of controls. Therefore, it does not fully demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Expand the Activity Risk Assessment/process to explicitly align with Part 3.1 risk management requirements: identify electrical hazards, assess risks (where required), implement controls using the hierarchy of control (e.g., de-energise/isolate, lockout-tagout, test for dead, PPE, procedures), ensure controls are maintained and reviewed, and document responsibilities/consultation. Ensure coverage extends beyond pre-work access/isolation to all relevant electrical risk activities (design, installation, maintenance, testing) as applicable.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Both texts concern preconditions and controls required before carrying out electrical work. The TARGET imposes specific duties on the PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, to treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and to earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. The SOURCE focuses on access/egress, clear and operable isolation points, and authorisation/consultation, but does not include any requirement for competent-person testing to confirm de-energised status, treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, or earthing high-voltage exposed parts. Therefore, the SOURCE does not satisfy the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Activity Risk Assessment and associated procedures to explicitly require: (1) pre-work testing of the equipment by a competent person to determine if it is energised (in accordance with applicable testing requirements), (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Include roles/responsibilities (PCBU/competent person), documentation of test results, and verification steps before work starts.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern electrical safety risk management prior to performing work. The TARGET imposes duties on a PCBU to maintain safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, and where that is not reasonably practicable, to conduct a risk assessment and implement control measures consistent with that assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements.
