The SOURCE describes items to be addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment before carrying out electrical work (access/egress, isolation/disconnection point labeling/accessibility/quick operation, and authorization/consultation). This is functionally related because the SOURCE appears to be part of a risk assessment/control framework intended to meet electrical safety obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s core requirement: ensuring no person/plant/thing comes within an unsafe distance of overhead or underground electric lines, nor does it specify what to do when safe distance is not reasonably practicable (i.e., conduct a risk assessment specifically in relation to proximity to electric lines and implement controls consistent with that assessment and any electricity supply authority requirements). The SOURCE focuses on isolation points and access/egress for electrical equipment, which may be relevant to electrical work generally but does not demonstrate compliance with safe-distance-to-electric-line obligations or authority coordination requirements. Therefore, it is not compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the Activity Risk Assessment procedure/template to explicitly: (1) identify overhead/underground electric lines in/near the work area; (2) define and verify required safe approach distances; (3) require maintaining those distances so far as reasonably practicable; (4) where not reasonably practicable, require a documented risk assessment specific to line proximity and implement controls (e.g., de-energisation, isolation, barriers/exclusion zones, spotters, permits, signage, equipment limits); and (5) incorporate and document compliance with any electricity supply authority requirements (consultation/approvals/conditions).

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both concern controls around electrical equipment isolation/disconnection and safe management of electrical risks. The TARGET imposes a specific duty on the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/removed. The SOURCE focuses on pre-work activity risk assessment items (clear access/egress, clearly marked and accessible isolation point, and authorization/consultation) but does not address the TARGET’s core requirements: identifying “unsafe electrical equipment,” ensuring it is disconnected/isolated because it is unsafe, preventing reconnection until repaired/tested safe, or replacing/removing it from use. Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Update the Activity Risk Assessment/procedure to explicitly require: (1) identification/criteria for determining unsafe electrical equipment (reasonable grounds); (2) mandatory disconnection/isolation of unsafe equipment; (3) lockout/tagout or equivalent controls to prevent reconnection; (4) conditions for return to service only after repair or testing confirms safety; and (5) replacement or permanent removal where applicable, with records of actions taken.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related because both concern controls/requirements around performing electrical work, including conditions and preparatory measures before carrying out the work. The SOURCE describes items to be addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment prior to carrying out electrical work (access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility/operability, and authorization/consultation). The TARGET sets a specific legal prohibition/exception framework for carrying out electrical work on energised electrical equipment, requiring that energised work is only done under limited necessity-based circumstances.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET because it does not address the core requirement: ensuring energised electrical work is not carried out unless one of the permitted conditions applies (health/safety necessity, necessity for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The SOURCE focuses on access and isolation arrangements and authorization, which are relevant risk controls but do not establish that energised work is avoided unless justified under the TARGET. There is also no explicit requirement in the SOURCE to de-energise where reasonably practicable, nor to document the justification for energised work under the TARGET’s criteria.
compliance.remediation: Update the Activity Risk Assessment and associated procedure to explicitly require that electrical work is performed de-energised by default, and that energised work is prohibited unless one of the TARGET subregulation (1)(a)-(d) conditions is met. Add a mandatory documented justification checklist covering (a)-(d), including confirmation that there is no reasonable alternative, and (where applicable) that testing is required under reg 155. Ensure the authorization step includes verifying and recording which exception applies and why.

matches.target_title: 40 Duty in relation to general workplace facilities

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out controls to be addressed in an Activity Risk Assessment before electrical work, including clear access/egress and unobstructed, clearly marked isolation points. The TARGET imposes broader workplace layout and maintenance duties to ensure safe entry/exit, movement, adequate space, and other environmental conditions. The SOURCE is therefore functionally related as it attempts to implement part of the TARGET’s obligations (safe access/egress and safe work areas).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE only partially addresses the TARGET. It covers access/egress (TARGET (a)) in the context of electrical work areas and isolation points, and touches on obstruction-free access to essential service isolation (partly relevant to TARGET (g)). However, it does not address several required elements in the TARGET: adequate workspace generally (b) beyond access/egress, floor/surface safety (c), lighting (d), ventilation (e), temperature extremes (f), and broader controls for work near essential services (g) beyond isolation point accessibility. Because the TARGET requires ensuring these matters so far as reasonably practicable, and the SOURCE does not include them, the SOURCE is not compliant with the full scope of the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Expand the Activity Risk Assessment (or associated workplace controls) to explicitly assess and control: (1) sufficient workspace for tasks (not just access/egress), (2) floor/surface condition and slip/trip hazards, (3) lighting adequacy for work and emergency evacuation, (4) ventilation adequacy, (5) heat/cold exposure controls, and (6) risks from working near essential services (identify services, exclusion zones/permits, isolation/lockout where applicable), documenting reasonably practicable measures and responsibilities.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets pre-work Activity Risk Assessment requirements for carrying out electrical work (clear access/egress, clearly marked and accessible isolation/disconnection point, and authorization after consultation). The TARGET imposes a different obligation: regular inspection and testing (test-and-tag) of certain electrical equipment used in damaging environments, plus recordkeeping requirements. The SOURCE does not address inspection/testing frequency, competent person testing, tagging, or testing records, so it is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets out pre-work risk assessment considerations for electrical work (clear access/egress, clearly marked and accessible isolation/disconnection point, and authorization/consultation). The TARGET imposes a specific duty during energised electrical work to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components (i.e., control measures like barriers, insulation, exclusion zones, supervision). While both concern electrical safety, the SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s specific obligation (preventing contact with exposed energised components during energised work), so a direct compliance check is not applicable.

source_title: 7.2 Application to undertake energised electrical work
source_text:
To carry out energised electrical work, electrical workers must complete an Application to undertake energised electrical work (CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure Appendix 4).

A completed application must be endorsed by the electrical workers line manager and then submitted to the applicable Group Leader and BU Director for consideration and approval.

The purpose of the application form is to provide senior management with visibility of what if any energised electrical work is being conducted within a business unit and to ensure that the work:

 will be conducted by a competent person,   
 is justified and meets the energised electrical work exemptions under s157 of the WHS Regulations, and   
 will be conducted in a safe manner.

What to consider when completing an application?

When completing the application consider the following:

a. How do you plan on carrying out the electrical work – i.e. is it de-energised or energised? If energised, do you have an appropriate electrical licence? An application to undertake energised electrical work need to be completed to obtain permission to work energised.   
b. Does your training/qualifications/licences/experience address the competencies required for the electrical tasks you intend to carry out? – copies of qualifications/training/licences need to be attached to the verification form.   
c. Have you completed Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs, for the electrical work you intend to carry out – copies of the risk assessment/s need to be attached to the application.   
d. Does the risk assessment address all the considerations documented in Part 3 of the application form?   
e. Do you have a current and approved Verification of electrical competency form? Does it cover the electrical work listed in the application? Do you require an appropriate electrical licence to conduct the work – copy of the approved verification form needs to be attached to the application.   
f. Are there any unexpected events such as equipment failures, that may require you to perform non-routine energised electrical work? – make sure your application covers all routine and potential non-routine work to avoid having to apply for approval at short notice.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes an internal approval/application process for undertaking energised electrical work, including risk assessments, competency verification, and management visibility/approval. The TARGET sets WHS regulatory prerequisites that a PCBU must ensure before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded, site access/egress clear, isolation/disconnection point labelled/accessible/quick to operate, and authorisation after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace). The SOURCE is therefore functionally related as a mechanism intended to help meet these regulatory prerequisites.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns but does not fully demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements. It requires risk assessments/SWIs to be completed and attached, and focuses on competency and senior management approval, which supports TARGET (1)(a) and (2) (risk assessment conducted by a competent person and recorded) and partially supports authorisation. However, the SOURCE does not explicitly require ensuring (before work commences) that: (b) the work area is clear of obstructions for easy access/exit; (c) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and capable of being operated quickly; and (d) authorisation occurs after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace (the SOURCE specifies endorsement by line manager and approval by Group Leader/BU Director, but does not explicitly require consultation with the workplace management/control holder). Because these specific preconditions are not addressed, the SOURCE cannot be considered fully compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the energised electrical work application/procedure to explicitly require and evidence, prior to commencement: (1) confirmation that the work area is clear of obstructions to allow easy access and exit; (2) identification of the isolation/disconnection point and confirmation it is clearly labelled/marked, unobstructed, readily accessible for access/exit, and can be operated quickly; (3) explicit authorisation step that includes documented consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace; and (4) ensure the risk assessment record explicitly states it was conducted by a competent person (or references competency verification) and is retained as the recorded risk assessment.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes an internal approval/application process for undertaking energised electrical work, including ensuring competency, justification under WHS exemptions, and attaching risk assessments/SWIs. The TARGET sets specific WHS regulatory duties for a PCBU when energised electrical work is carried out (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS content requirements, safety observer requirements and exceptions, and ensuring proper use of equipment). The SOURCE is therefore functionally related as a control intended to manage/authorize energised electrical work that must meet the TARGET obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns but does not fully satisfy the TARGET requirements. It addresses: (a) competency (SOURCE requires competent person and verification of competency; TARGET requires competent person), and (b) risk assessment/SWIs (SOURCE requires Activity Risk Assessments and SWIs; TARGET requires a SWMS with specific required content). However, the SOURCE does not explicitly require: (1) that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable, properly tested, and maintained in good working order (TARGET 1(a)(i)-(iii)); (2) that the PCBU ensures, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly (TARGET (2)); (3) that a SWMS is prepared and includes all elements in TARGET (3)(a)-(d) (SOURCE references SWIs and risk assessments but not SWMS nor its mandated content); (4) that a safety observer is present for energised work, including the observer’s competence and 12-month rescue/resuscitation assessment, and the limited exception for testing supported by a specific risk assessment outcome (TARGET (1)(c), (4), (5)). Because these mandatory elements are not clearly required by the SOURCE process, it cannot be considered compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the energised electrical work application/procedure to explicitly require and evidence the TARGET controls: (1) mandate a SWMS for energised electrical work that includes identification of work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review; (2) require confirmation/records that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable, in-date tested, and maintained; (3) include a requirement that supervisors/PCBU ensure proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE (e.g., supervision, checks); (4) require a safety observer for energised work, with documented competence and rescue/resuscitation competency assessment within the last 12 months, and document the testing-only exception supported by a risk assessment showing no serious risk.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE describes an internal approval/application process for undertaking energised electrical work, explicitly referencing that the work must meet energised electrical work exemptions under WHS Regulations s157. The TARGET sets the legal conditions under which energised electrical work may be carried out (i.e., only if one of the necessity/no-alternative/testing conditions applies). Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to the TARGET because it is intended to control/permit energised work in a way that should ensure the TARGET conditions are met.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring justification and stating the work must meet WHS Regulation exemptions, and by requiring risk assessments, competency verification, and management approval. However, it does not explicitly require the applicant/approvers to demonstrate and document that at least one of the specific TARGET conditions is satisfied (health and safety necessity, necessity for proper work, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The SOURCE also frames compliance around “exemptions under s157” without clearly mapping to the TARGET’s specific necessity/no-alternative criteria, creating a gap where energised work could be approved without explicit confirmation that de-energised work is not reasonably practicable or that another TARGET condition applies. Because the required decision criteria are not explicitly embedded as mandatory checks, the SOURCE cannot be confirmed compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the application/approval procedure and form to require explicit selection and written justification of at least one permitted condition for energised work (e.g., (a) health/safety necessity, (b) necessary for proper work, (c) testing required under reg 155, (d) no reasonable alternative), including evidence that de-energising is not reasonably practicable where relevant. Add an approver checklist that must confirm the applicable condition(s) are met before approval, and ensure the risk assessment/SWI specifically addresses why energised work is necessary and what alternatives were considered.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets internal requirements for undertaking energised electrical work (application/approval, competency verification, risk assessments) and explicitly references WHS Regulations s157 exemptions. The TARGET imposes WHS duties on the PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is tested to determine whether it is energised before electrical work, and to treat exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and to earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Both address controls around energised/de-energised status and safe conduct of electrical work, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require (1) pre-work testing by a competent person to determine energisation status, (2) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, or (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. While the SOURCE requires competency, risk assessments, SWIs, and management visibility/approval, these are not a direct substitute for the specific procedural controls mandated in the TARGET. Therefore, based on the text provided, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the energised/de-energised work procedure and the application/approval workflow to explicitly require: (a) testing of equipment by a competent person before electrical work to determine whether it is energised (with reference to the applicable testing method requirements), (b) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised, and (c) for high-voltage work, mandatory earthing of exposed parts after de-energising. Ensure these controls are embedded in SWIs and risk assessments and require evidence/recording (e.g., test results, isolation verification, earthing confirmation) as part of the application package.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out an internal approval and competency/risk-assessment process for undertaking energised electrical work. The TARGET imposes a WHS duty on the PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, persons are prevented from inadvertently contacting exposed energised components (i.e., controlling access/exposure to live parts). Because the SOURCE governs how energised work is authorised and planned, it is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET obligation and warrants a compliance check.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE focuses on obtaining permission, management visibility, justification under WHS exemptions, and ensuring competent persons and risk assessments/SWIs are in place. However, it does not explicitly require or specify controls to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components while the work is being performed (e.g., barriers/insulation, exclusion zones, guarding, lockable covers, spotters, access control, signage, or other measures). While risk assessments and SWIs could include such controls, the SOURCE text does not state this requirement or ensure it is implemented. Therefore, based on the text provided, it does not demonstrably meet the TARGET duty.
compliance.remediation: Amend the application procedure/form and associated SWI/risk assessment requirements to explicitly require controls that prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised work (e.g., physical barriers/guards/insulating covers, exclusion zones and access control, signage, supervision/spotter where needed, and verification that exposed live parts are shielded). Include a mandatory checklist/approval criterion confirming these controls are in place before and during energised work.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets an internal approval/control process for undertaking energised electrical work (application, endorsements, competency and risk assessment checks). The TARGET defines the scope of what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded. These are functionally related because the SOURCE process applies to a subset of activities that fall within the TARGET’s definition of electrical work (and particularly energised electrical work), so a compliance check is relevant to ensure the SOURCE’s controls are triggered for the right activities.

STEP 2 (Compliance): Not assessable as strictly compliant/non-compliant against the TARGET because the TARGET is definitional (what is/is not electrical work) and does not impose the specific approval workflow described in the SOURCE. The SOURCE does not contradict the TARGET, but it also does not explicitly incorporate the TARGET’s inclusions/exclusions to clarify when the application process is required. Therefore a binary compliance determination is not possible from the provided text alone.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure to explicitly align its scope with the TARGET definition by: (1) stating that the procedure applies to “electrical work” as defined in the relevant WHS/ES legislation/regulations; (2) clarifying that excluded activities (e.g., plug-and-socket connection, non-electrical component work with no electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs where safely done by non-experts, etc.) do not require an energised electrical work application unless they involve exposure to energised parts; and (3) adding a decision step/checklist item to determine whether the task is “electrical work” (and whether it is energised) before requiring the application.

matches.target_title: 87 Application for high risk work licence

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE describes an internal CSIRO approval process for undertaking energised electrical work (manager endorsement, BU Director approval, competency verification, risk assessments) to satisfy WHS Regulation s157 exemptions for energised electrical work. The TARGET sets out statutory requirements for applying to a regulator for a high risk work licence (identity details, photo, VET certification within 60 days, declarations about prior licences/convictions/enforceable undertakings, and payment of a fee). These are different functions: the SOURCE is not an application for a high risk work licence and is not attempting to meet the TARGET’s regulator-facing licence application requirements. Therefore a direct compliance check between them is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a primary legal duty that energised electrical work must not be carried out (subject to exceptions in the Division). The SOURCE describes an internal approval/application process specifically for undertaking energised electrical work, intended to ensure it is justified under WHS Regulations exemptions and performed safely. This is functionally related because the SOURCE is a control mechanism intended to manage/permit energised work only when allowed under the regulatory framework referenced by the TARGET.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly state the overriding prohibition that energised work must not occur unless permitted under the applicable Division/exemptions, nor does it clearly require de-energisation as the default and energised work only as an exception. It focuses on obtaining permission to work energised and management visibility, but does not demonstrate that the PCBU 'must ensure' energised work is not carried out except as allowed. Therefore, as written, it is not fully compliant with the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the procedure/application guidance to explicitly state that electrical work must be performed de-energised by default and must not be carried out while energised except where permitted under the relevant WHS regulatory exemptions/conditions. Add a mandatory decision step requiring documented justification that de-energisation is not reasonably practicable (or other applicable exemption), and include controls to ensure energised work cannot proceed without meeting those conditions and formal approval.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general WHS obligation on the PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace (per Part 3.1). The SOURCE describes an internal approval and risk-control process for energised electrical work (competency verification, risk assessments/SWIs, management visibility/approval, and ensuring work is justified and conducted safely). This is functionally related because it is a mechanism to manage electrical risks.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with the TARGET’s requirement to manage electrical risks by requiring: (1) assessment of whether work can be de-energised vs energised, (2) confirmation of licensing/competency, (3) completion and attachment of risk assessments and SWIs, (4) review of risk assessment considerations, and (5) senior management oversight/approval. These elements support risk management for electrical work activities. The TARGET is broad and does not prescribe specific controls beyond managing risks in accordance with Part 3.1; nothing in the SOURCE contradicts that obligation.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text sets an internal approval and competency/risk-assessment process for undertaking energised electrical work (permissioning, management visibility, competency verification, risk assessments, WHS Reg s157 exemptions). The TARGET text imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested (test-and-tag style requirements), including recordkeeping and tagging. These address different compliance domains (energised work authorization vs equipment inspection/testing and records). The SOURCE does not attempt to fulfill the TARGET obligations, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

source_text:
To ensure that energised electrical work is carried out safely and risk minimised to yourself and others, the work must be performed:

a. By a competent person who has the tools, testing equipment and PPE that are suitable for the work, have been properly tested and are maintained in good working order;   
b. In accordance with a Safe Work Instruction prepared for the work; and   
c. Subject to the exception explained below – with a safety observer present who is competent: To implement control measures in an emergency, and – To rescue and resuscitate the worker who is carrying out the work if necessary, and Has been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person.

A safety observer is not required if the work consists only of testing and the risk assessment shows there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE is an instruction describing how energised electrical work must be performed (competent person with suitable/maintained tools/PPE, safe work instruction, safety observer requirements and an exception for testing). The TARGET is a legal duty on a PCBU specifying substantially the same controls (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS requirements, safety observer requirements and testing-only exception). Therefore the SOURCE is functionally related to, and appears intended to implement, the TARGET obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is partially aligned but not fully compliant with the TARGET.
- Competent person + suitable/tested/maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE: SOURCE matches TARGET 1(a).
- Safe work documentation: SOURCE requires a “Safe Work Instruction”, while TARGET requires a “safe work method statement” (SWMS) with specific required contents (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review). SOURCE does not specify SWMS nor the required SWMS content.
- Safety observer: SOURCE generally aligns with TARGET 1(c)/(4) by requiring a competent safety observer able to implement emergency controls and rescue/resuscitate, and assessed within previous 12 months. However, it does not explicitly tie to the competence/qualifications specification as in the regulation (though it largely covers competence).
- Testing-only exception: SOURCE matches TARGET 5, but refers generally to “the risk assessment” rather than explicitly requiring the PCBU to conduct the risk assessment under the referenced regulation.
- Additional TARGET duty: TARGET 2 requires the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly. SOURCE does not include this obligation.
Because the SOURCE omits/does not clearly meet the SWMS requirement (and its mandated content) and omits the duty to ensure proper use of equipment/PPE, it does not fully satisfy the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE to: (1) require a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) (or explicitly state that the “Safe Work Instruction” is a SWMS) and include the mandatory SWMS elements: identification of the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls will be implemented/monitored/reviewed; (2) add the PCBU duty to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; (3) align the testing-only exception wording to require the PCBU-conducted risk assessment (and reference the applicable regulation if needed); (4) clarify safety observer competence/qualifications consistent with the regulation.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address prerequisites/controls for performing electrical work on energised electrical equipment. The SOURCE describes how energised work must be performed (competent person, SWI, safety observer exception), which is functionally related to the TARGET’s regulatory requirements that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment, site access/egress, isolation/disconnection point requirements, and authorisation/consultation). Therefore a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not cover several mandatory TARGET requirements: (1)(a) conducting a risk assessment before work commences (and (2) that it be conducted by a competent person and recorded); (1)(b) ensuring the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c) ensuring the disconnection/isolation point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed for access/exit, and operable quickly (including consideration of the exception in (3)); and (1)(d) authorisation of the work after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace. While the SOURCE includes competency, tools/PPE, SWI, and safety observer requirements, it does not demonstrate or require these specific pre-commencement controls and administrative steps. Hence it is not compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/SWI to explicitly require, before energised work starts: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area is free of obstructions to allow easy access/exit; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is clearly labelled, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly (and include the supply-side/main-switch exception conditions where applicable); and (4) formal authorisation of the energised work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records retained.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety requirements for performing electrical work where energisation is a hazard. The SOURCE sets procedural controls for energised electrical work (competency, SWI, safety observer exception). The TARGET imposes specific pre-work and handling obligations: testing to determine energisation before electrical work, treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Because the SOURCE appears to be an internal instruction intended to manage electrical-work risks, it is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET’s regulatory duties.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require (1) testing of electrical equipment before electrical work to determine whether it is energised, (2) treating each exposed part as energised until isolated and determined not energised, or (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after being de-energised. While the SOURCE mentions competent persons and testing equipment, and allows an exception when work consists only of testing, it does not state the mandatory pre-work test requirement or the exposed-part treatment/earthing rules. Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the Safe Work Instruction/procedure to explicitly require: (1) before electrical work on equipment, testing by a competent person to determine whether the equipment is energised (and reference the applicable testing method requirements); (2) treating all exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage exposed parts, earthing after de-energising. Ensure these requirements are embedded in the SWI and training/verification for competent persons.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address when and how energised electrical work may be performed. The TARGET sets threshold conditions that must be met before energised work is allowed (necessity/no reasonable alternative, including testing). The SOURCE provides procedural safety requirements for carrying out energised work (competent person, SWI, safety observer exception for low-risk testing). Because the SOURCE appears to be an internal instruction intended to govern energised work, it should align with the TARGET’s legal preconditions; therefore a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not include the TARGET’s mandatory precondition that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless one of the listed necessity criteria applies (health/safety necessity, necessary to perform properly, necessary testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). Instead, it assumes energised work will occur and focuses on controls during the work. While the SOURCE’s controls may be compatible with the TARGET, it fails to incorporate the TARGET’s prohibition/decision gate and does not require documenting/justifying that energised work is necessary or that no reasonable alternative exists. Therefore it is not compliant as a complete implementation of the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure/SWI to include an explicit precondition/decision step: energised electrical work must not proceed unless one of the TARGET criteria is satisfied (including testing under the relevant regulation) and there is no reasonable alternative. Require a documented risk assessment and justification referencing the applicable criterion before authorisation, then apply the SOURCE controls (competency, tools/PPE, SWI, safety observer/exception) once the precondition is met.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for performing energised electrical work safely (competent worker, safe work instruction, safety observer/rescue capability, PPE/tools). The TARGET imposes duties on a PCBU to ensure certain plug-in electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested, with exemptions for new/unused equipment, plus recordkeeping requirements. These address different compliance obligations (work method controls vs equipment inspection/testing and records), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation and a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets safety requirements for carrying out energised electrical work (competent person with suitable tools/PPE, safe work instruction, and generally a safety observer with rescue/resuscitation competence, with an exception for low-risk testing). The TARGET imposes duties on the person with management/control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is done by a competent person and per designer/manufacturer recommendations or a maintenance manual, plus an added qualification requirement for detailed inspections involving electrical installations. While both mention competence and testing, they regulate different activities and duty-holders: the SOURCE is about how energised electrical work must be performed; the TARGET is about ensuring maintenance/inspection regimes for amusement devices/ropeways. The SOURCE is not clearly intended to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET, so a direct compliance check is not necessary based on the provided texts alone.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety requirements for carrying out electrical work on energised equipment. The SOURCE sets out procedural controls (competent person, SWI, safety observer) intended to minimise risk during energised work, which is functionally related to the TARGET duty to ensure persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require that all persons are prevented from inadvertently contacting exposed energised components, nor does it specify controls such as barriers, insulation, isolation of the area, exclusion zones, guarding, or access control to prevent contact by any person (including bystanders). While the SOURCE includes general risk-minimisation measures (competency, PPE, safety observer), these do not directly satisfy the TARGET’s specific prevention requirement. Therefore, the SOURCE is not demonstrably compliant with the TARGET obligation as written.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements and controls to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components by any person during energised work (e.g., establish exclusion zones, barricades/physical guarding, covers/insulation, signage, controlled access/spotter responsibilities, and procedures in the SWI to ensure no person can contact exposed live parts). Ensure the SWI and risk assessment document these controls and their implementation.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general duty for a PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace in accordance with Part 3.1. The SOURCE provides specific controls/procedural requirements for managing risks during energised electrical work (competent person, suitable/maintained tools and PPE, safe work instruction, safety observer with rescue/resuscitation competence, and an exception based on testing-only plus risk assessment). These are functionally related because the SOURCE describes measures that would be used to meet the TARGET’s risk-management obligation for electrical risks.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The TARGET is high-level and requires risk management; it does not prescribe particular controls, but expects risks to be managed. The SOURCE aligns with this by requiring competency, appropriate equipment/PPE, documented safe work instructions, use of a safety observer for higher-risk energised work, and a risk-assessment-based exception for testing-only work with no serious risk. These elements are consistent with managing electrical risks and reflect a risk-based approach. While the SOURCE does not explicitly reference Part 3.1 processes (e.g., hazard identification, elimination/minimisation, review), it nonetheless sets concrete controls that support compliance with the general duty described in the TARGET. Therefore, it is compliant with the TARGET as a supporting procedure.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for performing energised electrical work safely (competent person, safe work instruction, safety observer/rescue capability, with an exception for low-risk testing). The TARGET imposes duties on a PCBU to prevent persons/plant/things coming within unsafe distances of overhead/underground electric lines, or otherwise to conduct a risk assessment and implement controls consistent with that assessment and any electricity authority requirements. These address different hazards and contexts (energised work procedures vs proximity to electric lines). The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET’s specific obligation about maintaining safe distances to electric lines or implementing controls for line proximity.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets safety requirements for performing energised electrical work (competent worker, safe work instruction, PPE/tools, and generally a safety observer with rescue/resuscitation competence, with an exception for low-risk testing). The TARGET imposes duties on the person with management/control of plant to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing of plant is performed by a competent person and in accordance with manufacturer/competent-person recommendations (or annually for inspection if not practicable). These address different obligations: the SOURCE is about how to conduct energised electrical work safely, while the TARGET is about ensuring plant maintenance/inspection/testing regimes and frequencies. Although both mention competence and testing, the SOURCE is not clearly intended to fulfill the TARGET’s plant maintenance/inspection/testing obligation, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 299 Safe work method statement required for high risk construction work
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of high risk construction work must, before high risk construction work commences, ensure that a safe work method statement for the proposed work: 

(a) is prepared; or (b) has already been prepared by another person. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) A safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the work that is high risk construction work; and   
(b) specify hazards relating to the high risk construction work and risks to health and safety associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(3) A safe work method statement must: 

(a) be prepared taking into account all relevant matters, including: 

(i) circumstances at the workplace that may affect the way in which the high risk construction work is carried out; and   
(ii) if the high risk construction work is carried out in connection with a construction project—the WHS management plan that has been prepared for the workplace; and 

(b) be set out and expressed in a way that is readily accessible and understandable to persons who use it.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal CSIRO requirements for Safe Work Instructions (SWIs) for high-risk work including energised electrical work, treating SWI as the internal equivalent of a SWMS. The TARGET imposes a legal duty specifically for 'high risk construction work' to have a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS) meeting prescribed content and preparation requirements before the work commences. Because the TARGET is limited to high risk construction work (construction context), and the SOURCE addresses energised electrical work/high-risk work generally (not necessarily construction work), the texts are not functionally aligned in scope. A compliance check against the TARGET cannot be reliably performed without establishing that the energised electrical work in the SOURCE is being carried out as high risk construction work.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets CSIRO’s internal requirement for energised electrical work to be conducted under a Safe Work Instruction (SWI), described as equivalent to the WHS Regulations’ Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS). The TARGET is the WHS regulatory obligation requiring energised electrical work to be carried out in accordance with an SWMS and includes additional requirements (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE; safety observer unless exempt; and specific SWMS content requirements). Therefore, the SOURCE is intended to fulfill (at least part of) the TARGET obligations and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET subregulation (3) by requiring the SWI to describe the work, specify hazards, and include health and safety considerations and emergency steps. However, it does not explicitly require: (i) description of risk control measures and how they are implemented/monitored/reviewed (TARGET 3(c)-(d)); (ii) ensuring the work is carried out by a competent person with suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE, and ensuring proper use (TARGET (1)(a) and (2)); (iii) presence of a qualified safety observer and the 12-month rescue/resuscitation competency assessment, or the testing-only exemption conditions (TARGET (1)(c), (4), (5)). The SOURCE also includes an SWI review requirement, but it is not as specific as the TARGET requirement to describe how controls are implemented, monitored and reviewed within the SWMS itself. As written, the SOURCE does not fully cover the TARGET obligations, so it is not compliant.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI/SWI procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly incorporate all TARGET requirements: (1) require and document that the task is performed by a competent person and that tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, tested, and maintained, and require verification of proper use; (2) require a safety observer meeting the specified competence and current (within 12 months) rescue/resuscitation assessment, and document the limited exemption for testing-only work supported by a risk assessment showing no serious risk; (3) expand SWI content to include risk control measures and explicitly describe how controls will be implemented, monitored, and reviewed (not just periodic document review).

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets WHS regulatory preconditions that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point requirements, and authorisation after consultation). The SOURCE describes CSIRO’s internal mechanism (SWI, equivalent to SWMS) for managing energised electrical work and requires SWIs to include hazards from a risk assessment and approval/authorisation arrangements. This is functionally related because the SWI is intended to help fulfill/operationalise regulatory requirements for energised electrical work.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns but does not fully cover the TARGET requirements. It requires identifying hazards from a risk assessment and documenting authorisation arrangements, but it does not explicitly require: (1) that a risk assessment be conducted before work commences, (2) that the risk assessment be conducted by a competent person and recorded, (3) that the work area be clear of obstructions for access/exit, (4) that the isolation/disconnection point be clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and operable quickly, or (5) that authorisation occur after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace. Therefore, based on the text provided, the SOURCE does not demonstrate full compliance with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI requirements/template (and/or associated procedure) for energised electrical work to explicitly mandate, before work starts: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area allows clear access/egress; (3) verification that the isolation/disconnection point is labelled/marked, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly (and address the stated exception where applicable); and (4) an authorisation step that includes consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace, with records of that authorisation/consultation.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace (per WHS risk management requirements). The SOURCE describes CSIRO’s mechanism (SWI, equivalent to SWMS) for managing risks specifically for energised electrical work, including hazard identification, controls, authorisations, emergency steps, and review. This is functionally related because SWIs are a control/risk-management tool intended to help meet the TARGET duty.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE sets requirements for SWIs for energised electrical work and review triggers, but it does not explicitly state that the PCBU must manage electrical risks across the broader scope referenced in the TARGET (e.g., design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment/installations) nor does it explicitly tie the process to Part 3.1 risk management steps (identify hazards, assess risks where required, implement controls, review effectiveness) for all electrical risks. It is therefore only a partial measure and does not, on its face, fully satisfy the broader obligation to manage electrical risks at the workplace.
compliance.remediation: Expand/align the procedure to explicitly require management of electrical risks across all relevant electrical activities (not only energised electrical work) in accordance with Part 3.1, including explicit steps for hazard identification, risk assessment (where required), implementation of controls, and review of control effectiveness. Ensure SWI/SWMS requirements are integrated as one control within this broader electrical risk management framework.

matches.target_title: 162 Record keeping
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies if a person conducting a business or undertaking prepares: 

(a) a risk assessment under regulation 158; or (b) a safe work method statement under regulation 161. 

(2) Subject to subregulation (3), the person must keep: 

(a) a copy of the risk assessment until at least 28 days after the work to which it relates is completed; and 

(b) a copy of the safe work method statement until the work to which it relates is completed. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(3) If a notifiable incident occurs in connection with the work to which the assessment or statement relates, the person must keep the assessment or statement (as applicable) for at least 2 years after the incident occurs. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.   
(4) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is readily accessible to any worker engaged by the person to carry out electrical work to which the assessment or statement relates. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.   
(5) The person must ensure that, for the period for which the assessment or statement must be kept under this regulation, a copy is available for inspection under the Act. Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for energised electrical work to be conducted under an SWMS-equivalent document (CSIRO SWI), including content and review expectations.
