The TARGET imposes recordkeeping and accessibility obligations for electrical-work risk assessments and SWMSs (retain for specified periods, make readily accessible to workers, and available for inspection). Because the SOURCE is describing the control document used in place of an SWMS for this work, it is functionally related to the TARGET’s obligations around SWMS/risk assessment retention and access, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s requirements to (a) keep copies of the risk assessment for at least 28 days after completion, (b) keep copies of the SWMS/SWI until work is completed, (c) retain for at least 2 years after a notifiable incident, (d) ensure copies are readily accessible to workers during the retention period, and (e) ensure copies are available for inspection. The SOURCE focuses on SWI content and review/revision, but omits retention, accessibility, and inspection provisions. Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI/SWI procedure for energised electrical work to explicitly require: (1) retention of the associated risk assessment for at least 28 days after the work is completed; (2) retention of the SWI/SWMS until the work is completed; (3) retention of the relevant documents for at least 2 years after any notifiable incident connected to the work; (4) ensuring copies are readily accessible to workers performing the electrical work during the required period; and (5) ensuring copies are available for inspection under the Act. Define where documents are stored, who is responsible, and how access/inspection will be provided.

matches.target_title: 300 Compliance with safe work method statement
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of high risk construction work must put in place arrangements for ensuring that high risk construction work is carried out in accordance with the safe work method statement for the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If high risk construction work is not carried out in accordance with the safe work method statement for the work, the person must ensure that the work: 

(a) is stopped immediately or as soon as it is safe to do so; and (b) resumed only in accordance with the statement. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure high risk construction work is carried out in accordance with a Safe Work Method Statement (SWMS), and to stop/resume work if it is not. The SOURCE addresses a closely related control: ensuring energised electrical work (a type of high-risk work) is conducted in accordance with a documented method (SWI as CSIRO’s equivalent to SWMS) and sets content/review requirements. This is functionally related because it is intended to operationalize the requirement to perform high-risk work in accordance with an approved safe method document.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring an SWI for energised electrical work and specifying what it must contain and that it must be reviewed. However, it does not explicitly establish the required 'arrangements for ensuring' the work is carried out in accordance with the SWI/SWMS (e.g., supervision, verification, training/briefing, sign-off, monitoring), and it does not include the TARGET’s mandatory stop-work and resume-only-in-accordance provisions when work deviates from the SWI/SWMS. Therefore, against the specific obligations in the TARGET, the SOURCE is not fully compliant.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI/associated procedure to explicitly: (1) define arrangements to ensure the work is performed in accordance with the SWI/SWMS (e.g., pre-start briefing, competency/authorization checks, supervision, compliance verification/audits, sign-off); and (2) require that if work is not being carried out in accordance with the SWI/SWMS, it must be stopped immediately (or as soon as safe) and only resumed once it is back in accordance with the SWI/SWMS.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a legal obligation that energised electrical work must not be carried out unless specific necessity conditions are met (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, testing, or no reasonable alternative). The SOURCE describes procedural controls (requiring an SWI/SWMS-equivalent, contents, and review) for conducting energised electrical work. This is functionally related because the SOURCE is part of a control framework intended to manage/permit energised electrical work and therefore should align with the TARGET’s preconditions for when such work is allowed.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state that energised electrical work is prohibited unless one of the TARGET’s necessity criteria is satisfied. It focuses on requiring an SWI and what it must contain (authorisation, task description, hazards, safety requirements, emergency steps, review), but it does not require demonstrating that de-energised work is not reasonably practicable, nor does it explicitly limit energised work to the listed circumstances (including testing under the relevant regulation). Therefore, the SOURCE only partially addresses the regulatory intent (procedural documentation) and does not fully implement the TARGET’s threshold conditions for permitting energised work.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SWI/SWI framework for energised electrical work to explicitly require and document, before approval/authorisation, that energised work is only permitted when one of the regulatory necessity conditions applies (health/safety necessity, required for proper work, required testing, or no reasonable alternative). Include a mandatory decision/justification step (e.g., de-energisation assessment and ‘no reasonable alternative’ justification), and ensure the approval process verifies this criterion is met.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets procedural requirements for managing energised electrical work via Safe Work Instructions (SWIs), which is part of controlling electrical risks and aligns with WHS electrical safety obligations. The TARGET imposes specific pre-work and during-work duties on a PCBU regarding determining whether equipment is energised and treating exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise, plus earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Because SWIs are intended to govern how energised electrical work is performed, they should incorporate these mandatory testing/isolation/earthing requirements; therefore a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE lists required SWI content (authorisation, description of work, hazards, safety requirements, emergency steps, review) but does not explicitly require: (1) testing by a competent person before electrical work to determine energisation status; (2) treating each exposed part as energised until isolated and determined not energised; or (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energisation. Since the TARGET mandates these specific controls, and the SOURCE does not state them as required SWI elements, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SWI requirements/templates for electrical work to explicitly include: (1) mandatory pre-work testing by a competent person to confirm whether equipment is energised (and reference the applicable testing method requirements); (2) a rule that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) a requirement to earth high-voltage exposed parts after de-energisation. Ensure these steps are embedded as mandatory procedural controls and verified during SWI review.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets internal requirements for managing energised electrical work via Safe Work Instructions (SWIs), including approvals, hazard identification, controls, emergency steps, and review. The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” (and exclusions) for the purposes of the regulatory Part. Because the SOURCE governs a subset of activities that fall within the TARGET definition (e.g., testing, repairing, maintaining electrical equipment/installation, including energised work), the texts are functionally related and a compliance check is relevant.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not comply with the TARGET as written because the TARGET is a definitional provision (scope/meaning of “electrical work” and what is excluded), while the SOURCE does not incorporate or align to that definition or exclusions. The SOURCE treats “energised electrical work” as high-risk requiring an SWI, but it does not clarify which tasks are considered “electrical work” versus excluded activities (e.g., plug-in connection via cord/plug, non-electrical component work without electrical risk, replacing fuses/light bulbs, manufacturing assembly, certain conduit/duct work under conditions, supervised assistance, or supervised eligibility work). Without mapping the SWI requirement to the TARGET scope, the SOURCE could over-apply or misapply controls to excluded tasks, or fail to clearly capture all included electrical work activities under the defined scope.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE policy/procedure to explicitly adopt or reference the regulatory definition of “electrical work” (including the listed exclusions) and clarify how SWI/SWMS requirements apply across: (1) energised electrical work, (2) de-energised electrical work, and (3) excluded activities. Add guidance/examples to ensure workers correctly classify tasks and apply SWIs only where required (or document any intentional stricter internal standard).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for preparing, reviewing, and using Safe Work Instructions (SWIs) for energised electrical work (a high-risk work control/documentation requirement akin to SWMS). The TARGET sets obligations to regularly inspect and test certain electrical equipment used in damaging environments, and to keep specific testing records (test-and-tag style requirements). These address different compliance domains: work method documentation/authorisation and emergency steps (SOURCE) versus equipment inspection/testing frequency and recordkeeping (TARGET). The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations defined in the TARGET, so a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for when a safety observer must be present during energised electrical work and specifies observer competency/fitness expectations. The TARGET is a legal-style obligation on a PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is performed with a competent worker, suitable/maintained tools/PPE, a safe work method statement (SWMS), and (subject to an exception) a competent safety observer assessed within 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. The SOURCE is clearly intended to operationalize part of the TARGET (especially subregs (1)(c), (4), and (5)), so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is partially aligned but not fully compliant with the TARGET because it omits several mandatory elements required by the TARGET. Specifically: (1) TARGET 1(a) requires ensuring the energised work is carried out by a competent person with suitable, properly tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; SOURCE does not address tools/testing equipment/PPE suitability, testing, or maintenance. (2) TARGET 1(b) and (3) require the work be carried out in accordance with a SWMS with specific required contents; SOURCE does not mention SWMS or its required components. (3) TARGET (2) requires ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; SOURCE does not address this. (4) Safety observer requirements: SOURCE matches the exception concept (testing-only plus risk assessment showing observer not required) and includes annual CPR/rescue competency, and adds additional 'should' controls (one task/one worker, communication, no disabilities). However, SOURCE states the observer 'must be competent to rescue and resuscitate' and 'competent to effectively respond to an electrical emergency' which is consistent with TARGET's competency requirement, but it does not explicitly tie the exception to 'no serious risk' as in TARGET 5(b) (it uses 'hazards and risks eliminated or effectively managed'), which may be less strict/clear than the TARGET threshold.

Because multiple mandatory TARGET obligations (SWMS; tools/PPE requirements; ensuring proper use) are missing from the SOURCE, the SOURCE does not fully fulfill the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure/policy to explicitly include all TARGET requirements: (1) require energised electrical work be performed by a competent person using tools/testing equipment/PPE that are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (2) require a SWMS for energised electrical work and specify the SWMS must include identification of the work, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed; (3) require the PCBU to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers use tools/testing equipment/PPE properly; (4) align the safety observer exemption wording to the TARGET by stating the testing-only exemption applies only where a risk assessment shows there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

matches.target_text:

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:

source_text:
Section 6.3 of the Code of Practice 2018 – Managing electrical risks in the workplace provides additional guidance on requirements and recommendations to comply with the legislation regarding carrying out energised electrical work, as well as:

 Hazards indirectly caused by electricity, such as conductive materials;  Work positioning that minimises the risk of inadvertent contact with exposed energised parts; and  Safety barriers and signs that must be erected when work is carried out.

Note

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE references Section 6.3 of a Code of Practice that provides guidance on managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work, including measures like work positioning and safety barriers/signs. The TARGET imposes a specific duty on a PCBU to ensure that, during energised electrical work, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. Because the SOURCE is guidance intended to support compliance with legal requirements for energised electrical work, it is functionally related to the TARGET obligation and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state the required obligation that the PCBU must ensure all persons are prevented from inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. It only notes that the Code provides additional guidance and lists example topics (indirect hazards, work positioning, barriers/signs). This is not an explicit control requirement or assurance that persons are prevented from inadvertent contact. Therefore, the SOURCE text as written does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET duty.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE content (or the associated procedure it describes) to explicitly require and document that, during energised electrical work, the PCBU ensures all persons are prevented from inadvertently contacting exposed energised components. Include specific controls aligned with the Code guidance (e.g., insulating covers, exclusion zones, barriers/signage, supervision, access control, safe work positioning) and state responsibility/accountability for implementing and verifying these controls.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE discusses guidance for complying with legislation when carrying out energised electrical work and related controls. The TARGET sets specific legal duties before electrical work is carried out: testing to determine if equipment is energised, treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised, and earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Because the SOURCE is positioned as guidance to comply with legislation on energised electrical work, it is functionally related to the TARGET obligations and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s required controls: (1) mandatory testing by a competent person before work, (2) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised, and (3) earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising. Instead, it mentions indirect hazards (conductive materials), work positioning, and safety barriers/signage. These may be complementary controls but do not demonstrate compliance with the specific testing/isolation/earthing duties in the TARGET. Therefore, the SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE (or the associated procedure) to explicitly include the TARGET requirements: (1) require pre-work testing of electrical equipment by a competent person to determine energisation status (with reference to applicable testing method requirements), (2) mandate that all exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and verified de-energised, and (3) require that each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised. Ensure these steps are integrated into the energised work/lockout-tagout and verification process, alongside the existing controls (positioning, barriers, signage).

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE discusses guidance for complying with legislation on carrying out energised electrical work. The TARGET sets the legal conditions under which energised electrical work may be performed. Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to the TARGET and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The TARGET requires that energised electrical work is not carried out unless one of the listed necessity conditions (a)–(d) applies (and notes testing may be included). The SOURCE does not state these conditions or an equivalent prohibition/decision rule; it only references additional guidance topics (indirect hazards, work positioning, barriers/signs). Because the SOURCE does not reflect the core legal requirement (only perform energised work when one of the necessity criteria is met / no reasonable alternative), it is not compliant as a standalone statement against the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE-aligned procedure/policy to explicitly incorporate the TARGET requirement: prohibit energised electrical work unless one of (a)–(d) applies, document the justification for the applicable condition, and note that permitted work may include testing. Then retain the additional controls (indirect hazards, positioning, barriers/signage) as supplementary measures.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE references guidance in a Code of Practice on managing electrical risks (including safety barriers/signs and work positioning to avoid inadvertent contact with energised parts). The TARGET imposes a legal duty to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that persons/plant/things do not come within an unsafe distance of overhead/underground electric lines, and if not practicable, to conduct a risk assessment and implement controls consistent with that assessment and any electricity authority requirements. Both address preventing unsafe proximity/contact with energised electrical hazards and the use of controls, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is descriptive and does not state the required duty to maintain safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, nor does it specify the mandatory fallback requirements when safe distance is not reasonably practicable (risk assessment and controls consistent with the assessment and electricity supply authority requirements). While it mentions barriers/signs and positioning, it does not demonstrate that the TARGET obligations are being met. Therefore, it is not compliant as written.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE-aligned procedure/policy to explicitly: (1) require maintaining safe/unsafe approach distances from overhead and underground electric lines so far as reasonably practicable; (2) require a documented risk assessment when safe distance cannot be ensured; (3) require implementation of control measures consistent with the risk assessment; and (4) require compliance with any applicable electricity supply authority requirements for the line (including consultation/permits/isolations as applicable).

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE cites Section 6.3 of a Code of Practice about managing electrical risks and specifically mentions guidance for carrying out energised electrical work. The TARGET sets mandatory requirements for how energised electrical work must be carried out (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS, safety observer, and proper use). Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to the TARGET and could be used to support compliance with those obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements. It only states that Section 6.3 provides additional guidance and lists example topics (indirect hazards, work positioning, safety barriers/signs). It does not address or evidence the TARGET’s specific mandatory controls: (1)(a) competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (1)(b) a safe work method statement with required contents; (1)(c) presence and qualifications/competence of a safety observer (or the testing-only exception with risk assessment); and (2) ensuring proper use of tools/PPE. Because the SOURCE lacks these required elements, it is not sufficient to claim compliance with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure/documentation for energised electrical work to explicitly meet the TARGET requirements by: (1) requiring a competent person and specifying that tools/testing equipment/PPE must be suitable, tested, and maintained; (2) requiring a SWMS that identifies the work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review; (3) requiring a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation, or documenting the testing-only exception supported by a risk assessment showing no serious risk; and (4) including supervisory/verification steps to ensure workers properly use the tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 154 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—prohibited
matches.target_text:
Subject to this Division, a person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work is not carried out on electrical equipment while the equipment is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because the TARGET sets a primary legal duty: electrical work must not be carried out on energised equipment (subject to exceptions in the Division). The SOURCE discusses guidance for managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work (and related controls like barriers, positioning, and indirect hazards). This does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET prohibition; instead it assumes energised work may occur and focuses on how to do it safely. Without stating that energised work is avoided except where permitted, the SOURCE does not satisfy the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE-aligned procedure/policy to explicitly require that electrical work is not performed while equipment is energised, except where an applicable exception under the relevant Division is met. Where an exception applies, document the conditions/justification for energised work and incorporate the Code of Practice controls (risk assessment, safe work method, barriers/signage, positioning, management of conductive materials, isolation/verification steps, supervision/competency requirements).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The texts are functionally related because both concern safety requirements when performing electrical work, including controls to prevent electrical hazards during work. However, the TARGET imposes a specific duty: ensuring de-energised equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out (i.e., lockout/tagout or equivalent isolation controls). The SOURCE discusses guidance on energised electrical work and mentions indirect hazards, work positioning, and safety barriers/signs, but it does not address preventing inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised equipment. Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the specific obligation in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Add or reference procedures and controls that ensure de-energised equipment cannot be inadvertently re-energised during work (e.g., isolation and lockout/tagout steps, securing isolation points, tagging, verification/testing for dead, control of keys, and re-energisation authorization/process).

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text provides guidance from a Code of Practice about managing risks when carrying out energised electrical work (e.g., indirect electrical hazards, safe work positioning, and use of safety barriers/signage). The TARGET text imposes a specific legal duty to regularly inspect and test certain plug-in electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments, and to keep testing records with specified details. These address different compliance topics (energised electrical work controls vs. inspection/testing and recordkeeping for electrical equipment), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

source_title: 7.7 Energised electrical work – testing and fault-finding

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety requirements around electrical testing and determining whether equipment is energised prior to performing electrical work. The SOURCE provides procedural guidance for testing/fault-finding (including in energised environments) and use of test instruments/PPE, which is functionally related to the TARGET’s mandatory requirement to test equipment before electrical work and to treat exposed parts as energised until proven otherwise.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised (TARGET (1)). It discusses testing/fault-finding and emphasizes safety during testing, but it does not clearly mandate pre-work energisation testing for all electrical work. The SOURCE also does not clearly state that each exposed part must be treated as energised until isolated and determined not energised (TARGET (2)(a)); it mentions identifying exposed conductive parts that could become energised, but not the strict presumption rule. Finally, the SOURCE does not address the specific requirement that each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being de-energised (TARGET (2)(b)). Therefore, the SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure to explicitly require: (1) before any electrical work on electrical equipment, a competent person must test to determine whether the equipment is energised; (2) all exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) for high-voltage exposed parts, they must be earthed after de-energisation. Cross-reference or incorporate the applicable testing method requirements (e.g., per the cited regulations/standards) and ensure competency requirements are explicitly tied to the pre-work energisation test.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE provides guidance and requirements for conducting testing and fault-finding, including energised electrical work, covering tools/test instruments/PPE suitability and maintenance, competency/training, use of safety observers based on risk assessment, and safe conduct of work. The TARGET is a legal duty on a PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is carried out by a competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE, in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS), and (subject to an exception) with a safety observer meeting specified competence/rescue/resuscitation requirements. Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to the TARGET and can be checked for coverage of those obligations.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with parts of the TARGET: it requires suitable, tested and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; requires workers to be appropriately trained and competent; requires PPE selection/maintenance and training; and mentions using a safety observer if required by risk assessment. However, it does not require that energised electrical work be carried out "in accordance with a safe work method statement" nor does it specify the SWMS content requirements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, implementation/monitoring/review). It also does not include the TARGET’s specific safety observer requirements (assessed within previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate) and does not clearly state the default requirement that a safety observer be present for energised work except where the testing-only + risk assessment no serious risk exception applies. Additionally, while the SOURCE says PPE should be used/worn, it does not explicitly impose the PCBU duty to ensure proper use "so far as is reasonably practicable". Due to these gaps against explicit TARGET obligations, the SOURCE is not fully compliant.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE (or associated procedures) to explicitly require: (1) a SWMS for energised electrical work and that work be performed in accordance with it; include SWMS minimum contents (work identification, hazards/risks, control measures, and how controls are implemented/monitored/reviewed). (2) A safety observer for energised electrical work by default, with the specific exception for testing-only where a regulation 158 risk assessment shows no serious risk; and specify safety observer competency requirements including documented assessment within the previous 12 months for rescue and resuscitation. (3) An explicit PCBU obligation to ensure, so far as reasonably practicable, that workers properly use tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related because both concern electrical testing. The TARGET imposes a specific legal duty on a PCBU to ensure certain plug-in electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, and to keep specified records. The SOURCE provides guidance on how to conduct electrical testing/fault-finding safely (especially in energised environments), including competence, instrument standards, and PPE. This could be part of a broader program intended to meet the TARGET’s testing obligation, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET because it does not: (a) specify the scope of equipment covered (plug-in equipment supplied via socket outlets used in damaging environments); (b) require regular inspection and testing intervals/frequency; (c) explicitly require inspection/testing be performed by a competent person for the TARGET scenario (it mentions training/competence generally, but not as a mandated control for the specific duty); (d) include the recordkeeping requirements (retain records until next test/removal; record must include tester name, date, outcome, next test date; tag option); or (e) address the exemption/requirement for new and unused equipment (inspect for obvious damage before use). Therefore, based on the provided SOURCE text alone, it is not compliant with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Add a formal inspection-and-testing program that: (1) identifies in-scope equipment (socket-outlet supplied) and environments likely to damage equipment; (2) mandates regular inspection/testing at defined intervals based on risk/conditions; (3) requires testing by a competent person; (4) requires pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; and (5) implements recordkeeping meeting TARGET fields (tester name, test date, outcome, next due date) and retention rules, including allowing tagging as the record.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address prerequisites and controls for performing electrical work in an energised environment. The SOURCE provides procedural guidance for testing/fault-finding when energised (barriers, authorised access, instrument checks, PPE, safety observer, coordination). The TARGET imposes specific preconditions that a PCBU must ensure before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment, clear access/egress, isolation/disconnection point marked/accessible/quick to operate, and formal authorisation after consultation). Therefore a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET but does not fully satisfy it. It implies risk assessment (e.g., safety observer “if required by the risk assessment”) but does not explicitly require that a risk assessment be conducted before work, nor that it be conducted by a competent person and recorded (TARGET (1)(a), (2)). It includes ensuring only authorised persons enter the immediate area, but does not require PCBU authorisation of the work after consulting with the person with management/control of the workplace (TARGET (1)(d)). It discusses barriers and coordination procedures but does not require the work area to be clear of obstructions for easy access/exit (TARGET (1)(b)). It also does not address marking/labelling and accessibility/quick operability of the isolation/disconnection point (TARGET (1)(c)), nor the exception conditions in (3). Because multiple mandatory preconditions in the TARGET are missing or not explicit, the SOURCE is not compliant as a complete implementation of the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure to explicitly require, before energised testing/fault-finding begins: (1) a documented risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) verification that the work area and access/egress routes are clear of obstructions; (3) identification of the isolation/disconnection point, ensuring it is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly (or document applicability of the supply-side exception); and (4) formal authorisation by the PCBU after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a legal precondition for when energised electrical work (including testing) may be performed (only under specific necessity criteria). The SOURCE provides operational safety guidance for conducting testing/fault-finding in energised environments and explicitly prefers de-energised methods first. Because the SOURCE describes how to perform energised testing/fault-finding, it is functionally related to the TARGET’s restriction on when such energised work is permitted, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state or require that energised electrical work/testing is only carried out when one of the TARGET conditions is met (necessity for health and safety, necessity for proper work, necessity for required testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). While it recommends considering de-energised methods before energised methods, it does not mandate a decision gate or documentation demonstrating that energised work is justified under the TARGET’s criteria. Therefore, the SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET as it lacks the required threshold/justification conditions for permitting energised work.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit requirement that energised testing/fault-finding must not proceed unless one of the permitted conditions in the regulation is satisfied (health/safety necessity, necessary to perform the work properly, required testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative), supported by a documented risk assessment/justification and authorisation before commencing energised work.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to prevent persons from creating electrical risk by inadvertent contact with exposed energised components during energised electrical work. The SOURCE addresses testing/fault-finding in energised environments and includes controls specifically aimed at preventing inadvertent contact (e.g., identifying exposed conductive parts that could become energised, installing temporary/fixed barriers, restricting access to authorised persons). Therefore, the texts are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE describes measures that satisfy the TARGET’s requirement to prevent inadvertent contact with exposed energised components: (1) identification of exposed conductive parts that could become energised, (2) installation of barriers to prevent inadvertent contact, and (3) ensuring only authorised persons enter the immediate area. These controls directly align with the TARGET obligation to prevent persons from creating electrical risk via inadvertent contact. While the SOURCE is broader (tools/PPE/training/coordination), it includes the key prevention mechanisms required by the TARGET. Hence it is compliant.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage health and safety risks associated with electrical risks at the workplace (including testing). The SOURCE provides detailed controls and procedures for managing risks during electrical testing and fault-finding (especially in energised environments), which is a subset of the electrical risks referenced in the TARGET. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with the TARGET’s requirement to manage electrical risks by specifying risk controls and safe systems of work: preference for de-energised methods, pre-work hazard identification and access control, use of barriers, verification of test instruments, use of insulated/rated tools and PPE, use of safety observers based on risk assessment, ongoing hazard review, restoration to a safe condition after testing, and coordination/switching procedures. It also addresses competence/training, equipment standards (AS/NZS 3017, AS61010.1), calibration, and PPE selection/maintenance and prohibition of conductive jewelry—collectively demonstrating a structured approach to risk management consistent with Part 3.1-style risk management expectations. The TARGET is high-level and does not require specific additional elements beyond risk management; the SOURCE meets and operationalizes that duty.

matches.target_title: 165 Testing of residual current devices
matches.target_text:
(1) A person with management or control of a workplace must take all reasonable steps to ensure that residual current devices used at the workplace are tested regularly by a competent person to ensure that the devices are operating effectively. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) The person must keep a record of all testing of a residual current device (other than any testing conducted daily) until the earlier of the following occurs: 

(a) the device is next tested;   
(b) the device is permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern electrical safety testing obligations in a workplace. The TARGET imposes a specific duty to ensure residual current devices (RCDs) are tested regularly by a competent person and that records of testing (other than daily tests) are kept. The SOURCE provides general requirements for electrical testing and fault-finding (competency, safe methods, suitable/calibrated instruments, PPE, procedures), which would apply to conducting RCD testing, but it does not explicitly address the TARGET’s specific RCD testing frequency/regularity requirement or the recordkeeping requirement.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state that RCDs must be tested regularly, does not specify that such testing must be performed by a competent person in the context of RCDs (though it discusses competency generally), and does not require keeping records of RCD testing until the next test or removal from use. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with TARGET clauses (1) and (2).
compliance.remediation: Add explicit workplace procedures requiring: (1) RCDs to be tested at defined regular intervals by a competent person (aligned to applicable regulatory/standard intervals), and (2) retention of records for each non-daily RCD test until the device is next tested or permanently removed from use. Implement a recordkeeping system (logbook/register or digital CMMS) capturing device ID/location, test date, tester competency/identity, results, and corrective actions.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The TARGET imposes an obligation on the PCBU to ensure de-energised electrical equipment is not inadvertently re-energised during work (i.e., lockout/tagout or equivalent controls). The SOURCE discusses testing and fault-finding, including preferring de-energised methods, controlling access, using barriers, suitable instruments/PPE, restoring circuits after testing, and having coordination procedures for switching circuits on/off. However, it does not explicitly require or describe controls to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised equipment while work is being performed (e.g., isolation, lockout/tagout, tagging, securing isolators, verification of isolation, or preventing others from re-energising). Therefore it does not demonstrate compliance with the specific TARGET duty.
compliance.remediation: Add and implement a documented isolation and re-energisation prevention procedure for de-energised work, including: identify all energy sources; isolate and secure (lock) isolators/breakers; apply tags/signage; control keys/access; verify isolation before work; maintain exclusion/control of switchboards; and require authorisation/coordination for any re-energisation (including during testing), with clear responsibilities for the PCBU.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern electrical safety controls around testing/fault-finding and the safe use/condition of electrical equipment. The TARGET imposes a duty to disconnect/isolated unsafe electrical equipment and prevent reconnection until repaired/tested safe (or replaced/removed). The SOURCE discusses testing/fault-finding practices, instrument suitability, PPE, barriers, and restoring circuits, which intersects with identifying and managing unsafe equipment during electrical work—so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated, nor does it clearly prohibit reconnection until the equipment is repaired or tested and found safe, or replaced/removed. While it mentions ensuring test instruments are appropriate/functioning and restoring circuits to a safe condition, it lacks the specific control mandated by the TARGET (mandatory isolation/disconnection of unsafe equipment and lockout against reconnection pending repair/test/replace/remove). Therefore it does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit requirement/procedure that any electrical equipment reasonably believed to be unsafe must be immediately disconnected/isolated from supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and must not be reconnected until it is repaired or tested and verified safe, or else replaced/permanently removed from service. Include criteria for determining 'unsafe', responsibilities, and verification/recordkeeping steps.

source_text:
When purchasing electrical equipment, you must ensure that the equipment meets applicable Australian Standards prior to it being commissioned such as AS/NZS 3820 Essential safety requirements for electrical equipment or equivalent international standards recognised within Australia. Electrical equipment that comply with Australia Standards will have a regulatory compliance mark (a triangle with a circled tick inside) displayed on the equipment.

This is a particular issue for equipment that is purchased internationally, so inspect newly acquired electrical equipment and if there is any doubt engage an electrician to inspect the equipment.

If the equipment is electrically unsafe return it to the supplier or arrange for the equipment to be modified and certified to meet Australian Standards.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety obligations for electrical equipment at workplaces. The SOURCE provides guidance on ensuring purchased equipment meets Australian Standards and is inspected/verified before commissioning/using, which relates to the TARGET’s legal duties around inspection/testing and not using unsafe equipment.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET subregulation (2)(b) by requiring inspection of newly acquired equipment and engaging an electrician if in doubt, and it aligns with the note/reg 149 concept by directing return/modify if unsafe. However, it does not address the core TARGET requirement in subregulation (1) to ensure electrical equipment (when socket-supplied and used in damaging environments) is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person. It also does not require keeping testing records with the specified details until next test/removal (subregulations (3) and (4)). Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add a program requiring regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet equipment used in harsh/damaging environments (moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosives, dust). Define testing intervals consistent with applicable WHS guidance/standards. Implement recordkeeping for each test (tester name, test date, outcome, next test due date) and retain records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed; allow tagging as the record. Ensure procedures explicitly prohibit use of unsafe equipment.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets procurement/commissioning safety expectations for electrical equipment (meeting Australian Standards, checking for compliance marks, inspection and remediation if unsafe). The TARGET only defines what counts as “electrical equipment” (and exclusions) for the purposes of a particular regulatory Part; it does not impose procurement, commissioning, inspection, certification, or marking obligations. Because the TARGET is definitional rather than an obligation the SOURCE is trying to satisfy, a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 163 Duty of person conducting business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking that includes the carrying out of construction work must comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations—Construction and demolition sites). Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1), AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) applies as if any term that is defined in that Standard and that is also defined in the Act or these Regulations has the same meaning as it has in the Act or these Regulations.   
(3) If any requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 (Electrical installations— Construction and demolition sites) deals with the same matter as a requirement under this Part, it is sufficient that the person conducting the business or undertaking complies with the requirement in AS/NZS 3012:2019 as modified by subregulation (2).

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets guidance for purchasing/commissioning electrical equipment to ensure it meets applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820) and has the Australian regulatory compliance mark, with inspection/rectification steps for imported or doubtful equipment. The TARGET imposes a specific legal duty for construction work to comply with AS/NZS 3012:2019 for electrical installations on construction and demolition sites. These address different compliance obligations (general equipment safety/RCM vs construction-site electrical installation standard). The SOURCE is not attempting to implement or demonstrate compliance with AS/NZS 3012:2019, so a direct compliance check against the TARGET is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 15 References to standards
matches.target_text:
(1) In these Regulations, a reference consisting of the words "Australian Standard" or the letters "AS" followed in either case by a number or a number accompanied by a reference to a calendar year is a reference to the standard so numbered published by or on behalf of Standards Australia.

(2) In these Regulations, a reference consisting of the expression "Australian/New Zealand Standard" or "AS/NZS" followed in either case by a number or a number accompanied by a reference to a calendar year is a reference to the standard so numbered published jointly by or on behalf of Standards Australia and the Standards Council of New Zealand.

Notes

1 A person is only required to comply with an Australian Standard or Australian/New Zealand Standard that is applied, adopted or incorporated by, or otherwise referred to in, a regulation to the extent the regulation provides, whether expressly or by necessary implication. For example, in regulation 5, definition boiler, there is an exception in paragraph (e)(iii). The definition does not require a person to comply with AS 2593:2021, but equipment must be certified in compliance with AS 2593:2021 in order to fall within that particular exception. The reference to an Australian Standard in this case is part of a description of equipment excluded from the definition boiler. As a result, the equipment is not covered by particular provisions of these Regulations relating to high risk work.

2 See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE instructs purchasers to ensure electrical equipment meets applicable Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820) and references compliance marking and certification. The TARGET defines what references to “AS” and “AS/NZS” mean within the Regulations and clarifies that compliance with a Standard is only required where a regulation applies/adopts/incorporates or otherwise refers to it. These are functionally related because the SOURCE’s obligations hinge on what counts as an Australian Standard/AS/NZS Standard and when such standards are legally required under regulations.
