STEP 2 (Compliance): A definitive compliance determination cannot be made from the provided texts alone because the TARGET does not itself impose a requirement to comply with AS/NZS 3820 (or any specific standard); it only defines references and limits when standards are mandatory (only when incorporated/referred to by a regulation). The SOURCE asserts a general requirement to meet applicable Australian Standards prior to commissioning, but without identifying the specific regulation(s) that apply to the equipment/context, it is not possible to verify whether the SOURCE’s stated requirement aligns with the regulatory trigger described in the TARGET (i.e., whether the relevant standards are actually applied/adopted/incorporated by regulation in the given scenario).
compliance.remediation: Identify the specific regulation(s) governing the electrical equipment being purchased/commissioned and confirm which AS/AS/NZS standards are applied/adopted/incorporated or otherwise referenced by those regulations. Update the SOURCE guidance to explicitly tie the obligation to those regulatory references (e.g., “where required by applicable regulations/legislation”) and list the relevant standards for the equipment category.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning: Both texts address management of electrical equipment safety in the workplace. The TARGET imposes a specific duty on a PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/permanently removed. The SOURCE focuses on procurement-stage compliance with Australian Standards (inspection of newly acquired equipment, engaging an electrician if in doubt, and returning/modifying/certifying unsafe equipment). While this supports preventing unsafe equipment from being used, it does not explicitly require immediate disconnection/isolation of unsafe equipment already at the workplace, nor does it state the prohibition on reconnection until repaired/tested safe, or the replace/permanently remove requirement. Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully meet the operational control obligations in the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit procedures requiring that any electrical equipment suspected/known to be unsafe is immediately disconnected or isolated from supply, tagged/secured to prevent reconnection, and only returned to service after repair and testing confirms it is safe; otherwise it must be replaced or permanently removed from use. Define responsibilities (PCBU/manager/electrician), verification/testing criteria, and recordkeeping.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text addresses procurement/commissioning controls for electrical equipment (ensuring equipment meets Australian Standards, checking for compliance marks, inspection of imported equipment, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment). The TARGET text imposes preconditions before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment (risk assessment, clear access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility/operability, and authorisation after consultation). These are different compliance domains (equipment conformity vs. controls for energised electrical work), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE addresses procurement/commissioning requirements for electrical equipment to meet Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820), including checking for a regulatory compliance mark and arranging inspection/modification/certification if unsafe. The TARGET imposes a different duty: before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, it must be tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (plus earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising). These are separate compliance domains (equipment conformity vs. safe work/testing for energisation prior to electrical work), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for purchasing/commissioning electrical equipment to ensure it meets Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820), checking for the regulatory compliance mark, inspecting imported equipment, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The TARGET sets duties for how electrical work on energised electrical equipment must be carried out (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, safe work method statement, safety observer, and related risk assessment). These address different compliance domains: equipment procurement/standards compliance vs. controls for performing energised electrical work. The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general duty for a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety from electrical risks at the workplace (Part 3.1). The SOURCE provides specific controls for one aspect of managing electrical risk—procurement/commissioning of electrical equipment (ensuring compliance with Australian Standards, inspection, electrician verification, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment). Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related as a partial method to meet the TARGET obligation.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE addresses only a subset of electrical risk management (mainly purchasing/commissioning and ensuring equipment meets standards). It does not explicitly cover broader electrical risk management across the lifecycle noted in the TARGET (design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, and testing of electrical equipment and installations), nor does it describe a risk management process consistent with Part 3.1 (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment where required, implementation and review of controls). As written, it is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the full TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Expand the procedure/policy to explicitly require managing electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1 across the full lifecycle (design, installation, protection, maintenance, testing) and include a documented risk management process (identify hazards, assess risks as required, implement controls, verify/maintain controls, and review). Include responsibilities, inspection/testing intervals, and controls for electrical installations (not just purchased equipment).

matches.target_title: 164 Use of socket outlets in hostile operating environment
matches.target_text:
(1) This regulation applies in the following circumstances: 

(a) electrical equipment is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust; 

(b) electrical equipment is moved between different locations in circumstances where damage to the equipment or to a flexible electricity supply cord is reasonably likely; 

(c) electrical equipment is frequently moved during its normal use; 

(d) electrical equipment forms part of, or is used in connection with, an amusement device. 

(2) In a circumstance set out in subregulation (1), a person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that any electrical risk associated with the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment through a socket outlet is minimised by the use of an appropriate residual current device. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) Without limiting subregulation (2), the residual current device must have a tripping current that does not exceed 30 milliamps if electricity is supplied to the equipment through a socket outlet not exceeding 20 amps. 

(4) Subregulation (2) does not apply if the supply of electricity to the electrical equipment: 

(a) does not exceed 50 volts alternating current; or 

(b) is direct current; or 

(c) is provided through an isolating transformer that provides at least an equivalent level of protection; or 

(d) is provided from a non-earthed socket outlet supplied by an isolated winding portable generator that provides at least an equivalent level of protection.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for purchasing/commissioning electrical equipment to ensure it meets Australian Standards (e.g., AS/NZS 3820), checking for the regulatory compliance mark, inspecting imported equipment, and returning/modifying unsafe equipment. The TARGET regulation is about managing electrical risk in certain operating environments/usage scenarios by ensuring an appropriate residual current device (RCD) is used (typically ≤30 mA) when equipment is supplied via socket outlets, with specified exceptions. These address different obligations (equipment conformity at procurement/commissioning vs. in-use electrical risk control via RCDs), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific RCD obligation in the TARGET.

source_text:
Modifications made to electrical equipment such as repairing electrical wiring or replacing faulty parts must only be carried out by a competent person.
Upon completion of the work, the equipment must be electrically tested and tagged and certified prior to it being commissioned.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Both texts concern safety controls around electrical work on electrical equipment and the role of a competent person. The TARGET requires that, before electrical work is carried out, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether it is energised, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised (and high-voltage exposed parts earthed after de-energising). The SOURCE instead focuses on who may perform modifications/repairs (competent person) and requires testing/tagging/certification after completion, prior to commissioning. It does not require pre-work testing to confirm de-energised status, nor does it include the exposed-parts treated-as-energised and HV earthing requirements. Therefore it does not meet the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure to require that before any electrical work on equipment begins, a competent person tests the equipment to determine whether it is energised, and mandate that exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and confirmed de-energised; for high-voltage exposed parts, require earthing after de-energising. Keep the post-work test/tag/certification requirement as an additional control.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern electrical safety controls requiring involvement of a competent person and post-work verification/testing. The SOURCE describes requirements for modifications/repairs (competent person; test/tag/certify before commissioning). The TARGET imposes a broader duty on the PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person in specified environments, and to keep testing records with specified details (often via a tag).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring testing and tagging by a competent person after modifications. However, it does not address key TARGET obligations: (1) regular inspection/testing for in-scope equipment used in damaging environments; (2) inspection of new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use; (3) keeping records of testing until next test/removal; and (4) ensuring records include required fields (tester name, date, outcome, next test date). The SOURCE also mentions 'certified' but does not specify the record content/retention required by the TARGET. Therefore, it is not fully compliant with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Add procedures to: (1) identify in-scope plug-in electrical equipment used in harsh/damaging environments and implement a schedule for regular inspection and testing by a competent person; (2) inspect new/unused equipment for obvious damage before first use; (3) maintain testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed; and (4) ensure tags/records include tester name, test date, outcome, and next due test date.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address who may perform electrical work and conditions for doing so. The SOURCE sets requirements for modifications/repairs to electrical equipment (competent person; test/tag/certify before commissioning). The TARGET imposes specific duties on a PCBU for electrical work on energised electrical equipment (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS, and generally a safety observer). Therefore, they are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary where the SOURCE activity involves energised electrical equipment.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET by requiring work be done by a competent person. However, it does not address several mandatory TARGET elements for energised electrical work: (a) ensuring tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, properly tested, and maintained; (b) performing the work in accordance with a safe work method statement meeting specified content; (c) having a qualified safety observer present unless an exception applies; and (d) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, that the worker uses the tools/PPE properly. The SOURCE’s post-work testing/tagging/certification is not a substitute for these energised-work controls. As written, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirements.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure to explicitly cover energised electrical work requirements: require a SWMS that meets the regulation content; mandate a safety observer with required competence/rescue-resuscitation assessment (unless testing-only with documented risk assessment showing no serious risk); require verification that tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, in-date tested, and maintained; and require supervision/controls to ensure proper use of tools/PPE. Clarify applicability to energised vs de-energised work and include PCBU responsibilities.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts concern controls around electrical work and who may perform it. The SOURCE sets requirements for modifications/repairs (competent person; post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification). The TARGET sets mandatory preconditions before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded; site access/egress; isolation point marking/access/quick operation; authorisation after consultation). Therefore, they are functionally related and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET only on the concept of a 'competent person'. However, it does not address the TARGET’s required pre-work controls for energised electrical work: (1)(a) conducting a risk assessment; (2) recording the risk assessment; (1)(b) clearing obstructions for access/exit; (1)(c) marking/clearing/ensuring operability of isolation/disconnection points; and (1)(d) authorisation after consultation with workplace management/control. Because these obligations are not covered in the SOURCE, it is not compliant with the TARGET requirements for energised electrical work commencement.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure to include, before commencing energised electrical work: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) ensuring the work area is clear for safe access/exit; (3) ensuring the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked, unobstructed, and can be operated quickly (or document applicability of any exception); and (4) formal authorisation of the work after consultation with the person with management/control of the workplace.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address safety obligations around maintenance/modification of equipment involving electrical components and require work/testing to be performed by competent/qualified persons. The SOURCE appears to describe a procedure intended to meet obligations similar to those in the TARGET (maintenance/inspection/testing by competent persons, with additional qualification requirements for electrical installations).

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring modifications to be carried out by a competent person and requiring electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. However, it does not cover key TARGET requirements: (1) it does not assign responsibility to the person with management or control to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is carried out; (2) it does not require maintenance/inspection/testing to be in accordance with designer/manufacturer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual; and (3) it does not address the specific requirement that a person conducting a detailed inspection involving an electrical installation must be qualified to inspect electrical installations (or assisted by someone who is). Therefore, the SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the procedure/policy to: (1) explicitly require the person with management or control to ensure maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing is performed; (2) require work to follow designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person; and (3) require that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation is performed by a qualified electrical installation inspector or by a competent person assisted by such a qualified person, with records evidencing qualifications and inspections.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for who may perform modifications/repairs to electrical equipment and requires testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” (including testing, replacing, repairing, altering or maintaining electrical equipment) and lists exclusions. Because the SOURCE activities (repairing wiring, replacing faulty parts, testing) fall within the TARGET definition of electrical work (and are not clearly within the listed exclusions), a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE requires work be done by a “competent person,” but the TARGET framework repeatedly references work being performed/supervised by a “licensed or registered electrical worker” for certain activities and defines electrical work broadly. The SOURCE does not explicitly require a licensed/registered electrical worker (or supervision arrangements) and therefore may not meet the jurisdictional/licensing expectations implied by the TARGET. Additionally, the TARGET is definitional and does not itself impose the SOURCE’s post-work testing/tagging/certification requirement; however, the key compliance gap is that the SOURCE’s competency standard may be weaker/less specific than the licensing/registration standard contemplated in the TARGET context.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE requirement to specify that electrical work (as defined) must be carried out by a licensed/registered electrical worker (or under the direct supervision of one where permitted), rather than only a “competent person,” and align any exceptions with the TARGET exclusions (e.g., fuse/light bulb replacement). Retain/confirm the testing/tagging/certification steps as applicable under the relevant jurisdictional rules.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address controls around electrical equipment safety. The TARGET imposes a duty to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is isolated/disconnected and not reconnected until repaired or tested and found safe (or replaced/removed). The SOURCE describes how modifications/repairs must be performed (by a competent person) and requires testing/tagging/certification before commissioning, which is part of ensuring equipment is safe before being put back into service.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring competent repair and electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning (i.e., before returning to service). However, it does not explicitly require that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated immediately, nor does it explicitly prohibit reconnection until repaired or tested and found safe, nor does it address the alternative controls of replacement or permanent removal. Therefore, it does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit requirements that: (1) any unsafe electrical equipment must be immediately disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply; (2) it must not be reconnected until it has been repaired or tested and found safe; and (3) if not repaired/tested safe, it must be replaced or permanently removed from use. Also define/align criteria for when equipment is considered unsafe (e.g., reasonable grounds).

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for who may modify/repair electrical equipment (competent person) and post-work verification (test, tag, certify before commissioning). The TARGET imposes a different duty: ensuring de-energised equipment is not inadvertently re-energised during electrical work (lockout/tagout or equivalent control). While both concern electrical work safety, the SOURCE does not address preventing inadvertent re-energisation during the work, so it is not functionally fulfilling the TARGET obligation and a direct compliance check is not possible.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address obligations around who may perform work on electrical equipment/plant and the need for testing/inspection. The SOURCE sets requirements for modifications/repairs to electrical equipment (competent person; test/tag/certify before commissioning). The TARGET imposes a duty on the person with management/control of plant to ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is carried out by a competent person and done per manufacturer/competent-person recommendations or annually if not practicable. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET by requiring modifications/repairs be done by a competent person and requiring testing after completion. However, it does not cover key TARGET requirements: (i) it does not assign the duty to the person with management/control of plant; (ii) it does not require maintenance/inspection/testing to follow manufacturer recommendations (or competent-person recommendations where none exist); and (iii) it does not include the fallback annual inspection requirement where compliance with (a) or (b) is not reasonably practicable. Because these elements are missing, the SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE/control procedure to: (1) explicitly require the person with management or control of the plant to ensure maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing are performed; (2) require the work be conducted in accordance with manufacturer recommendations, or if none, recommendations of a competent person; and (3) include an annual inspection requirement where it is not reasonably practicable to follow (1) or (2). Retain the existing competent-person and post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification requirements.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts regulate electrical work on electrical equipment. The SOURCE sets requirements for who may perform modifications/repairs and requires post-work electrical testing/tagging/certification before commissioning. The TARGET imposes restrictions on carrying out electrical work on energised equipment, allowing it only under specific necessity-based exceptions and noting testing may occur while energised under those exceptions. Because the SOURCE describes how electrical work is to be performed and verified, it is functionally related to the TARGET’s constraints on when such work may be performed while energised, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not address the TARGET’s core obligation: ensuring electrical work on energised equipment is not carried out unless one of the listed exceptions applies (health/safety necessity, work requires energisation, testing under reg 155, or no reasonable alternative). The SOURCE also does not require de-energisation/isolation as the default or any decision process/controls for energised work. Therefore, the SOURCE is not compliant with the TARGET as it omits the energised-work prohibition and exception conditions.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure/policy to explicitly prohibit electrical work on energised equipment except where one of the TARGET exceptions applies, and require documenting the justification (which exception applies) and confirming no reasonable alternative. Include controls for energised work (e.g., risk assessment, safe work method, competent/authorised persons, and testing conditions) in addition to the existing requirements for competent persons and post-work testing/tagging/certification.

source_text:
It is important that electrical equipment is made safe before it is disposed of to prevent any risk of injury to workers or public when handling the equipment.

Prior to disposing of electrical equipment, consider the following:

 How can the equipment be made safe and ready for disposal i.e. can the electrical cord be cut/removed to prevent further use?   
 Does the equipment contain chemicals or radiation sources that need to be managed before it is disposed?   
 Can the equipment be disposed of through an e-waste stream?   
 Is there an alternate energy source within the equipment that retains residual energy, i.e. a capacitor?

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address controls for unsafe electrical equipment. The SOURCE provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., cutting/removing cords, managing residual energy), which overlaps with the TARGET’s legal requirement to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/permanently removed from use.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require that unsafe electrical equipment be disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply, nor does it state that once disconnected it must not be reconnected until repaired/tested safe, or replaced/permanently removed from use. It focuses on disposal preparation and hazard considerations (chemicals, radiation, capacitors, e-waste) rather than the mandated isolation and reconnection controls. Therefore it does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure to explicitly require: (1) identification of unsafe electrical equipment; (2) immediate disconnection or isolation from the electricity supply; (3) controls to prevent reconnection (e.g., lockout/tagout, labeling) until the equipment is repaired or tested and confirmed safe; and (4) if not repaired/tested, replacement or permanent removal from use prior to disposal. Include responsibilities, verification steps, and records.
