matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., cutting cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, e-waste stream, residual energy in capacitors). The TARGET text sets obligations that must be met before commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment (risk assessment, clear access/egress, isolation/disconnection point marking and accessibility, authorisation/consultation, and recording the risk assessment). Disposal preparation is a different activity from performing energised electrical work, and the SOURCE is not attempting to satisfy the TARGET’s preconditions for energised electrical work. Therefore, a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., cutting cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, e-waste streams, residual energy in capacitors). The TARGET text imposes duties before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, specifically requiring testing to determine whether equipment is energised and treating exposed parts as energised until isolated, with additional earthing requirements for high-voltage exposed parts. Disposal preparation is a different activity from performing electrical work; the SOURCE is not attempting to satisfy the TARGET’s pre-electrical-work testing/isolation/earthing obligations. While both concern electrical safety, they do not align as the same compliance obligation context.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., removing cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, residual energy, using e-waste streams). The TARGET text imposes obligations to regularly inspect and test certain in-service electrical equipment used in damaging environments, and to keep testing records until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed. These address different lifecycle stages and duties (disposal safety vs. in-use inspection/testing and recordkeeping). The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET’s inspection/testing/recordkeeping obligations, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 144 Meaning of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) In this Part, electrical equipment means any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that: 

(a) is used for controlling, generating, supplying, transforming or transmitting electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(b) is operated by electricity at a voltage greater than extra-low voltage; or   
(c) is part of an electrical installation located in an area in which the atmosphere presents a risk to health and safety from fire or explosion; or   
(d) is, or is part of, an active impressed current cathodic protection system within the meaning of AS 2832.1:2015 (Cathodic protection of metals—Pipes and cables). 

(2) In this Part, electrical equipment does not include any apparatus, appliance, cable, conductor, fitting, insulator, material, meter or wire that is part of a vehicle that is a motor car or motorcycle if: 

(a) the equipment is part of a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle; or   
(b) the electricity source for the equipment is a unit of the vehicle that provides propulsion for the vehicle. 

Note 

See the jurisdictional note in the Appendix.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE provides disposal safety guidance for “electrical equipment.” The TARGET provides a legal/technical definition of “electrical equipment” for the purposes of a specific Part, including inclusions (equipment > extra-low voltage, certain hazardous-area installations, cathodic protection systems) and exclusions (certain vehicle propulsion-related equipment). Because the SOURCE uses the same term and could be intended to apply to the same regulated scope, a compliance check is relevant to ensure the SOURCE guidance aligns with the TARGET-defined scope.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The TARGET text is definitional and does not itself impose disposal obligations or specific disposal steps. The SOURCE sets out safety considerations for disposal but does not claim to implement or reflect the TARGET definition, nor does it specify voltage thresholds, hazardous-area context, cathodic protection systems, or the vehicle-related exclusions. As a result, compliance cannot be determined against a purely definitional provision; there is no direct obligation to satisfy, only a scope definition that the SOURCE may or may not be adopting.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE provides practical guidance for making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., removing cords, managing residual energy, chemicals/radiation, e-waste). The TARGET imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace. Disposal/handling of electrical equipment at a workplace is an activity that can create electrical risks, so the SOURCE is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET’s risk-management obligation.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with the intent of managing electrical risks by identifying several disposal-related hazards and controls. However, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirement to manage risks “in accordance with Part 3.1” (i.e., the formal risk management process). It lacks explicit steps such as hazard identification, risk assessment (as required/appropriate), implementation of controls using the hierarchy of controls, and ongoing review/monitoring. It also does not assign responsibilities, specify procedures/verification (e.g., isolation, test for dead, lockout/tagout), or reference required processes for electrical work/competency where relevant. Therefore, it is not sufficient on its own to evidence compliance with the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Augment the SOURCE into a procedure that explicitly follows Part 3.1 risk management: (1) identify electrical hazards during decommissioning/disposal (including stored energy, damaged insulation, live parts, batteries/capacitors, contaminated/radiological components); (2) assess risks where required; (3) implement controls per hierarchy (e.g., de-energise/isolate, lockout-tagout, test for dead, discharge capacitors, remove batteries, restrict access, competent persons, PPE); (4) document responsibilities, training/competency, and disposal pathways (e-waste, hazardous waste); and (5) review controls after incidents/changes and periodically. Include records/checklists to demonstrate the process.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., cutting cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, e-waste streams, residual energy in capacitors) to prevent injury during handling. The TARGET imposes a duty during electrical work to ensure de-energised equipment is not inadvertently re-energised while work is being carried out. Disposal safety guidance does not address lockout/tagout or controls to prevent re-energisation during electrical work, so the texts are not functionally related for a compliance check.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE provides safety guidance for preparing electrical equipment for disposal (e.g., cutting cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, residual energy, e-waste streams). The TARGET defines what constitutes “electrical work” and lists exclusions for licensing/regulated electrical work activities (installation, repair, testing, etc.). The SOURCE is not attempting to define, authorize, or describe electrical work activities under the TARGET, nor to fulfill an obligation created by the TARGET; it addresses disposal safety rather than regulated electrical work scope. Therefore, a direct compliance check against the TARGET is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., removing cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, e-waste stream, residual energy in capacitors). The TARGET sets legal requirements for carrying out electrical work on energised electrical equipment (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, safe work method statement, safety observer, and related risk assessment). Disposal preparation is not the same functional activity as performing energised electrical work, and the SOURCE is not attempting to satisfy the TARGET’s obligations (SWMS, safety observer, competency/rescue assessment). Therefore a compliance check against the TARGET is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text provides guidance on making electrical equipment safe prior to disposal (e.g., cutting cords, managing chemicals/radiation sources, e-waste streams, residual energy in capacitors). The TARGET text imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure electrical work is not carried out on energised electrical equipment unless specific exceptions apply (necessity for safety, proper work, testing, or no reasonable alternative). Disposal preparation is not the same as performing electrical work on energised equipment, and the SOURCE does not address when/why energised electrical work may be permitted or the required decision criteria. Therefore, the texts are not functionally related for a compliance check.

source_text:
A person in control of electrical equipment at the workplace must ensure that the electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if it is:

 supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
 is used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes it to an environment that are likely to result in damage to that equipment or it reduces its expected life (e.g. conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust.)

Visual inspections of electrical equipment should be carried out each time the equipment is used to help determine whether it is safe. Regular visual inspections can identify any signs of damage, wear and tear or other defects and if present (even if there is doubt), must be isolated or taken out of service until repaired and tested as safe.

Scheduled testing and tagging of electrical equipment can detect electrical faults and deterioration that cannot be detected by visual inspection. The frequency of testing must be conducted in accordance with AS3760 In-service safety inspection and testing of electrical equipment.

New Equipment

Brand-new electrical equipment that has never been put into use does not have to be tested before its first use. However, a ‘new to service’ tag must be attached to the equipment by a person with control or management of that equipment before it goes into service.

It is advisable to conduct a visual inspection of the electrical equipment to ensure that it hasn’t been damaged during transportation, delivery, installation or commissioning.

Once new electrical equipment has been connected for use, it will require testing and tagging at the next scheduled test interval.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out workplace requirements for inspection/testing of electrical equipment, including conditions triggering testing, visual inspections, and handling of new equipment. The TARGET is a legal-style obligation on a PCBU to ensure regular inspection/testing in specified conditions, plus requirements for inspection of new/unused equipment and mandatory recordkeeping. The SOURCE is clearly intended to operationalize the same obligation, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE is partially aligned but not fully compliant with the TARGET. It matches the core trigger for inspection/testing (socket outlet supply + harsh/damaging operating conditions) and references competent persons and AS3760 frequency guidance, which supports subregulation (1). It also addresses new equipment not needing testing before first use and recommends inspection for damage, which aligns in substance with subregulation (2)(a)-(b). However, the SOURCE introduces a requirement for a “new to service” tag before first use; while not prohibited, it does not substitute for the TARGET’s explicit requirements.

Key gaps against the TARGET:
1) Recordkeeping: The TARGET requires keeping a record of any testing until next test or removal/disposal, and specifies mandatory record contents (tester name, test date, outcome, next test date) and allows a tag as the record. The SOURCE discusses testing/tagging generally but does not require keeping records, does not specify retention period, and does not specify the required record fields.
2) Mandatory pre-use inspection for new/unused equipment: The TARGET makes inspection for obvious damage before use mandatory. The SOURCE states it is “advisable” to conduct a visual inspection, which is weaker than a must/shall requirement.
3) Unsafe equipment not to be used: The SOURCE says damaged/defective equipment must be isolated/out of service until repaired and tested safe, which is consistent with the note concept, but it does not explicitly reference the general prohibition on using unsafe equipment; this is not necessarily a noncompliance by itself, but the recordkeeping and mandatory inspection language gaps are sufficient to render it noncompliant overall.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE policy/procedure to explicitly: (1) require keeping a testing record for each item tested, retained until the next test or until the equipment is removed/disposed; (2) require the record to include tester name, test date, outcome, and next test due date, and clarify that a tag may serve as the record; (3) change guidance for new/unused equipment from “advisable” to a mandatory requirement to inspect for obvious damage before first use. Optionally add an explicit statement that unsafe electrical equipment must not be used (cross-referencing the applicable internal rule/regulation).

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for routine inspection, testing and tagging of plug-in electrical equipment used in potentially damaging environments (in-service safety inspection/testing per AS3760), including visual checks and tagging of new equipment. The TARGET sets a different obligation: before electrical work is performed on electrical equipment, it must be tested by a competent person to confirm whether it is energised, and exposed parts must be treated as energised until isolated/de-energised (with additional earthing requirements for high voltage). These address different compliance contexts (in-service equipment safety maintenance vs. pre-work isolation/energisation testing for electrical work), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation.

matches.target_title: 213 Maintenance and inspection of plant
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of plant at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the plant is carried out by a competent person. Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

(2) The maintenance, inspection and testing must be carried out:

(a) in accordance with the manufacturer's recommendations, if any; or   
(b) if there are no manufacturer's recommendations, in accordance with the recommendations of a competent person; or   
(c) in relation to inspection, if it is not reasonably practicable to comply with paragraph (a) or (b), annually.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out requirements for inspection/testing of workplace electrical equipment (a type of plant) by a competent person, including visual inspections, scheduled testing/tagging, and handling of new equipment. The TARGET imposes a general duty on the person with management/control of plant to ensure maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing is carried out by a competent person, and specifies the required basis/frequency (manufacturer recommendations; otherwise competent person recommendations; otherwise annual inspection if not reasonably practicable). Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET obligation and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE aligns with the TARGET in requiring inspection/testing by a competent person and describing inspection/testing practices. However, it does not fully satisfy the TARGET’s specific requirements for how the maintenance/inspection/testing schedule is determined: (a) it does not require compliance with manufacturer recommendations; (b) it does not provide the fallback hierarchy (manufacturer recommendations → competent person recommendations → annual inspection if not reasonably practicable); and (c) it mandates testing frequency per AS3760 without stating this is contingent on manufacturer recommendations or competent person recommendations where applicable. Additionally, the TARGET includes 'maintenance' explicitly, while the SOURCE focuses mainly on inspection/testing/tagging and isolation/repair, without a clear maintenance program requirement. Because these elements are missing or potentially inconsistent with the TARGET’s prescribed basis for frequency, the SOURCE is not fully compliant with the TARGET as written.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure to: (1) explicitly require maintenance, inspection and (if necessary) testing to be performed by a competent person; (2) state that inspection/testing intervals must follow the manufacturer’s recommendations where they exist; (3) where manufacturer recommendations do not exist, require intervals based on a competent person’s recommendations (and document that basis); (4) for inspection, include the annual inspection fallback where it is not reasonably practicable to comply with (2) or (3); and (5) clarify how AS3760 testing/tagging is applied in a way that does not override manufacturer/competent-person recommendations (e.g., use AS3760 as the method/standard and/or minimum baseline where consistent).

matches.target_title: 151 Untested electrical equipment not to be used
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that electrical equipment is not used if the equipment: 

(a) is required to be tested under regulation 150; and (b) has not been tested. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets out when electrical equipment must be inspected/tested (including scheduled testing/tagging per AS3760) and when it must be removed from service, which is functionally related to the TARGET obligation that equipment required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used if it has not been tested.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET requirement because it does not explicitly state the prohibition on use where testing is required but has not been completed. It also states that brand-new equipment does not have to be tested before first use (only a 'new to service' tag), which could conflict with the TARGET if regulation 150 requires testing before use in certain circumstances. While the SOURCE promotes testing/tagging and isolation when defects are found, it does not clearly ensure that equipment required to be tested is not used until tested.
compliance.remediation: Add an explicit rule: any electrical equipment that is required to be tested under regulation 150 must not be used until it has been tested (and is within its test interval). Clarify how 'new equipment' is handled where regulation 150 applies (i.e., confirm whether any categories require testing before first use despite being new), and require controls to prevent use (e.g., lockout/out-of-service tagging) until testing is completed.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for inspection, testing/tagging, and visual checks of portable electrical equipment used via socket outlets in potentially damaging environments (per AS/NZS 3760), including rules for new equipment tagging. The TARGET sets preconditions for commencing electrical work on energised electrical equipment (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility/operability, and authorisation/consultation). These address different duties: ongoing equipment safety maintenance vs controls for performing energised electrical work. The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET’s obligations, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 165 Testing of residual current devices
matches.target_text:
(1) A person with management or control of a workplace must take all reasonable steps to ensure that residual current devices used at the workplace are tested regularly by a competent person to ensure that the devices are operating effectively. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) The person must keep a record of all testing of a residual current device (other than any testing conducted daily) until the earlier of the following occurs: 

(a) the device is next tested;   
(b) the device is permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts address workplace electrical safety inspection/testing obligations performed by a competent person. The SOURCE sets out requirements for inspection, testing and tagging of electrical equipment (including frequency per AS3760) and record-like tagging practices. The TARGET imposes a specific legal duty to regularly test residual current devices (RCDs) and to keep records of RCD testing (other than daily tests) until the next test or removal from use. Because RCDs are a type of electrical safety device used at workplaces and are commonly part of electrical safety testing regimes, the SOURCE could be intended to support compliance with the TARGET, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require regular testing of RCDs (it focuses on electrical equipment supplied via socket outlets and testing/tagging of that equipment). It also does not require keeping records of RCD testing as specified in the TARGET (retaining records until next test or removal). Therefore, the SOURCE does not fully satisfy the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add explicit procedures requiring: (1) regular testing of all workplace RCDs by a competent person to confirm effective operation (including defining the test method and interval consistent with applicable standards/regulations), and (2) retention of records for each non-daily RCD test until the RCD is next tested or permanently removed from use. Ensure responsibilities, record format/location, and auditability are documented.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets requirements for inspection, testing, and tagging of plug-in electrical equipment used in potentially damaging environments (visual inspections, scheduled testing per AS3760, and “new to service” tagging). The TARGET sets requirements for carrying out electrical work on energised electrical equipment (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, SWMS, safety observer unless exempt, and ensuring proper use). These address different activities: routine equipment inspection/testing vs performing energised electrical work. The SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET’s obligations (SWMS, safety observer, energised work controls), so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 149 Unsafe electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace: 

(a) is disconnected (or isolated) from its electricity supply; and (b) once disconnected (or isolated): 

(i) is not reconnected until it is repaired or tested and found to be safe; or (ii) is replaced or permanently removed from use. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of this regulation, electrical equipment or a component of electrical equipment is unsafe if there are reasonable grounds for believing it to be unsafe.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The texts are functionally related. The SOURCE sets out operational controls for identifying unsafe electrical equipment (visual inspections each use; scheduled testing/tagging; isolation/out-of-service when defects are found). The TARGET imposes a legal duty on the PCBU to ensure unsafe electrical equipment is disconnected/isolated and not reconnected until repaired or tested safe, or else replaced/removed.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by requiring defective equipment to be isolated/taken out of service until repaired and tested as safe. However, it does not fully meet the TARGET obligation because it does not explicitly require disconnection/isolating from the electricity supply, does not clearly prohibit reconnection until repaired or tested and found safe (it implies this but not as an explicit control), and does not include the alternative compliance pathway of replacement or permanent removal from use. The SOURCE also focuses on equipment supplied via socket outlets and certain damaging environments, whereas the TARGET duty applies to any unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE procedure to explicitly require that any electrical equipment reasonably believed to be unsafe is disconnected/isolated from the electricity supply immediately; prohibit reconnection until it is repaired or tested and found safe; and include an option/process to replace or permanently remove unsafe equipment from use. Ensure the procedure applies to all unsafe electrical equipment at the workplace (not only socket-outlet supplied equipment in harsh environments).

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a general duty on a PCBU to manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks, including risks arising from maintenance and testing of electrical equipment. The SOURCE sets out specific controls (inspection, testing/tagging, isolation of defective equipment, and handling of new equipment) that are clearly intended to manage electrical risks at the workplace. Therefore, a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE describes a risk-control regime consistent with managing electrical risks: (1) requires regular inspection and testing by a competent person for socket-outlet supplied equipment used in damaging environments; (2) requires visual inspection each use and removal from service if defects are suspected; (3) requires scheduled testing/tagging aligned to AS/NZS 3760; and (4) addresses new equipment controls (new-to-service tag, visual check, then testing at next interval). These measures align with the TARGET’s requirement to manage electrical risks associated with maintenance and testing. The TARGET is high-level and does not prescribe specific measures beyond risk management; the SOURCE provides concrete measures that satisfy that intent. No direct contradiction or omission relative to the TARGET is evident.

matches.target_title: 240 Maintenance, inspection and testing of amusement devices and passenger ropeways
matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): Both texts impose duties on a person with management/control to ensure inspection/testing is performed by a competent person. Although the SOURCE is about workplace electrical equipment (plug-in equipment) and the TARGET is about amusement devices/passenger ropeways, the TARGET explicitly covers detailed inspections that include an electrical installation, making the SOURCE’s electrical inspection/testing concepts functionally related to part of the TARGET obligation.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with TARGET(1) by requiring regular inspection/testing by a competent person and describing visual inspection and scheduled testing/tagging. However, it does not ensure maintenance/inspection/testing is carried out in accordance with the designer/manufacturer recommendations or a competent-person-prepared maintenance manual as required by TARGET(1)(b). Additionally, the SOURCE does not address TARGET(2)’s requirement that a person conducting a detailed inspection that includes an electrical installation must be qualified to inspect electrical installations (or be assisted by someone qualified). The SOURCE’s reference to a “competent person” and AS3760 for in-service testing of electrical equipment does not explicitly satisfy the qualification requirement for inspecting electrical installations within an amusement device/ropeway context.
compliance.remediation: Add requirements that maintenance/inspection/testing of the amusement device/ropeway is performed in accordance with the designer/manufacturer recommendations or an approved maintenance manual prepared by a competent person. Also specify that any detailed inspection involving an electrical installation must be performed by a person qualified to inspect electrical installations (or under assistance of such a qualified person), and document competency/qualification criteria and records accordingly.

source_title: 9.1 Business Unit and Site Electrical Safety Officers
source_text:
The primary purpose of an ESO is to provide advice and support to the business unit regarding CSIRO electrical work requirements and safe electrical work practices.

The ESO is not responsible for managing or approving electrical work and safety activites on site and/or across a business unit.

A senior manager of a business unit/function MUST appoint a Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) at each site where recurring electrical work is carried out by staff members or affiliates within the business unit.

A senior manager of a business unit/function may also appoint a Business Unit Electrical Safety Officer (BU ESO) where recurring electrical work is carried out by staff members and affiliates across multiple sites within the business unit.

Note: A Site ESO does not need to be appointed for work on extra low voltage equipment or installations.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines internal CSIRO governance roles and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs), clarifying that ESOs provide advice/support and are not responsible for managing/approving electrical work. The TARGET text imposes operational WHS-style duties on a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) for energised electrical work (competent person, suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, safe work method statement, safety observer, and related risk assessment conditions). The SOURCE does not attempt to address or implement these specific operational controls for energised electrical work; it addresses role appointment and responsibility boundaries. Therefore, the SOURCE is not functionally aimed at fulfilling the TARGET obligations, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines internal roles/responsibilities and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) within a business unit (advice/support function; not responsible for managing/approving electrical work; when to appoint Site/BU ESOs). The TARGET text imposes operational safety duties on a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) to ensure electrical equipment is tested for energisation before work, that exposed parts are treated as energised until proven otherwise, and that high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energisation. The SOURCE does not describe procedures for testing, isolation, treating exposed parts as energised, or earthing high-voltage parts, nor does it purport to implement the PCBU duties in the TARGET. Therefore, the texts are not functionally related in a way that enables a direct compliance check.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines internal CSIRO governance roles and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs), clarifying that ESOs provide advice/support and are not responsible for managing/approving electrical work. The TARGET text imposes operational legal duties on a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) before commencing energised electrical work (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility, and authorisation after consultation). The SOURCE does not address energised electrical work prerequisites (risk assessment, isolation/labeling, authorisation) and is not written to implement or satisfy the TARGET obligations. Therefore, a direct compliance check between these two specific texts is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE defines internal CSIRO roles and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs), including their purpose and when Site/Business Unit ESOs must/may be appointed. The TARGET provides a definitional clarification about what counts as electrical equipment/installation "under a person's management or control" for a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU). The TARGET does not set an obligation about appointing ESOs or managing electrical work; it only defines scope/meaning of a term. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill an obligation defined in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines the role and appointment requirements of Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) within a business unit (advisory/support function; not responsible for managing/approving electrical work). The TARGET text imposes an operational duty on a PCBU to prevent electrical risk during energised electrical work by preventing inadvertent contact with exposed energised components. The SOURCE does not describe controls, procedures, or responsibilities for preventing contact during energised work, nor does it claim to implement the PCBU duty in the TARGET. Therefore, a direct compliance check between these two specific provisions is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a duty on the PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace. The SOURCE describes an internal role (ESO) intended to support safe electrical work practices and sets appointment requirements for Site/BU ESOs where recurring electrical work occurs. This is functionally related because appointing competent roles and defining responsibilities can be part of how a PCBU manages electrical risks.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not state that the PCBU (or business unit management) must manage electrical risks, nor does it describe a risk management process consistent with Part 3.1 (e.g., identify hazards, assess risks, implement and review controls) for electrical risks across design/installation/maintenance/testing. It also explicitly limits the ESO’s responsibility (advice/support only) without clearly assigning who is responsible for managing/approving electrical work and safety activities, creating a potential governance gap against the TARGET duty to manage risks. Therefore, as written, the SOURCE is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Amend the SOURCE (or associated procedure) to explicitly assign responsibility to the PCBU/senior management for managing electrical risks in accordance with Part 3.1 (hazard identification, risk assessment where required, control implementation, and review). Clarify who has authority/responsibility to manage and approve electrical work and safety activities (e.g., supervisors/permit authorities/electrical managers), and describe how ESO advice integrates into the risk management system. Ensure coverage of electrical risks across design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing, and document the process and accountabilities.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines internal roles and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) and clarifies their advisory (non-approval/management) function. The TARGET text imposes a legal duty on a PCBU to ensure energized electrical work is not performed unless specific necessity conditions are met. The SOURCE does not address energized work prohibitions/conditions, risk controls, authorization, or testing requirements under the cited regulation. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligation in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 146 Meaning of electrical work

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines internal CSIRO governance roles and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs), including their purpose and limits of responsibility, and when Site/Business Unit ESOs must/may be appointed. The TARGET text defines what constitutes “electrical work” and what is excluded from that definition for the purposes of a regulatory/legislative Part. While both concern electrical work, the TARGET does not impose obligations about appointing ESOs or defining ESO responsibilities, and the SOURCE is not attempting to satisfy the TARGET’s definitional requirements. Therefore, there is no direct functional relationship requiring a compliance check between these specific provisions.

matches.target_title: 156 De-energised equipment must not be inadvertently re-energised
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical equipment that has been de-energised to allow electrical work to be carried out on it is not inadvertently re-energised while the work is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines the purpose and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) within a business unit (governance/roles and responsibilities). The TARGET text imposes an operational safety duty on a PCBU to prevent inadvertent re-energisation of de-energised electrical equipment during electrical work (lockout/tagout or equivalent control). The SOURCE does not address controls for preventing re-energisation, nor does it state procedures or responsibilities for ensuring isolation is maintained during work. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligation in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text defines internal CSIRO roles and appointment requirements for Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs), clarifying their advisory function and when Site/Business Unit ESOs must be appointed. The TARGET text imposes operational legal duties on a person conducting a business or undertaking (PCBU) to maintain safe distances from overhead/underground electric lines, or otherwise conduct a risk assessment and implement controls consistent with that assessment and any electricity authority requirements. The SOURCE does not address working near electric lines, safe approach distances, risk assessments for line proximity, or control measures; it only addresses governance/role assignment. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET, and a direct compliance check is not applicable.

source_text:
Individuals appointed to a BU or Site ESO role must have a level of training, qualification, licencing, etc that is commensurate to the competencies required for electrical work that is carried out on the site they primarily work at.

Example - ESO competency

CASS conducts electrical work on 2 different sites Marsfield and Narrabri.

At Marsfield they conduct work on portable electrical equipment and at Narrabri they conduct work on fixed and portable electrical equipment. Based on this information, a BU or Site ESO located at:

 Marsfield will need to have completed an electrical competency assessment.   
 Narrabri will need to hold an appropriate electrical licence.

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c):

The TARGET imposes duties on a PCBU to ensure energised electrical work is carried out by a competent person (with suitable/maintained tools/PPE), in accordance with a SWMS, and (generally) with a competent safety observer with current rescue/resuscitation assessment. Compliance: The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET only on the general concept of competency/qualification (e.g., competency assessment or licence depending on work type). compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE/control framework to explicitly require for energised electrical work: (a) verification of worker competency for the specific energised task (not only ESO role competency), (b) availability and verification that tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, tested, and maintained, (c) preparation and use of a SWMS meeting the TARGET content requirements, (d) provision of a competent safety observer with documented rescue/resuscitation competency assessed within the last 12 months (or documented justification via risk assessment where the exception applies), and (e) supervision/controls to ensure proper use of tools/testing equipment/PPE.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The SOURCE sets requirements for training/qualification/licensing for BU/Site ESOs based on the type of electrical work performed at a site. The TARGET requires that certain electrical equipment be inspected and tested by a competent person and that testing records be kept. These are functionally related because the SOURCE is about ensuring personnel competency for electrical work, which would include (or overlap with) competency to inspect/test electrical equipment where required.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE does not explicitly require (a) regular inspection and testing of in-scope electrical equipment used in damaging environments, (b) that the inspection/testing be performed by a competent person for that specific task, (c) inspection of new/unused equipment for obvious damage before use, or (d) creation and retention of testing records with the specified minimum contents (tester name, date, outcome, next test date) and retention rules. It only addresses ESO competency/credentialing (assessment vs licence) based on fixed vs portable work at different sites. Therefore, it does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Add/implement a documented inspection and testing program for in-scope electrical equipment (socket-supplied equipment used in harsh/damaging environments), including: (1) defined inspection/testing intervals; (2) requirement that testing is performed by a competent person; (3) pre-use inspection for obvious damage for new/unused equipment; (4) mandatory testing records retained until next test or disposal, capturing tester name, test date, outcome, and next due date (e.g., via compliant tags/registry). Ensure ESO competency requirements explicitly cover competence to perform/oversee these inspection/testing and recordkeeping duties.

matches.target_title: 267 When is a person competent to inspect plant
matches.target_text:
A person is a competent person to inspect an item of plant for registration if the person has: 

(a) educational or vocational qualifications in an engineering discipline relevant to the plant to be inspected; or

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets competency/training/licensing requirements for individuals appointed to BU or Site Electrical Safety Officer (ESO) roles, tied to the type of electrical work performed at specific sites. The TARGET defines who is a competent person to inspect plant for registration based on engineering qualifications. These address different roles and regulatory contexts (electrical work/ESO competency vs plant inspection for registration), so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the obligation in the TARGET.

matches.target_text:
(1) The person with management or control of an amusement device or passenger ropeway at a workplace must ensure that the maintenance, inspection and, if necessary, testing of the device or ropeway is carried out:

(a) by a competent person; and (b) in accordance with:

(i) the recommendations of the designer or manufacturer or designer and manufacturer; or

(ii) if a maintenance manual for the device or ropeway has been prepared by a competent person, the requirements of the maintenance manual.

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

(2) A person is not a competent person to carry out a detailed inspection of an amusement device or passenger ropeway that includes an electrical installation unless the person is qualified, or is assisted by a person who is qualified, to inspect electrical installations.

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace.
