WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets a general duty on a PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace. The SOURCE describes a control measure within that risk management framework: ensuring BU/Site ESOs have training/qualifications/licensing commensurate with the electrical work performed at the site. This is functionally related because competent/appropriately licensed personnel are a key means of managing electrical risks.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE focuses narrowly on competency and licensing requirements for individuals appointed to ESO roles, with examples of differing requirements by site/work type. However, the TARGET obligation is broader: managing electrical risks associated with design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance, and testing of electrical equipment/installations in accordance with Part 3.1. The SOURCE does not demonstrate that the PCBU has implemented a comprehensive electrical risk management process (e.g., hazard identification, risk assessment/control, maintenance/testing regimes, installation protection measures) as required by the TARGET. Therefore, the SOURCE alone does not satisfy the TARGET requirement.
compliance.remediation: Expand the procedure to explicitly require compliance with Part 3.1 electrical risk management, including documented processes for identifying electrical hazards, assessing risks, implementing and verifying controls (e.g., isolation/LOTO, RCDs, inspection/testing schedules, maintenance, installation protection), and ensuring only competent/licensed persons perform relevant electrical work. Map ESO competency/licensing requirements into this broader risk management system and retain records demonstrating implementation.

source_text:
A formal letter of appointment to the position of BU ESO or Site ESO is to be issued by the Business Unit/Function Director.

Prior to the appointment taking place, the proposed candidates completed verification of electrical competency should be reviewed to confirm they meet the applicable competency requirements.

On the appointment of a new BU and/or Site ESO, notify your HSE Manager and/or local HSE Advisor to ensure the ESO contact list on MyCSIRO is updated.

matches.target_title: 177 Appointment of competent person to supervise diving work
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must appoint 1 or more competent persons to: 

(a) supervise general diving work carried out in the business or undertaking; and (b) perform other functions under this Division. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE concerns appointment and competency verification for Electrical Safety Officers (BU ESO/Site ESO) and updating an internal contact list. The TARGET imposes a duty on a PCBU to appoint competent persons to supervise general diving work and perform functions under a diving-work regulatory division. These address different roles (electrical safety vs diving supervision) and different regulatory contexts, so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the TARGET obligation.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
Relevance: The SOURCE describes internal controls for appointing Electrical Safety Officers (ESOs) and verifying electrical competency. The TARGET imposes legal duties on a PCBU to ensure energized electrical work is performed by competent persons with suitable/tested/maintained tools and PPE, in accordance with a SWMS, and (generally) with a competent safety observer assessed within the last 12 months for rescue/resuscitation. Both texts concern ensuring electrical work is performed competently and safely, so a compliance check is necessary.

Compliance: The SOURCE partially supports the TARGET by requiring review of candidates’ electrical competency verification before appointment. However, it does not address key TARGET requirements for energized electrical work: (1) ensuring suitable, properly tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (2) requiring and specifying SWMS content; (3) requiring a safety observer and the observer’s competency and 12‑month rescue/resuscitation assessment; (4) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/PPE; (5) the testing-only exception and associated risk assessment. Therefore, the SOURCE does not demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligations.
compliance.remediation: Update the SOURCE procedure (or add linked procedures) to explicitly require that energized electrical work is: (a) performed only by competent persons with suitable, tested, and maintained tools/testing equipment/PPE; (b) performed in accordance with a SWMS meeting the specified content requirements; (c) performed with a competent safety observer who has been assessed within the last 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate, unless a documented risk assessment shows no serious risk for testing-only work; and (d) subject to supervision/verification measures to ensure workers properly use tools/testing equipment/PPE. Maintain records of competency, equipment testing/maintenance, SWMS, observer assessments, and risk assessments.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text describes an internal appointment/administrative process for Electrical Safety Officers (issuing appointment letters, reviewing competency verification for candidates, and updating an internal contact list). The TARGET text imposes operational safety duties on a PCBU to ensure electrical equipment is tested for energisation before electrical work, and that exposed parts are treated as energised until proven otherwise (and high-voltage parts earthed after de-energising). These are different functions: the SOURCE does not address pre-work testing/verification of energisation or treatment/earthing of exposed parts, and the TARGET does not concern appointment letters or contact list updates. Therefore, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligations in the TARGET, so a direct compliance check is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 148 Electrical equipment and electrical installations to which this Division applies
matches.target_text: In this Division, a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation in relation to a person conducting a business or undertaking is a reference to electrical equipment or an electrical installation that is under the person's management or control.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE sets internal process requirements for appointing Electrical Safety Officers (issuance of appointment letter, competency verification review, and updating an internal contact list). The TARGET defines the scope/meaning of “electrical equipment” or “electrical installation” as being those under a PCBU’s management or control. The TARGET does not impose or describe obligations about appointing ESOs, competency verification, or notification processes, so the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill an obligation defined in the TARGET.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text sets internal administrative requirements for appointing Business Unit/Site Electrical Safety Officers (formal appointment letter, review of competency verification prior to appointment, and updating an internal contact list). The TARGET text imposes operational preconditions that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment by a competent person and recorded, work area access/egress, isolation point marking/access/operability, and authorisation after consultation). While both relate broadly to electrical safety, the SOURCE is not describing controls or processes that fulfill the specific pre-work obligations in the TARGET. Therefore, a direct compliance check between these two texts is not necessary.

matches.target_title: 159 Unauthorised access to equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that only persons authorised by the person conducting the business or undertaking enter the immediate area in which electrical work on energised electrical equipment is being carried out. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text describes internal administrative controls for appointing Business Unit/Site Electrical Safety Officers (formal appointment letter, competency verification review, and updating an internal contact list). The TARGET text imposes an operational duty during energised electrical work to restrict access to the immediate work area to authorised persons only. The SOURCE does not address access control, authorisation of entrants, or controls during energised electrical work, so it is not functionally aimed at fulfilling the TARGET obligation.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text describes internal administrative controls for appointing Electrical Safety Officers (issuance of appointment letter, review of competency verification, and updating an internal contact list). The TARGET text imposes an operational duty during energized electrical work to prevent persons from inadvertently contacting exposed energized components. The SOURCE does not address controls, procedures, or safeguards for energized work activities, so it is not attempting to fulfill the specific obligation in the TARGET.

matches.target_title: 150 Inspection and testing of electrical equipment
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure that electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person if the electrical equipment is: 

(a) supplied with electricity through an electrical socket outlet; and   
(b) used in an environment in which the normal use of electrical equipment exposes the equipment to operating conditions that are likely to result in damage to the equipment or a reduction in its expected life span, including conditions that involve exposure to moisture, heat, vibration, mechanical damage, corrosive chemicals or dust. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text describes an internal process for appointing Business Unit/Site Electrical Safety Officers (issuance of appointment letter, review of candidates’ electrical competency verification, and updating an internal contact list). The TARGET text imposes a duty on a PCBU to ensure certain electrical equipment is regularly inspected and tested by a competent person, with specific recordkeeping requirements for testing. The SOURCE does not address inspection/testing of equipment, testing frequency, conditions triggering testing, or the required testing records. While both concern electrical safety, the SOURCE is not attempting to fulfill the specific inspection/testing and recordkeeping obligations in the TARGET, so a direct compliance check is not applicable.

matches.target_title: 408 Duty to ensure health monitoring is supervised by registered medical practitioner with relevant experience
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that the health monitoring of a worker referred to in this Division is carried out by or under the supervision of a registered medical practitioner with experience in health monitoring. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) The person must consult the worker in relation to the selection of the registered medical practitioner. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text concerns internal appointment and competency verification processes for Electrical Safety Officers (BU/Site ESO) and updating internal HSE contact lists. The TARGET text imposes duties on a PCBU regarding worker health monitoring being conducted by (or supervised by) an appropriately experienced registered medical practitioner and consulting the worker on practitioner selection. These address different compliance domains (ESO appointment vs. health monitoring) and the SOURCE does not attempt to implement or satisfy the TARGET obligations.

matches.target_title: 437 Duty to ensure health monitoring is supervised by registered medical practitioner with relevant experience
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that the health monitoring of a worker referred to in regulation 435 is carried out by or under the supervision of a registered medical practitioner with experience in health monitoring. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.   
(2) The person must consult the worker in relation to the selection of the registered medical practitioner. Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: The SOURCE text concerns internal appointment and competency verification processes for Electrical Safety Officers (BU ESO/Site ESO) and updating internal HSE contact lists. The TARGET text imposes duties on a PCBU to ensure worker health monitoring is conducted by/supervised by an appropriately experienced registered medical practitioner and to consult the worker on practitioner selection. These address different compliance domains (role appointment/competency vs. medical health monitoring) and the SOURCE does not attempt to fulfill or reference the TARGET obligations.

source_text:
Business Unit Electrical Safety Officer (BU ESO) Duties

The BU ESO primarily provides:

 advice to business unit senior and line managers regarding the management of electrical work conducted by staff members and affiliates within the business unit; and  support the business unit with meeting its electrical work obligations under the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure and applicable laws.

Persons in this role have the following duties:

 Ensure business unit senior management are educated in their responsibilities under the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure.

 Upon request, 

Assess and advise the business unit on the nature of electrical risks arising from work by the business unit, and the controls necessary to eliminate or minimise those risks; Inform senior management of electrical work matters within the business unit; and Assist with the investigation of electrical related incidents.

 Assist line managers with reviewing Verification of electrical competency forms and subsequently endorse them, if required.

 Together with site ESOs, ensure all energised electrical work undertaken in the business unit is covered by an approved Application to undertake energised electrical work.

 Assist Group Leaders and Directors with reviewing Applications to undertake energised electrical work.

 Advise other business unit ESOs when electrical competency assessments plan to be organised within the business unit.

 If observed, notify and advise relevant managers of possible remedial actions required where electrical licence conditions, the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure or any SWIs are not being effectively implemented.

 Organise regular meetings with Site ESOs within the business unit.

 Maintain competence and training to fulfil this role.

Site Electrical Safety Officer (Site ESO) Duties

The Site ESO primarily provides advice and support to business unit staff members and affiliates on a site regarding CSIRO electrical work requirements and safe work practices.

Persons in this role have the following duties:

 Maintain and manage a list of CSIRO electrical workers for the business unit located on site and the type(s) of electrical work they are verified to carry out.

 Advise electrical workers on site of their responsibilities:

under the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure and Work Health and Safety Regulations 2011 (Cth); and   
to record and maintain their individual competency status, e.g. competency assessments, electrical licences, utilising LMS.

 Assist and advise line managers:

on appropriate electrical qualifications, training and knowledge required by staff members and affiliates to undertake required electrical work; and with reviewing Verification of electrical competency forms for electrical workers on site.

 Endorse Verification of electrical competency forms for electrical workers on site.

 Provide advice on electrical licencing and competency assessment requirements as well as CSIRO preferred electrical competency assessment training providers.

 Assist Group Leaders and Directors with reviewing applications to undertake energised electrical work for activities undertaken on site, if required.

 Ensure all energised electrical work undertaken in the business unit on site is covered by an approved Application to undertake energised electrical work.

 Review risk assessments and Safe Work Instructions for all electrical work conducted within the Business Unit on site.

 If observed, notify the relevant manager where electrical licence conditions, the CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure or any SWIs are not being effectively implemented and provide advice on possible remedial action.

 Upon request, 

assess and advise the business unit on the nature of electrical risks arising from work occurring on site by the business unit, and the controls necessary to eliminate or minimise those risks. assist with the investigation of electrical related incidents that occur on site.

 Maintain competence and training to fulfil this role.

matches.target_title: 161 How the work is to be carried out
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is carried out: 

(a) by a competent person who has tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment that: 

(i) are suitable for the work; and (ii) have been properly tested; and (iii) are maintained in good working order; and 

(b) in accordance with a safe work method statement prepared for the work; and   
(c) subject to subregulation (5), with a safety observer present who has the competence and qualifications specified in subregulation (4). 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that the person who carries out the electrical work uses the tools, testing equipment and personal protective equipment properly. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(3) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(b), the safe work method statement must: 

(a) identify the electrical work; and   
(b) specify hazards associated with that electrical work and risks associated with those hazards; and   
(c) describe the measures to be implemented to control the risks; and   
(d) describe how the risk control measures are to be implemented, monitored and reviewed. 

(4) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(c): 

(a) the safety observer must be competent: 

(b) the safety observer must have been assessed in the previous 12 months as competent to rescue and resuscitate a person. 

(5) A safety observer is not required if: 

(a) the work consists only of testing; and   
(b) the person conducting the business or undertaking has conducted a risk assessment under regulation 158(1)(a) that shows that there is no serious risk associated with the proposed work.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET sets legal requirements for energised electrical work (competent person with suitable/tested/maintained tools/PPE, SWMS content, and safety observer requirements). The SOURCE defines BU ESO and Site ESO duties that include ensuring all energised electrical work is covered by an approved application, reviewing risk assessments and SWIs, and advising on competency/licensing. These roles are part of the organisation’s governance intended to help meet electrical safety obligations, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially supports the TARGET but does not fully demonstrate coverage of the TARGET’s specific mandatory elements. It addresses (i) competency governance (verification/endorsement of competency forms; advice on licensing/competency), and (ii) procedural controls via “approved Application to undertake energised electrical work” and review of risk assessments/SWIs. However, the TARGET specifically requires: (a) ensuring the competent person has suitable tools/testing equipment/PPE that are properly tested and maintained; (b) work is carried out in accordance with a safe work method statement (SWMS) with specified minimum content; (c) a safety observer with specified competence and rescue/resuscitation assessment within the previous 12 months (unless the testing-only/risk assessment exception applies); and (d) ensuring, so far as reasonably practicable, proper use of tools/PPE. The SOURCE does not explicitly require SWMS (as distinct from SWIs), does not specify SWMS content requirements, does not mention safety observer requirements or the 12-month rescue/resuscitation competency assessment, and does not address ensuring tools/testing equipment/PPE are suitable, tested, maintained, and properly used. Therefore, based on the text provided, the SOURCE is not sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update BU ESO/Site ESO duties and/or the energised work application process to explicitly require: (1) a SWMS for energised electrical work meeting the regulation’s content requirements (identify work, hazards/risks, controls, and implementation/monitoring/review); (2) verification that tools, testing equipment and PPE are suitable, properly tested, maintained, and used properly (include checks/records); (3) a safety observer for energised work unless the testing-only exception applies, including documented competence and rescue/resuscitation assessment within the last 12 months; and (4) clear assignment of responsibility for confirming these items before approving/authorising energised work.

matches.target_title: 158 Preliminary steps
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure the following before electrical work on energised electrical equipment commences: 

(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed electrical work;   
(b) the area where the electrical work is to be carried out is clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit;   
(c) the point at which the electrical equipment can be disconnected or isolated from its electricity supply is: (i) clearly marked or labelled; and (ii) clear of obstructions so as to allow for easy access and exit by the worker who is to carry out the electrical work or any other competent person; and (iii) capable of being operated quickly;   
(d) the person authorises the electrical work after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) For the purposes of subregulation (1)(a), the risk assessment must be: 

(a) conducted by a competent person; and (b) recorded. 

(3) Subregulation (1)(c) does not apply to electrical work on electrical equipment if: 

(a) the work is to be carried out on the supply side of the main switch on the main switchboard for the equipment; and   
(b) the point at which the equipment can be disconnected from its electricity supply is not reasonably accessible from the work location.

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes WHS regulatory requirements that must be ensured before energised electrical work commences (risk assessment, access/egress, isolation point marking/accessibility/operability, and authorisation after consultation). The SOURCE defines BU ESO and Site ESO duties that include ensuring energised electrical work is covered by an approved application, assisting with reviews of applications, and reviewing risk assessments and SWIs (Site ESO). These duties are part of an organisational control framework intended to help meet electrical safety legal obligations, so a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns but does not fully demonstrate the TARGET requirements are ensured. It references risk assessments (Site ESO: “Review risk assessments and Safe Work Instructions”) and an approval/application process for energised work (“covered by an approved Application to undertake energised electrical work”), which may correspond to authorisation. However, the SOURCE does not explicitly require that: (a) a risk assessment is conducted (not just reviewed) before energised work commences; (b) the risk assessment is conducted by a competent person and recorded; (c) the work area is clear of obstructions for access/exit; (d) the isolation/disconnection point is clearly marked/labelled, unobstructed, and capable of being operated quickly; (e) authorisation occurs after consulting with the person with management or control of the workplace. Because these specific preconditions are not stated as duties/controls to be ensured, the SOURCE cannot be confirmed as compliant with the TARGET.
compliance.remediation: Update the BU ESO/Site ESO duties and/or the energised work application procedure to explicitly require and verify, before energised work starts: (1) a recorded risk assessment conducted by a competent person; (2) confirmation the work area is free of obstructions for safe access/egress; (3) confirmation the isolation/disconnection point is labelled/marked, unobstructed, quickly operable, and accessible (or document the exception conditions where applicable); and (4) documented authorisation after consultation with the person with management or control of the workplace. Add checklist fields/evidence requirements in the ‘Application to undertake energised electrical work’ and assign responsibility for verifying each item.

matches.target_title: 155 Duty to determine whether equipment is energised
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, before electrical work is carried out on electrical equipment, the equipment is tested by a competent person to determine whether or not it is energised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Note 

Regulation 157 allows electrical testing to be carried out on electrical equipment for the purposes of this regulation. Regulation 161 sets out how the testing is to be carried out. 

(2) The person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that: 

(a) each exposed part is treated as energised until it is isolated and determined not to be energised; and   
(b) each high-voltage exposed part is earthed after being deenergised. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning:
Relevance: The TARGET imposes specific WHS electrical safety obligations on the PCBU (ensuring equipment is tested for energisation before electrical work; treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; earthing high-voltage exposed parts after de-energising). The SOURCE describes BU ESO and Site ESO duties intended to support compliance with electrical safety procedures and applicable laws, including reviewing SWIs/risk assessments and ensuring energised electrical work is covered by an approved application. This is functionally related because these roles are part of the governance framework that would be expected to help the business unit meet WHS electrical safety regulatory duties.

Compliance: The SOURCE does not explicitly require (1) pre-work testing by a competent person to determine energisation status, (2) treating exposed parts as energised until isolated and proven not energised, or (3) earthing each high-voltage exposed part after de-energising. While it references risk assessments, SWIs, and controls for electrical risks, it does not state these specific mandatory controls or assign responsibility to ensure they occur. Therefore, based on the text provided, the SOURCE does not demonstrate coverage of the TARGET requirements and cannot be considered compliant.
compliance.remediation: Update the BU ESO/Site ESO duties and/or referenced CSIRO Electrical Safety Procedure/SWIs to explicitly require and verify that: (1) before electrical work on equipment, a competent person tests to confirm whether it is energised; (2) exposed parts are treated as energised until isolated and proven de-energised; and (3) high-voltage exposed parts are earthed after de-energising. Add clear role accountabilities (e.g., Site ESO to verify SWIs/risk assessments include these steps; line managers/electrical workers to perform and record testing/earthing; audit/spot-check mechanism and recordkeeping requirements).

matches.target_title: 147 Risk management
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must manage risks to health and safety associated with electrical risks at the workplace, in accordance with Part 3.1. 

Example 

Electrical risks associated with the design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment and electrical installations at a workplace. 

Note 

WHS Act—section 19 (see regulation 9).

compliance.reasoning:
STEP 1 (Relevance): The TARGET imposes a legal duty on the PCBU to manage electrical health and safety risks at the workplace (per WHS framework/Part 3.1). The SOURCE describes internal BU ESO and Site ESO duties that are intended to support management of electrical work risks (risk assessment advice, SWIs, incident investigation support, competency verification, and controls for energised electrical work). Therefore, the SOURCE is functionally related to fulfilling the TARGET obligation and a compliance check is necessary.

STEP 2 (Compliance): The SOURCE partially aligns with the TARGET by establishing roles and processes that contribute to electrical risk management (e.g., advising on risks and controls, reviewing risk assessments and SWIs, ensuring energised work is covered by approved applications, incident investigation support, and competency/licensing oversight). However, the TARGET duty is broader (PCBU must manage risks associated with design, construction, installation, protection, maintenance and testing of electrical equipment/installations, in accordance with Part 3.1). The SOURCE is limited to role duties and does not explicitly require the PCBU/business unit to implement a complete Part 3.1 risk management process (systematic hazard identification, risk assessment where required, implementation of controls per hierarchy, and ongoing review/monitoring) across all electrical risks at the workplace. It also does not clearly assign accountability for ensuring risk controls are implemented (beyond advising/endorsing/reviewing) or specify mechanisms for ensuring compliance across all relevant electrical activities (including equipment/installation lifecycle elements cited in the TARGET). On that basis, the SOURCE alone is insufficient to demonstrate compliance with the TARGET obligation.
compliance.remediation: Augment the procedure/role duties (or reference the governing WHS risk management procedure) to explicitly require and evidence Part 3.1-compliant electrical risk management across the workplace, including: (1) defined accountability for implementing controls (not just advising), (2) systematic hazard identification for all electrical activities (design through testing/maintenance), (3) application of the hierarchy of controls and verification of control effectiveness, (4) scheduled review/audit of electrical risk controls and SWIs, and (5) documented records demonstrating the above (risk registers, inspection/testing regimes, corrective actions).

(2) In the case of electrical equipment that is new and unused at the workplace, the person conducting the business or undertaking: 

(a) is not required to comply with subregulation (1); and (b) must ensure that the equipment is inspected for obvious damage before being used. 

Maximum penalty: tier G monetary penalty. 

Note 

However, electrical equipment that is unsafe must not be used (see regulation 149). 

(3) The person must ensure that a record of any testing carried out under subregulation (1) is kept until the electrical equipment is: 

(a) next tested; or (b) permanently removed from the workplace or disposed of. 


Maximum penalty: tier I monetary penalty. 

(4) The record of testing: 

(a) must specify the following: (i) the name of the person who carried out the testing; (ii) the date of the testing; (iii) the outcome of the testing; (iv) the date on which the next testing must be carried out; and   
(b) may be in the form of a tag attached to the electrical equipment tested.

matches.target_title: 166 Duty of person conducting a business or undertaking
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking at a workplace must ensure, so far as is reasonably practicable, that no person, plant or thing at the workplace comes within an unsafe distance of an overhead or underground electric line. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) If it is not reasonably practicable to ensure the safe distance of a person, plant or thing from an overhead or underground electric line, the person conducting the business or undertaking at the workplace must ensure that:
(a) a risk assessment is conducted in relation to the proposed work; and 

(b) control measures implemented are consistent with: 

(i) the risk assessment; and 

(ii) if an electricity supply authority is responsible for the electric line, any requirements of the authority. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

Notes 

1 [Relevant electricity safety legislation] will also apply to the person conducting the business or undertaking.

matches.target_title: 160 Contact with equipment being worked on
matches.target_text:
A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that, while electrical work is being carried out on energised electrical equipment, all persons are prevented from creating an electrical risk by inadvertently making contact with an exposed energised component of the equipment. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty.

compliance.reasoning: Error after 3 attempts: Connection error.

matches.target_title: 157 Electrical work on energised electrical equipment—when permitted
matches.target_text:
(1) A person conducting a business or undertaking must ensure that electrical work on energised electrical equipment is not carried out unless: 

(a) it is necessary in the interests of health and safety that the electrical work is carried out on the equipment while the equipment is energised; or Example 

It may be necessary that life-saving equipment remain energised and operating while electrical work is carried out on the equipment.   
(b) it is necessary that the electrical equipment to be worked on is energised in order for the work to be carried out properly; or   
(c) it is necessary for the purposes of testing required under regulation 155; or   
(d) there is no reasonable alternative means of carrying out the work. 

Maximum penalty: tier E monetary penalty. 

(2) The electrical work that may be carried out under subregulation (1)(a), (b) and (d) may include testing of the energised electrical equipment.
